title: 3GP Video
documentation:
## Purpose
3GP is a multimedia container format defined by the Third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) for delivering audio and video over 3G mobile networks. It is based on the MPEG-4 Part 12 container and optimized for low-bandwidth, constrained-device environments.

## When to use
Use [3GP Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:3gp;video) when ingesting video recorded on older mobile handsets or multimedia messaging service (MMS) content. The closely related 3G2 variant was introduced by 3GPP2 for CDMA networks; both extensions are covered by this media type.

## Notes
3GP files carry H.263 or MPEG-4 Part 2 video and AMR or AAC audio at low resolutions and bitrates suited to early mobile hardware. Modern smartphones no longer produce 3GP; they use [MOV](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mov;video) or [MP4](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video) instead. Implementers should expect very small frame sizes (typically 176x144 or 320x240) and limited metadata.

title: 7-Zip Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
7-Zip (7z) is a high-ratio archive format that supports LZMA2 compression, AES-256 encryption, and solid compression across multiple files. It consistently achieves some of the highest compression ratios among common archive formats.

## When to use
Use [7-Zip Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:7z;archive) when maximum compression ratio is the priority and the recipient has 7-Zip or a compatible extractor available. It is a good choice for distributing large binary assets or software packages where download size matters.

## Notes
7z solid archives compress files together as a single block, which greatly improves ratio on collections of similar files but means any single file extraction requires decompressing from the start. While 7z supports encryption, the format is not as universally supported as [ZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zip;archive); verify recipient tooling before using it in automated pipelines.

title: AAC Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an AAC (Advanced Audio Coding) audio file, the successor to MP3 that achieves better sound quality at equivalent or lower bitrates. AAC is the default audio format on Apple platforms and is widely used in streaming and broadcast.

## When to use
Use `media:aac;audio` when a node produces or consumes AAC audio, such as audio extracted from video, Apple Music files, or streaming content. For lossless audio, see [FLAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flac;audio). For the Apple container variant, see [M4A Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:m4a;audio).

## Notes
AAC is commonly wrapped in an MP4 or M4A container. Bare AAC in an ADTS stream uses the `.aac` extension. Consumers should confirm whether the payload is a raw ADTS stream or a containerised file, as the parsing approach differs.

title: Adapter Selection Result
description: Set of media URNs identified by a cartridge content-inspection adapter
documentation:
## Purpose
Returned by a cartridge content-inspection adapter (`cap:in="media:";out="media:adapter-selection;json;record"`) to report which media URNs the adapter believes match the inspected file content. The orchestrator uses this to select the most specific matching URN or present choices when multiple URNs are returned.

## When to use
This media type is produced exclusively by adapter caps declared in cartridge cap groups. It is not intended for general use in pipelines.

## Schema
The payload is a JSON object with a single required field:

- `media_urns` — a non-empty array of media URN strings that the adapter is confident the file content matches.

When multiple URNs are returned, the orchestrator selects the most specific one (highest specificity score). If the array is empty or the adapter returns an empty END frame, the orchestrator falls back to extension-based lookup in the media spec registry.

## Example
A JSON adapter inspecting a file that starts with `[{` might return:
```json
{"media_urns": ["media:json;list;record;textable", "media:json;list;textable", "media:json;textable"]}
```
The orchestrator would pick `media:json;list;record;textable` as the most specific.

A simpler adapter that only identifies the family:
```json
{"media_urns": ["media:json;textable"]}
```

## Notes
The URNs returned must be URNs that exist in the media spec registry. The orchestrator validates each returned URN and discards any that are not registered. If all are discarded, it falls back to extension-based lookup.

schema.title: Adapter Selection Result
schema.description: Set of media URNs identified by a cartridge content-inspection adapter.

media_urns.description: Media URN strings the adapter is confident match the inspected content. Must reference URNs registered in the media spec registry.

title: AIFF Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an AIFF (Audio Interchange File Format) file, Apple's native uncompressed audio container. AIFF stores PCM audio with full lossless fidelity and is widely used in professional audio production on macOS.

## When to use
Use `media:aiff;audio` when a node produces or consumes AIFF audio, particularly in professional audio production or archival workflows on macOS. For lossless but compressed audio, prefer [FLAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flac;audio). For general WAV-format PCM, see [WAV Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wav;audio).

## Notes
AIFF is the macOS equivalent of Windows WAV, both storing uncompressed PCM data. AIFF-C (AIFFC) is a compressed variant; consumers should check the compression type when parsing. Metadata is stored in AIFF chunks, which is distinct from ID3 or Vorbis comment conventions.

title: Android Package
documentation:
## Purpose
APK (Android Package Kit) is the binary distribution format for Android applications. Structurally a ZIP archive, it contains compiled Dalvik bytecode (`classes.dex`), resources, a manifest, and signing metadata. The [Android Package](https://capdag.com/browse/media:apk;archive) media type represents any `.apk` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes Android application binaries — for example, CI/CD build artifact pipelines, app distribution workflows, automated testing frameworks, or security analysis tools that inspect APK contents.

## Notes
APK files are ZIP archives and can be opened with any ZIP library for inspection. The `AndroidManifest.xml` inside is binary XML (AXML format), not plain text, and requires an Android-aware parser such as `apktool` or `androguard`. Signing must conform to the APK signing scheme (v1/v2/v3) for Android to accept the package; signed APKs should not be re-zipped without re-signing. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: Arrow Data
documentation:
## Purpose
Apache Arrow defines an in-memory columnar format and an on-disk IPC file format (also called Feather v2). The [Arrow Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:arrow;list;record) media type represents files written in the Arrow IPC file format, which stores an ordered list of typed record batches.

## When to use
Use this type when low-latency data exchange between nodes running in the same process or over shared memory matters, or when you need zero-copy reads into data-science tooling. Arrow is preferable over Parquet when compression overhead is undesirable and when the consumer will process data immediately rather than storing it long term.

## Notes
Arrow IPC files begin with a magic number and contain one or more record batches preceded by a schema message. The `.feather` extension refers to the same format. Implementers should use the Apache Arrow libraries; the PyArrow `ipc.open_file` API is the standard entry point for readers.

title: AsciiDoc
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries AsciiDoc source documents used for technical writing, API documentation, and books. AsciiDoc is a lightweight markup language with richer semantics than Markdown, supporting tables, admonitions, cross-references, and multi-file includes natively.

## When to use
Use `media:asciidoc;textable` when a pipeline node renders, converts, or analyzes AsciiDoc — for example, an Asciidoctor HTML rendering step, a documentation site builder, or a prose style-checking tool.

## Notes
AsciiDoc is processed by Asciidoctor (Ruby) or AsciidoctorJ (JVM) and can target HTML, PDF, DocBook, and EPUB output. The `.adoc` extension is most common in modern projects; `.asciidoc` is the original but less frequently used extension. See also [AsciiDoc](https://capdag.com/browse/media:asciidoc;textable).

title: ASS Subtitles
documentation:
## Purpose
ASS/SSA (Advanced SubStation Alpha / SubStation Alpha) is a rich subtitle format that supports complex styling, positioning, karaoke effects, and per-line overrides through an INI-like text structure. The [ASS Subtitles](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ass;textable;list) media type represents `.ass` and `.ssa` files.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes subtitles that require precise typographic control — for example, anime subtitling, karaoke files, or broadcast-quality caption workflows where font, color, border, shadow, and position must be specified per cue. For simpler web delivery use [WebVTT](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vtt;textable;list) or [SRT](https://capdag.com/browse/media:srt;textable;list).

## Notes
ASS files are plain text with sections: `[Script Info]`, `[V4+ Styles]`, and `[Events]`. The `Dialogue:` lines in `[Events]` carry the actual cue entries. The `;textable;list` tags reflect the human-readable ordered-list nature. Implementers should use a library such as `libass` or `ass.js` for rendering, as hand-parsing override tags (`{\pos(x,y)}`, `{\fad(in,out)}`, etc.) is complex.

title: Astro Component
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Astro component files, which combine a frontmatter script block (TypeScript/JavaScript), HTML-like templating, and optional client-side component islands from any supported UI framework (React, Vue, Svelte, Solid). Astro targets high-performance static and server-rendered websites.

## When to use
Use `media:astro;textable;code` when a pipeline node processes `.astro` files — for example, a build step invoking the Astro CLI, a component analysis tool, or an LLM node generating Astro page templates.

## Notes
Astro's component model renders to HTML by default with zero JavaScript shipped unless an explicit `client:*` directive is used. The `.astro` file format is specific to the Astro framework and requires the Astro compiler for transformation. See also [Astro Component](https://capdag.com/browse/media:astro;textable;code).

title: Speech Audio
description: WAV audio data containing human speech for transcription by Whisper or similar models
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a WAV audio file whose primary content is human speech intended for automatic transcription. The semantic tag distinguishes it from general-purpose audio, allowing pipelines to route the file to speech-to-text services automatically.

## When to use
Use `media:audio;speech;wav` when producing or consuming WAV recordings that will be fed to a transcription model such as Whisper. Do not use the generic [WAV Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wav;audio) type for this purpose, as it carries no transcription intent.

## Notes
The file must be a standard PCM WAV. Whisper and most transcription APIs expect mono or stereo 16-bit PCM at 16 kHz or higher. Callers should resample or convert before passing audio to a model if the source does not meet those requirements.

title: AVI Video
documentation:
## Purpose
AVI (Audio Video Interleave) is a legacy Microsoft video container format introduced in 1992. It multiplexes audio and video streams into a single RIFF-structured file and remains widely supported across desktop platforms.

## When to use
Use [AVI Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:avi;video) when ingesting or archiving video content sourced from older Windows-based production pipelines, capture cards, or editing tools that produce AVI output by default. Prefer more modern containers such as [MKV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mkv;video) or [MP4](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video) for new content.

## Notes
AVI does not natively support modern codec features such as B-frames in some encoders, variable frame rates, or embedded subtitles. Files larger than 2 GB require the OpenDML extension. Implementers should be prepared for a wide variety of codec combinations inside AVI containers.

title: AVIF Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an AVIF image, a next-generation format based on the AV1 video codec. AVIF provides superior compression compared to JPEG, PNG, and WebP, with support for HDR, wide colour gamut, lossless compression, and alpha transparency.

## When to use
Use `media:avif;image` when a node produces or consumes AVIF images, especially where maximum compression efficiency or HDR support is required. For broader compatibility with older systems, prefer [WebP Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webp;image) or [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image).

## Notes
AVIF encoding is computationally intensive compared to older formats. Decoding support is available in modern browsers and operating systems but may be absent in older toolchains. Verify codec availability in the target environment before adopting AVIF as a pipeline format.

title: Avro
documentation:
## Purpose
Apache Avro is a row-oriented binary serialization format that embeds its schema as JSON in the file header. The [Avro](https://capdag.com/browse/media:avro;list;record) media type represents files that store an ordered list of records encoded under a shared Avro schema.

## When to use
Use this type when schema evolution is a priority — Avro's reader/writer schema resolution allows producers and consumers to evolve their schemas independently. It is commonly used in event streaming pipelines (Kafka), Hadoop-based data lakes, and RPC frameworks that need compact, self-describing records.

## Notes
The embedded schema allows readers to decode the file without any out-of-band schema registry, though many production deployments use a separate registry for schema management. Implementers should use the official Apache Avro library for the target language; schema fingerprinting via the `avro.schema.Schema` fingerprint methods helps detect incompatible changes.

title: Identity
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Identity](https://capdag.com/browse/media:) type is the base media type representing an opaque sequence of bytes with no further structure imposed. It is the root of the media type hierarchy.

## When to use
Use this type when a node passes raw binary data whose internal format is irrelevant or unknown — for example, a passthrough relay, a generic storage node, or a node that operates on bytes without interpreting them. When the format is known, use a more specific media type instead.

## Notes
The `media:` URN (bare, no suffix) is the identity type. Nodes typed as `media:` accept any byte sequence regardless of content. Implementers should treat it as a wildcard for type compatibility checking: a port typed `media:` is compatible with any other media type.

title: Untyped List
description: Ordered list of opaque byte sequences.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Untyped List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list) type represents an ordered collection of opaque byte sequences where the element type is unspecified. It is the binary form of a list with no textability constraint.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes an ordered series of binary blobs whose individual element format is either unknown or irrelevant. When the elements have a known structure, use a more specific list type such as `media:list;record` or a domain-specific variant.

## Notes
The list framing format (length-prefix, delimiter, etc.) is determined by the runtime or protocol layer, not by this media type. Implementers should document the framing convention used at each port.

title: Text List
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Text List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;textable) type represents an ordered collection of values that are each expressible as UTF-8 text. The canonical serialization is one value per line in a plain-text file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a simple line-delimited list of string values — for example, a list of file paths, identifiers, labels, or search terms. When element values have numeric or structured types, use the corresponding typed list variant.

## Notes
Line endings should be LF (`\n`). Empty lines are valid list entries. Implementers should define whether trailing newlines are significant. For lists with a specific element type (numbers, booleans, records), prefer the appropriate typed list URN over this generic form.

title: Record
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Record](https://capdag.com/browse/media:record) type (untyped binary form) represents a structured object with named fields stored as an opaque binary encoding. It is the binary counterpart to `media:record;textable`.

## When to use
Use this type when a node exchanges structured records in a binary encoding without a text-readable representation — for example, Protobuf messages, packed structs, or any custom binary record format. When the record must be human-readable, use `media:record;textable` (JSON) instead.

## Notes
The specific encoding and field schema are defined by the port contract, not by this media type. Implementers should document the encoding format (e.g., Protobuf schema, MessagePack map structure) alongside any port typed as `media:record`.

title: Record List
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Record List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;record) type represents an ordered collection of structured records in a binary encoding. It is the binary form of a tabular dataset where each row is a key-value record.

## When to use
Use this type when a node exchanges a sequence of structured objects in a compact binary format — for example, Parquet-like payloads, Protobuf repeated message lists, or any framed sequence of binary records. When the records must be column-oriented or text-readable, use [Parquet Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:parquet;list;record) or `media:record;textable`.

## Notes
The record schema and list framing convention must be documented at the port level. Implementers should specify whether the list is length-prefixed, newline-delimited, or uses a sentinel to indicate end-of-stream.

title: Record
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Record](https://capdag.com/browse/media:record;textable) type (textable form) represents a structured object with named fields encoded as a JSON object. It is the human-readable counterpart to the binary `media:record` type.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a single structured JSON object — for example, a configuration record, an API response payload, a metadata envelope, or a node's output parameters. For collections of records, use `media:list;record` or domain-specific types such as [Parquet Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:parquet;list;record).

## Notes
The JSON encoding must be a top-level object (`{...}`), not an array or scalar. The `;textable` tag indicates the value is human-readable UTF-8 text. Implementers should validate the JSON against the port's schema before processing, and use a streaming JSON parser for large objects to avoid excessive memory usage.

title: Number List
description: Text file with one floating-point numeric value per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Number List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;numeric;textable) type represents an ordered sequence of floating-point numbers encoded as plain text, one value per line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a simple sequence of numeric values — for example, a list of loss values from a training loop, a time series of measurements, or a ranked score list. For typed integer or real subsets, prefer `media:integer;list;numeric;textable` or `media:list;numeric;real;textable`.

## Notes
Each line must contain exactly one number in a format parseable by standard floating-point parsers (e.g., `1.5`, `-3e10`, `NaN`, `Inf`). Lines must be LF-terminated. Blank lines should be treated as errors unless the port contract specifies otherwise.

title: Number Value
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Number Value](https://capdag.com/browse/media:numeric;textable) type represents a single floating-point number encoded as UTF-8 text.

## When to use
Use this type for scalar numeric outputs — for example, a computed metric, a model confidence score, or a single measurement result. For typed subsets (integer, real, complex) use the corresponding subtype.

## Notes
The value must be a single line of text containing a valid floating-point literal. Trailing newlines are permitted. `NaN` and `Inf` / `-Inf` representations should follow IEEE 754 conventions as recognized by the target language's standard library parser.

title: Integer
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Integer](https://capdag.com/browse/media:integer;numeric;textable) type represents a single signed integer value encoded as UTF-8 text — a subtype of the general numeric type.

## When to use
Use this type when a node outputs or accepts a single whole-number value — for example, a count, an index, an HTTP status code, or any context where fractional values are meaningless. For sequences of integers, use `media:integer;list;numeric;textable`.

## Notes
The value must be a decimal integer literal, optionally prefixed with `-` for negative values. No fractional part, exponent notation, or leading zeros (beyond a single `0`) are permitted. Implementers should parse with 64-bit signed integer semantics by default.

title: Integer List
description: Text file with one signed integer value per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Integer List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:integer;list;numeric;textable) type represents an ordered sequence of signed integers encoded as plain text, one value per line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a list of whole numbers — for example, token IDs, category labels, page numbers, or sorted index sequences. For lists that may contain fractional values, use `media:list;numeric;textable` instead.

## Notes
Each line must contain exactly one decimal integer literal. No blank lines, no commas, no headers. Lines must be LF-terminated. Implementers should parse each line with 64-bit signed integer semantics.

title: Real
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Real](https://capdag.com/browse/media:numeric;real;textable) type represents a single real (non-complex, non-integer-constrained) floating-point value encoded as UTF-8 text. It is a subtype of `media:numeric;textable` that explicitly excludes complex numbers.

## When to use
Use this type when a node outputs a scalar real-valued measurement — for example, a probability, a physical measurement, a loss value, or a normalized score. Prefer this over the generic `media:numeric;textable` when the port semantics guarantee the value is not complex.

## Notes
The value must be a standard floating-point literal. `NaN` and infinity representations are permitted. Implementers should use 64-bit IEEE 754 double precision for parsing unless the port contract specifies otherwise.

title: Real List
description: Text file with one real numeric value per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Real List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;numeric;real;textable) type represents an ordered sequence of real (non-complex) floating-point values encoded as plain text, one per line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a sequence of real-valued measurements — for example, a time series of sensor readings, a list of probabilities, or an array of distances. For values that may be complex, use `media:complex;list;numeric;textable`.

## Notes
Each line must contain exactly one real-valued floating-point literal. Blank lines and non-numeric content are errors. Implementers should parse each value as a 64-bit IEEE 754 double unless the port contract specifies a different precision.

title: Complex
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Complex](https://capdag.com/browse/media:complex;numeric;textable) type represents a single complex number (with real and imaginary parts) encoded as UTF-8 text.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes complex-valued scalars — for example, outputs from FFT analysis, signal processing nodes, or quantum computing simulations.

## Notes
The canonical text representation is `a+bj` (Python-style) or `a+bi`, where `a` is the real part and `b` is the imaginary part. Implementers should document the exact notation convention at the port. For sequences of complex values, use `media:complex;list;numeric;textable`.

title: Complex List
description: Text file with one complex numeric value per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Complex List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:complex;list;numeric;textable) type represents an ordered sequence of complex numbers encoded as plain text, one value per line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a sequence of complex-valued data — for example, FFT output bins, IQ samples from a software-defined radio, or eigenvectors from complex matrix operations.

## Notes
Each line must contain one complex number in a consistent notation (e.g., `a+bj`). The notation convention must be agreed upon between producer and consumer and documented at the port. Blank lines are errors. Implementers should parse each value using the target language's complex number type (e.g., Python `complex`, C++ `std::complex<double>`).

title: Boolean
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Boolean](https://capdag.com/browse/media:bool;textable) type represents a single true/false value encoded as UTF-8 text.

## When to use
Use this type for scalar boolean outputs — for example, a validation result, a feature flag value, a predicate test outcome, or any binary decision. For sequences of booleans, use `media:bool;list;textable`.

## Notes
Accepted representations include `true`/`false`, `yes`/`no`, and `1`/`0` (case-insensitive). Implementers should normalize to a canonical form on output. The exact accepted vocabulary should be documented at the port if it diverges from this default set.

title: Text
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:textable) type represents an arbitrary UTF-8 string with no further structure imposed. It is the base textable type in the media hierarchy.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes unstructured plain text — for example, a human-readable summary, a prompt string, a log message, or any freeform text payload. When the text has a more specific structure (JSON record, code, subtitle, etc.) use the appropriate subtype.

## Notes
Content must be valid UTF-8. Line endings may be LF or CRLF; consumers should normalize if needed. The `;textable` tag in other URNs signals that the type has a canonical human-readable text representation; `media:textable` alone means only unstructured plain text is guaranteed.

title: Boolean List
description: Text file with one boolean value per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Boolean List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:bool;list;textable) type represents an ordered sequence of true/false values encoded as plain text, one value per line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a sequence of boolean decisions — for example, a mask array, a sequence of pass/fail results, or a list of feature flags. For a single boolean value, use `media:bool;textable`.

## Notes
Each line must contain exactly one boolean literal. Accepted values are `true`/`false`, `yes`/`no`, and `1`/`0` (case-insensitive). Lines must be LF-terminated. Blank lines are errors unless the port contract specifies otherwise.

title: Batch Script
documentation:
## Purpose
Windows batch scripts are plain-text files interpreted by the Windows Command Processor (`cmd.exe`), containing a sequence of commands and control-flow statements (IF, FOR, GOTO, CALL). The [Batch Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:batch;textable;code) media type represents any `.bat` or `.cmd` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes Windows automation scripts — for example, legacy build system integration, Windows-specific deployment scripts, CI/CD pipelines targeting Windows agents, or static analysis tools that inspect batch file content.

## Notes
Batch files are plain text, typically ASCII or Windows-1252 encoded (UTF-8 with BOM is also supported by `cmd.exe`). The `;textable;code` tags indicate the file is both human-readable and executable code. Key behavioral differences from POSIX shells include case-insensitive variable names, `%variable%` expansion syntax, and `^` as the line-continuation character. Implementers running batch files programmatically should use `cmd.exe /C <script>` and capture `ERRORLEVEL` for exit status.

title: BMP Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an uncompressed or minimally compressed Windows BMP raster image. BMP is a simple format with broad compatibility and no lossy compression, though it produces large file sizes.

## When to use
Use `media:bmp;image` when a node must produce or consume BMP files, typically for compatibility with legacy Windows applications or imaging pipelines that require uncompressed pixel data. For most other purposes, PNG or JPEG are preferable.

## Notes
BMP files store pixel data directly with little or no compression, resulting in file sizes much larger than PNG or JPEG equivalents. Consumers should convert BMP to a more efficient format before storage or transmission when file size is a concern.

title: BZIP2 Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
BZIP2 is a single-file compression format using the Burrows-Wheeler block-sorting algorithm followed by Huffman coding. It generally achieves better compression than [GZIP](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gzip;archive) at the cost of higher CPU usage and slower speed.

## When to use
Use [BZIP2 Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:bzip2;archive) when compression ratio is more important than speed, and the toolchain supports bzip2. It is commonly found in Linux package repositories (`.tar.bz2`) and some open-source source distributions.

## Notes
BZIP2 does not support random access or multi-file bundling; pair it with [TAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tar;archive) to create `.tar.bz2` archives. For modern use cases, [Zstandard](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zstd;archive) and [XZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive) offer comparable or superior compression with better performance characteristics.

title: C Header
documentation:
## Purpose
C header files declare the public interface of C libraries and modules: function prototypes, struct definitions, macros, and constants that can be shared across multiple translation units via `#include` directives.

## When to use
Use [C Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c-header;textable;code) for `.h` files that are included by [C Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c;textable;code) files. Note that `.h` files are also commonly used for C++ and Objective-C headers; if the content is clearly C++ or Objective-C, the more specific header types should be used: [C++ Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cpp-header;textable;code).

## Notes
Header files typically use include guards (`#ifndef`/`#define`/`#endif`) or `#pragma once` to prevent multiple inclusion. They should generally not contain definitions (only declarations) to avoid linker errors when included in multiple translation units. Public headers in libraries are often the primary API documentation surface.

title: C Source
documentation:
## Purpose
C source files contain implementation code written in the C programming language, a foundational systems language that remains central to operating systems, embedded firmware, compilers, and performance-critical libraries.

## When to use
Use [C Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c;textable;code) for `.c` implementation files. Header declarations are handled by [C Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c-header;textable;code). C is the lingua franca of system-level interoperability; most other languages provide FFI bindings to C.

## Notes
C source is typically plain ASCII or UTF-8 text. The applicable C standard (C89, C99, C11, C17, C23) significantly affects available features; implementations should not assume a specific standard without inspecting compiler flags or project configuration. The `.c` extension is also used by some Objective-C++ files in older codebases; use MIME type and include patterns to disambiguate.

title: Cache Dir
documentation:
## Purpose

Cache Dir specifies the filesystem path where the application stores cached data, including model artifacts, embeddings, and API response caches. Centralizing the cache directory simplifies backup and disk-space management.

## When to use

Set this when the default cache location (typically a platform-specific application data directory) is unsuitable, such as when a larger or faster disk is available at a different path. Relative paths are resolved from the application's working directory unless documented otherwise.

## Notes

The path must not contain null bytes. Ensure the process has read and write permission to the specified directory. Changing the cache directory does not migrate existing cached data; the new directory will be populated from scratch on first use.

title: CAF Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a CAF (Core Audio Format) file, Apple's flexible audio container designed to overcome size limitations of older formats. CAF can store any codec supported by Core Audio, along with rich metadata, markers, and regions.

## When to use
Use `media:caf;audio` when a node produces or consumes CAF files, typically from Apple development tools, iOS audio recordings, or macOS system audio APIs. CAF is rarely used outside the Apple ecosystem; consider converting to [AIFF Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aiff;audio) or [FLAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flac;audio) for broader compatibility.

## Notes
CAF has no 4 GB file size limit, unlike WAV and AIFF. The codec embedded in the container varies; `afconvert` on macOS can inspect and convert CAF files. Consumers on non-Apple platforms require a CAF-aware library for decoding.

title: Callback Enabled Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that controls whether a machine emits progress or result notifications to a registered callback endpoint during or after execution.

## When to use
Enable when a long-running machine should notify an external system of completion or intermediate results without requiring the caller to poll. Disable for synchronous, request-response pipelines where callbacks add unnecessary complexity.

## Notes
Callback endpoint configuration is managed separately from this flag. Enabling callbacks requires that the runtime supports the callback mechanism and that a valid endpoint is registered. See [Stream Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:stream-flag;textable) for token-level streaming as an alternative to callbacks.

title: CBOR
documentation:
## Purpose
CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation, RFC 8949) is a binary data format derived from JSON's data model, designed for small message size and deterministic encoding. The [CBOR](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cbor) media type represents files or byte streams containing a single top-level CBOR data item.

## When to use
Use this type when interoperability with IETF standards is required — CBOR is the wire format for COSE (signing/encryption), CWT (tokens), and CBOR-LD (linked data). It is also appropriate for constrained-device protocols (CoAP, Matter) and anywhere an IETF-standardized binary JSON equivalent is preferred over MessagePack.

## Notes
CBOR supports deterministic encoding (RFC 8949 Section 4.2) which is required for cryptographic use cases. Tag numbers extend the type system; commonly used tags include 0 (datetime string), 1 (epoch timestamp), and 37 (UUID). Implementers should validate that input data conforms to the expected CBOR diagnostic notation or schema when security is a concern.

title: Chunk Overlap
documentation:
## Purpose
Defines the number of tokens or characters shared between consecutive chunks when splitting a document. Overlap preserves context at chunk boundaries, reducing the risk of information loss.

## When to use
Use alongside [Chunk Size](https://capdag.com/browse/media:chunk-size;textable;numeric) on any machine that segments documents. A typical overlap is 10–20% of the chunk size. Set to 0 when strict non-overlapping segments are required (e.g., when deduplication is critical).

## Notes
Valid range: 0–500. Overlap must be less than chunk size. Larger overlaps increase index size and redundancy, which can reduce retrieval precision in embedding-based search.

title: Chunk Size
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies the maximum number of tokens or characters in a single chunk when splitting documents for processing. Controls the granularity of content segmentation.

## When to use
Set this parameter on chunking or ingestion machines that split large documents before passing them to models with limited context windows. Smaller chunks increase retrieval precision; larger chunks preserve more context per segment.

## Notes
Valid range: 10–2000. Pair with [Chunk Overlap](https://capdag.com/browse/media:chunk-overlap;textable;numeric) to retain context across chunk boundaries, and with [Max Content Length](https://capdag.com/browse/media:max-content-length;textable;numeric) to ensure chunks fit within downstream limits.

title: Clojure Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Clojure source files contain code written in Clojure, a dynamically typed, functional Lisp dialect that runs on the JVM (`.clj`), JavaScript runtimes via ClojureScript (`.cljs`), or both via reader conditionals (`.cljc`). Clojure emphasizes immutable data structures, persistent collections, and software transactional memory.

## When to use
Use [Clojure Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:clojure;textable;code) for `.clj` JVM files, `.cljs` ClojureScript files, `.cljc` portable files targeting multiple platforms, and `.edn` Extensible Data Notation data files (used for configuration, data exchange, and Clojure data literals). Clojure projects are typically built with Leiningen or the Clojure CLI (`deps.edn`).

## Notes
EDN (`.edn`) is a data format, not executable code, but it shares Clojure's reader syntax and is processed by the same toolchain. ClojureScript compiles to JavaScript and targets browser or Node.js environments; platform-specific APIs differ from JVM Clojure. Clojure's macro system and dynamic nature make static analysis harder than for statically typed languages — most Clojure tooling relies on runtime instrumentation or REPL evaluation.

title: CMake File
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries CMake build scripts that describe how to configure, build, and package C/C++ projects (and projects in other compiled languages). CMake generates native build files — Makefiles, Ninja files, Visual Studio projects — from a single cross-platform description.

## When to use
Use `media:cmake;textable` when a pipeline node processes `.cmake` files or `CMakeLists.txt` — for example, a configuration step invoking `cmake ..`, a linting step using `cmake-lint`, or a project generator that emits CMake scaffolding.

## Notes
The primary filename `CMakeLists.txt` uses no `.cmake` extension; this type covers reusable module and toolchain files (`.cmake`). Both are semantically equivalent and parsed by the same CMake interpreter. See also [CMake File](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cmake;textable).

title: Code Context
documentation:
## Purpose

Code Context provides background information about the codebase, architecture, or existing code that a model should take into account when generating or analyzing code. It is a free-form text field with a generous character limit.

## When to use

Use this media type to pass snippets of existing code, file structure descriptions, API contracts, or other developer context to a code-focused node. Pair it with [Programming Language](https://capdag.com/browse/media:programming-language;textable) and [Coding Style](https://capdag.com/browse/media:coding-style;textable) for best results.

## Notes

The maximum length is 65,536 characters. Prioritize the most relevant context rather than including entire files, as excess context can reduce output quality.

title: Coding Style
documentation:
## Purpose

Coding Style describes the formatting conventions, naming rules, and stylistic preferences that generated or reviewed code should follow. It is a free-form text field, allowing project-specific style guides to be expressed in plain language.

## When to use

Use this media type when a code generation node should adhere to a particular house style, linter configuration, or team convention. It works alongside [Programming Language](https://capdag.com/browse/media:programming-language;textable) and [Code Context](https://capdag.com/browse/media:code-contextable).

## Notes

Keep style descriptions concise and actionable. Examples such as "use snake_case for variables", "prefer early returns", or "max line length 100" are more effective than vague directives.

title: Confidence Threshold
documentation:
## Purpose
A probability cutoff below which a machine's output is considered unreliable and should be suppressed, flagged, or sent to a fallback path. Typically used in classification or decision machines.

## When to use
Set a confidence threshold when downstream processes require a minimum certainty level. Results with scores below the threshold can be routed for human review or discarded. A common starting point is 0.7–0.9 for high-precision tasks.

## Notes
Valid range: 0.0–1.0. A threshold of 0.0 accepts all results; 1.0 requires perfect certainty and will suppress most output. Pair with [Decision](https://capdag.com/browse/media:decision;json;record;textable) or classification output types that carry probability scores.

title: Core ML Model
description: Apple Core ML machine learning model
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a model in Apple's Core ML format, which is optimised for on-device inference on Apple platforms using the Neural Engine, GPU, and CPU. Core ML models are packaged as `.mlmodel` or `.mlpackage` bundles.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap targets the Core ML runtime on macOS or iOS. Core ML models offer the best energy efficiency and latency on Apple hardware for tasks that have been compiled to this format. For general Apple Silicon inference with more model flexibility, prefer the MLX backend and [MLX LLM Model Spec](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-spec;mlx;textable;llm).

## Notes
The `.mlpackage` format is the newer bundle-based container preferred for models compiled with the Core ML Tools v5+ toolchain. The `.mlmodel` format is the legacy flat-file format. Both are identified by `application/octet-stream` at the MIME level.

title: C++ Header
documentation:
## Purpose
C++ header files declare class definitions, template implementations, inline functions, and other interface elements shared across [C++ Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cpp;textable;code) files. Unlike C, C++ headers frequently contain full template and inline function bodies.

## When to use
Use [C++ Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cpp-header;textable;code) for files with extensions `.hpp`, `.hh`, `.hxx`, or `.h++`. Plain `.h` files that contain C++ content are covered by [C Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c-header;textable;code) unless the context clearly indicates C++.

## Notes
C++ headers frequently contain substantial implementation code, particularly for templates and inline methods, which makes the traditional header/source split less clear than in C. Header-only libraries are common in modern C++ (e.g., Catch2, nlohmann/json) and consist entirely of this media type. Module interfaces introduced in C++20 (`.ixx`, `.cppm`) represent a new alternative to traditional headers.

title: C++ Source
documentation:
## Purpose
C++ source files contain implementation code written in C++, a systems-level language that extends C with object-oriented programming, templates, and the standard library. C++ is used for game engines, real-time systems, compilers, high-performance libraries, and any domain where low-level control and high performance are both required.

## When to use
Use [C++ Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cpp;textable;code) for implementation files with extensions `.cpp`, `.cc`, `.cxx`, or `.c++`. Header declarations belong in [C++ Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:cpp-header;textable;code). For files mixing C++ with Objective-C, see [Objective-C++ Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:objcpp;textable;code).

## Notes
C++ standard versions (C++11, 14, 17, 20, 23) introduce significant language changes; tools must know the applicable standard to parse source correctly. Template-heavy code and metaprogramming patterns can be challenging for static analysis tools. Build systems (CMake, Meson, Bazel) control compilation flags and the C++ standard version used.

title: Creative Context
documentation:
## Purpose

Creative Context supplies background narrative, world-building details, character notes, or thematic constraints that ground a creative writing task. It is a large free-form text field allowing rich, multi-paragraph briefings.

## When to use

Use this media type when a generative writing node needs domain context beyond a simple prompt. It complements [Genre](https://capdag.com/browse/media:genre;textable), [Tone](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tone;textable), and [Creativity Level](https://capdag.com/browse/media:creativity-level;textable;numeric) by providing the substantive material the model should draw from.

## Notes

The maximum length is 16,384 characters. Structure the context with clear sections (setting, characters, constraints) to help the model prioritize when the field is densely populated.

title: Creativity Level
documentation:
## Purpose

Creativity Level is a normalized float between 0.0 and 1.0 that controls how adventurous or conservative the model's output should be. Lower values favor predictability and accuracy; higher values favor novelty and variation.

## When to use

Use this media type when a creative writing or brainstorming node exposes a creativity dial to callers. Pair it with [Genre](https://capdag.com/browse/media:genre;textable), [Tone](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tone;textable), and [Creative Context](https://capdag.com/browse/media:creative-contextable) to fully parameterize creative output.

## Notes

This value maps conceptually to a model's temperature parameter but is expressed on a 0–1 scale regardless of the underlying model's native range. Values above 0.8 may produce less coherent output for structured tasks; reserve them for purely generative use cases.

title: C# Source
documentation:
## Purpose
C# source files contain code written in C#, Microsoft's statically typed, object-oriented language that runs on the .NET runtime. C# is widely used for enterprise applications, game development (Unity), web backends (ASP.NET Core), and desktop applications on Windows.

## When to use
Use [C# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csharp;textable;code) for `.cs` files in .NET projects. C# projects are organized by `.csproj` solution files that declare framework targets, dependencies, and build configuration. For other .NET languages, see [F# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fsharp;textable;code) and [Visual Basic Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vb;textable;code).

## Notes
C# has evolved substantially from version 1.0 through C# 13; features such as nullable reference types, records, pattern matching, and top-level statements were added progressively. The `LangVersion` property in `.csproj` controls which language features are available. C# source is UTF-8 text with optional byte-order marks.

title: CSS Stylesheet
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries standard CSS stylesheets that define the visual presentation of HTML documents. CSS is the fundamental styling language of the web, supported natively in all browsers without a compilation step.

## When to use
Use `media:css;textable` when a pipeline node consumes or produces CSS — for example, a PostCSS transformation step, a CSS minifier, a design-token exporter, or an LLM node generating component styles. For preprocessor source, use [Less Stylesheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:less;textable) or [SCSS Stylesheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:scss;textable).

## Notes
Modern CSS includes custom properties (variables), container queries, cascade layers, and native nesting, reducing the gap with preprocessors. Minified CSS is still CSS and can be carried by this type, though it is not human-readable. See also [CSS Stylesheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:css;textable).

title: CSV Data
description: Comma-separated values with header row and multiple columns
documentation:
## Purpose
The standard CSV type: a header row followed by one or more data rows, where each row is a record with named fields. This is the most common CSV shape produced by spreadsheets, database exports, and data pipelines.

## When to use
Use `media:csv;list;record;textable` for tabular CSV files with a header. For a single-row CSV file, use [CSV Record](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csv;record;textable). For tab- or pipe-delimited alternatives, see [TSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tsv;textable) and [PSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:psv;textable).

## Notes
Field quoting follows RFC 4180. Consumers should use a standards-compliant CSV parser to correctly handle quoted fields, embedded commas, and newlines within values.

title: CSV Record
description: Single CSV record with header row and one data row
documentation:
## Purpose
A CSV file containing exactly two lines: a header row and one data row. This represents a single named record rather than a collection, and is useful when a pipeline step produces exactly one result per run.

## When to use
Use `media:csv;record;textable` when a node is guaranteed to produce a single result row with column names. For multi-row tabular data, use [CSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csv;list;record;textable).

## Notes
Consumers receiving this type can safely assume at most one data row after the header. A file with only a header row and no data row is degenerate and should be treated as an error.

title: CSV List
documentation:
## Purpose
A CSV file with a single column containing a list of values. There are no field names; the content is purely a sequence of scalar values, one per row.

## When to use
Use `media:csv;list;textable` when a node produces a simple enumeration of values without structural fields. For multi-column tabular data, use [CSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csv;list;record;textable).

## Notes
A header row may or may not be present. Consumers should confirm this via node metadata. Single-column CSV is often interchangeable with plain newline-delimited text.

title: Dart Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Dart source files used in Flutter applications, server-side programs, and general-purpose Dart packages. Dart combines a familiar C-style syntax with a sound type system and built-in async/await support.

## When to use
Use `media:dart;textable;code` when a pipeline node consumes or produces `.dart` source files — for example, a code generation step targeting Flutter widgets or a linter analyzing Dart business logic.

## Notes
Dart supports both ahead-of-time compilation (for native mobile/desktop targets) and just-in-time compilation (for development). The extensions `.dart` covers both library and application entry-point files. See also [Dart Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:dart;textable;code).

title: Debian Package
documentation:
## Purpose
DEB is the binary package format for Debian-based Linux distributions (Debian, Ubuntu, Linux Mint, etc.). A `.deb` file is an `ar` archive containing control metadata, file lists, and a compressed data archive. The [Debian Package](https://capdag.com/browse/media:deb;archive) media type represents any `.deb` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes Debian-family Linux packages — for example, software build pipelines, private APT repository management, automated deployment tools, or security scanning workflows that inspect package contents.

## Notes
A `.deb` archive contains three members: `debian-binary` (format version), `control.tar.*` (package metadata and scripts), and `data.tar.*` (actual files). Implementers can extract content using `dpkg-deb --extract` or by using the `ar` and `tar` utilities directly. Signing uses a detached `Release.gpg` file in the repository rather than within the `.deb` itself. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: Decision Type
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies the evaluation strategy a decision machine should apply. The value controls how the model frames and returns its judgment.

## When to use
Set this parameter on any machine that routes between multiple decision modes. Allowed values are `binary`, `classification`, `reasoning`, `verification`, and `comparison`. If omitted, machines typically default to `binary`.

## Notes
`binary` returns a true/false answer; `classification` selects from a label set; `reasoning` includes a rationale; `verification` checks a claim; `comparison` ranks two or more items. Pair with [Decision](https://capdag.com/browse/media:decision;json;record;textable) for the output format.

title: Decision
description: Boolean decision as a JSON object with identifier and value fields.
documentation:
## Purpose
Encodes the result of a binary evaluation as a structured JSON record. The `identifier` field records the question or criterion that was assessed; `value` holds the boolean answer.

## When to use
Use this type as the output of machines that perform yes/no classification, verification, or filtering. It is the canonical output for [Decision Type](https://capdag.com/browse/media:decision-type;textable) `binary` and `verification` modes.

## Notes
Schema requires exactly two fields: `identifier` (string) and `value` (boolean). No additional properties are allowed. For multi-class outputs, consider a classification-specific media type.

title: Detailed Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that switches a machine's output from a summary or compact form to a fully detailed form, including additional fields, reasoning steps, or metadata.

## When to use
Enable when debugging a pipeline, generating audit logs, or when downstream consumers need richer output for further processing. Disable in production paths where compact output is sufficient and verbosity adds latency or cost.

## Notes
The additional fields produced when this flag is set are machine-specific. See [Output Length](https://capdag.com/browse/media:output-length;textable) for qualitative length control and [Require Explanation Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:require-explanation-flag;textable) when you specifically need the model to justify its answer.

title: Device
documentation:
## Purpose

Device specifies the hardware target on which model inference or computation should run. It determines whether the workload is dispatched to the CPU, an NVIDIA CUDA GPU, an Apple MPS GPU, or selected automatically by the runtime.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node wraps a local model and the caller needs explicit control over hardware placement. In most deployments, `auto` is the appropriate choice; use `cpu`, `cuda`, or `mps` only when hardware availability is known and pinning is required.

## Notes

`mps` requires macOS with Apple Silicon. `cuda` requires an NVIDIA GPU with appropriate drivers. Specifying a device that is unavailable at runtime will cause the node to fail rather than fall back silently.

title: Dictionary Key
documentation:
## Purpose

Dictionary Key is a plain-text string that identifies an entry within a key-value dictionary or record. It is used by nodes that read from or write to structured data by specifying which slot to access.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node must address a specific field within a dictionary-shaped payload. It pairs naturally with [Substitutions](https://capdag.com/browse/media:substitutions;textable;record) or [Schema Variables](https://capdag.com/browse/media:schema-variables;textable;record) when a single key needs to be extracted or overridden.

## Notes

Key values are opaque strings; the system does not interpret dots or brackets as path separators unless the consuming node explicitly documents that behavior. Use a consistent naming convention across your pipeline to avoid ambiguity.

title: DjVu Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries DjVu files, a compressed scanned-document format developed by AT&T Labs specifically for digitized books, journals, and historical documents. DjVu achieves high compression ratios for scanned content by separately encoding foreground text, background, and color layers.

## When to use
Use `media:djvu` when a pipeline node processes scanned document archives — for example, a text extraction step using `djvulibre` tools for OCR pre-processing, a page rendering step converting DjVu pages to images, or a format conversion step targeting PDF.

## Notes
DjVu is common in academic and library digitization projects (e.g., Internet Archive). The `djvulibre` toolset provides command-line utilities for rendering, text extraction, and conversion. For native PDF scans, use the PDF media type instead. See also [DjVu Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:djvu).

title: macOS Disk Image
documentation:
## Purpose
DMG (Apple Disk Image) is a macOS container format for distributing software as a mountable virtual disk. When opened, macOS mounts the image and presents its contents (typically a `.app` bundle or installer) in Finder. The [macOS Disk Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:dmg;archive) media type represents any `.dmg` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes macOS application distribution images — for example, release artifact pipelines, notarization workflows, or automated testing infrastructure that mounts and inspects DMG contents. DMG is the conventional drag-to-install distribution format for macOS applications.

## Notes
DMG files commonly use UDIF (Universal Disk Image Format) with optional zlib or bzip2 compression. Implementers can attach (mount) a DMG using `hdiutil attach` on macOS or inspect its contents without mounting using `7-Zip` on other platforms. Apple notarization and stapling (`xcrun stapler staple`) are required for DMGs distributed to end users on macOS 10.15+. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: Word Document (Legacy)
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries legacy Microsoft Word documents in the binary Compound Document File Format (`.doc`) used by Word 97 through Word 2003. This format predates the Open XML standard and is still encountered in archival and enterprise contexts.

## When to use
Use `media:doc` when a pipeline node processes legacy Word files — for example, a document conversion step using LibreOffice or `antiword` to extract text, or an archival ingestion step that needs to distinguish legacy from modern Word format. For current Word documents, prefer [Word Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:docx).

## Notes
The binary `.doc` format is proprietary and complex; open-source support is incomplete compared to DOCX. Conversion to DOCX or PDF is recommended before performing text extraction or rendering. See also [Word Document (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:doc).

title: Dockerfile
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a Dockerfile that describes how to build a container image layer by layer. Dockerfiles are the primary artifact for reproducible containerization of applications and services.

## When to use
Use `media:dockerfile;textable` when a pipeline node builds, lints, or generates a Dockerfile — for example, a `docker build` executor node, a Hadolint analysis step, or an LLM node asked to containerize a given application.

## Notes
Dockerfiles have no standard file extension; the conventional filename is `Dockerfile` (no extension) or `Dockerfile.<variant>`. The `.dockerfile` extension registered here is used when the file must have an explicit extension. See also [Dockerfile](https://capdag.com/browse/media:dockerfile;textable).

title: Docker Ignore
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a `.dockerignore` file that lists patterns for files and directories to exclude from the Docker build context. Excluding unnecessary files reduces build times and prevents sensitive data from entering container images.

## When to use
Use `media:dockerignore;textable;list` when a pipeline node reads, generates, or audits a `.dockerignore` file — for example, a build optimization step that recommends exclusion patterns based on a project's file layout.

## Notes
The pattern syntax is similar to `.gitignore` but not identical; Docker applies patterns relative to the build context root and has its own negation and wildcard rules. See also [Docker Ignore](https://capdag.com/browse/media:dockerignore;textable;list).

title: Word Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Microsoft Word documents in the Open XML format (`.docx`), the current standard for word processing introduced with Office 2007. DOCX files are ZIP archives containing XML parts for content, styles, relationships, and embedded media.

## When to use
Use `media:docx` when a pipeline node processes Word documents — for example, a text extraction step, a template rendering step using `python-docx`, a document comparison tool, or a conversion step targeting PDF or HTML output.

## Notes
DOCX is an ISO/IEC 29500 standard and is supported by LibreOffice, Google Docs, and many other tools. Because it is a ZIP-based format, individual XML parts can be accessed by renaming the file to `.zip`. For the older binary format, see [Word Document (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:doc). See also [Word Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:docx).

title: DOT Graph
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Graphviz DOT language files that describe directed and undirected graphs using a declarative text syntax. DOT files are rendered to PNG, SVG, PDF, and other formats by Graphviz tools such as `dot`, `neato`, and `fdp`.

## When to use
Use `media:dot;textable` when a pipeline node produces or renders graph descriptions — for example, a dependency graph exporter that serializes a build DAG as DOT, or a rendering step that invokes `dot -Tsvg` to produce a visual output.

## Notes
The `.dot` and `.gv` extensions are interchangeable; `.gv` was introduced to avoid conflicts with Microsoft Word template files on Windows. Graphviz layout engines (`dot`, `neato`, `circo`, `twopi`) produce different spatial arrangements and should be chosen based on graph structure. See also [DOT Graph](https://capdag.com/browse/media:dot;textable).

title: Model Download Result
description: Result from a model download operation, including the local path and download metadata
documentation:
## Purpose
Reports the outcome of a completed model download, confirming whether it succeeded, where the model was saved, and how much data was transferred. This gives callers all the information they need to proceed with loading or verifying the downloaded model.

## When to use
Return this type from any cap that performs a model download operation. Downstream caps that need the local path to load the model can consume the `path` field directly. For in-progress download state, use [Model Status](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-status;textable;record) instead.

## Notes
`path` and `success` are required. When `success` is `false`, the `error` field should contain a human-readable explanation. The `total_bytes` and `file_count` fields summarise the download scope and are useful for logging and audit purposes.

path.description: Local filesystem path where the model was downloaded

model_spec.description: The model specification that was downloaded (e.g., 'hf:org/model-name')

title: EditorConfig
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries an `.editorconfig` file that declares per-file-glob formatting rules (indentation style, line endings, character encoding, trailing whitespace) consumed directly by editors and IDE plugins to enforce consistent style without per-developer configuration.

## When to use
Use `media:editorconfig;textable;record` when a pipeline node reads or generates an `.editorconfig` — for example, a project scaffolder that adds editor configuration alongside other dotfiles, or a style audit step that checks for rule consistency.

## Notes
EditorConfig is supported natively or via plugin in virtually all modern editors (VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Vim, Emacs, Sublime Text). Rules are hierarchical: inner directories override outer ones, and `root = true` stops the upward directory search. See also [EditorConfig](https://capdag.com/browse/media:editorconfig;textable;record).

title: Elixir Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Elixir source files contain code written in Elixir, a dynamic, functional language built on the Erlang VM (BEAM) that inherits Erlang's actor model concurrency and fault tolerance while offering a modern Ruby-inspired syntax. Elixir is widely used for scalable web services via the Phoenix framework and distributed systems.

## When to use
Use [Elixir Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:elixir;textable;code) for `.ex` compiled module files and `.exs` Elixir script files. Scripts (`.exs`) are interpreted directly by the Elixir runtime without generating compiled `.beam` files; Mix build scripts and test files commonly use the `.exs` extension.

## Notes
Elixir projects are built with Mix, which uses `mix.exs` as the project definition file. Elixir's macro system (via `defmacro`) enables powerful metaprogramming that can make static analysis challenging. The language interoperates fully with [Erlang Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:erlang;textable;code) at the module level; Elixir modules compile to the same BEAM bytecode.

title: Embedding Vector List
documentation:
## Purpose
Wraps a collection of [Embedding Vector](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-vector;textable;record) records produced from multiple documents or embedding passes in a single batch operation.

## When to use
Use this type when an embedding cap processes more than one source document in a single invocation and the caller needs all results returned together. Prefer the singular `embedding-vector` type when processing one document at a time.

## Notes
Each element in the list conforms to the same schema as a standalone embedding vector record. Consumers should iterate over entries and correlate them with their source documents by position or by the chunk metadata embedded in each entry.

title: Embedding Vector
description: Output from embedding generation including vectors, chunk metadata, and model information
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the result of running an embedding model over one or more chunks of text. Each result entry pairs a chunk (text, offsets, index) with its dense floating-point vector, allowing downstream consumers to build indexes, compute similarity, or store vectors without re-running the model.

## When to use
Use this type as the output of any embedding cap. Consumers expecting a list of pre-computed vectors from a single document or batch should accept this type. For multi-document batches see [Embedding Vector List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-vector;list;record;textable).

## Notes
The `embedding_dim` field on the top-level object must match the length of every `embedding` array in `results`. Implementers should validate this invariant. The `start_offset` and `end_offset` fields are byte offsets into the original source document and can be used to reconstruct which portion of a document each vector covers.

chunk.description: The document chunk this embedding is for

chunk_index.description: Index of this chunk in the document

start_offset.description: Start byte offset in original document

end_offset.description: End byte offset in original document

title: Email Message
documentation:
## Purpose
EML files represent individual email messages in the RFC 822 / RFC 5322 internet message format, including headers and a MIME body. The [Email Message](https://capdag.com/browse/media:eml;textable;record) media type covers any single message file following this standard.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes individual email messages — for example, email archiving pipelines, message parsing nodes, or notification delivery systems. For collections of messages use the [Mbox Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mbox;textable;list) type instead.

## Notes
The `;textable;record` tags indicate that the message is human-readable text and has a structured record shape (headers as key-value pairs, body as content). MIME multipart bodies may contain attachments encoded in Base64. Implementers should use an RFC 5322 parser (e.g., Python's `email` module) to reliably extract headers and body parts rather than splitting on raw newlines.

title: Environment File
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries `.env` files that define environment variable key-value pairs for local development, CI pipelines, and containerized deployments. The format is simple: one `KEY=value` pair per line, with optional comments.

## When to use
Use `media:env;textable;record` when a pipeline node reads, generates, or validates environment configuration — for example, a secret injection step that merges a `.env` file into a container environment, or a linting step that checks for required variables.

## Notes
`.env` files frequently contain sensitive credentials and should never be committed to version control. Tools like `dotenv`, `direnv`, and Docker Compose consume this format. Variable interpolation and multi-line values are supported inconsistently across parsers; check the target parser's documentation. See also [Environment File](https://capdag.com/browse/media:env;textable;record).

title: EPS Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an EPS (Encapsulated PostScript) file, a vector graphics format historically used in professional print and publishing workflows. EPS files contain PostScript language instructions for rendering graphics at arbitrary resolution.

## When to use
Use `media:eps;image` when a node produces or consumes EPS files, typically in print prepress pipelines or when working with legacy design assets. For modern vector graphics interchange, prefer [SVG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:svg;image;textable).

## Notes
EPS files may include a low-resolution raster preview alongside the PostScript data. Rendering requires a PostScript interpreter such as Ghostscript. EPS is largely superseded by PDF and SVG for new workflows.

title: EPUB Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries EPUB e-book files, the open standard format for digital publications maintained by the W3C. EPUB is a ZIP archive containing HTML content, CSS stylesheets, images, and a package manifest, supporting reflowable text that adapts to screen size.

## When to use
Use `media:epub` when a pipeline node produces or processes e-books — for example, a Pandoc conversion step generating EPUB from Markdown or DOCX, a text extraction step for an e-book library, or an e-reader delivery step.

## Notes
EPUB 3 (current standard) uses HTML5, CSS3, and supports MathML and SVG. EPUB 2 files are also common in older libraries. Tools like `epubcheck` validate conformance. For fixed-layout content (e.g., illustrated books), EPUB fixed layout is an option but has lower reader support than reflowable. See also [EPUB Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:epub).

title: Erlang Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Erlang source files contain code written in Erlang, a concurrent, functional language designed at Ericsson for building fault-tolerant, distributed telecommunications systems. Erlang's process model, OTP framework, and "let it crash" philosophy underpin highly available services with nine-nines uptime targets.

## When to use
Use [Erlang Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:erlang;textable;code) for `.erl` module implementation files and `.hrl` header files (which declare record types, macros, and constants shared across modules). Erlang projects are built with `rebar3` or `erlang.mk`.

## Notes
Erlang modules are compiled to `.beam` bytecode that runs on the BEAM virtual machine, the same runtime used by [Elixir Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:elixir;textable;code). The language uses single-assignment variables and pattern matching extensively. OTP behaviors (gen_server, gen_statem, supervisor) are the primary structuring mechanism for production Erlang applications.

title: Exclude Pattern
documentation:
## Purpose

Exclude Pattern specifies items that should be removed from a collection even if they match other selection criteria. It acts as a deny-list filter applied after inclusion rules.

## When to use

Use this media type together with [Include Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:include-pattern;textable) and [Filter Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:filter-pattern;textable) when a node supports a layered matching strategy. Exclude patterns are evaluated last, so they take precedence over include patterns.

## Notes

The pattern syntax is determined by the consuming node. A common convention is glob-style wildcards (e.g., `*.tmp`, `build/**`). Verify the node's documentation before relying on regular expression syntax.

title: OpenEXR Image
description: OpenEXR high dynamic range image data
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an OpenEXR image, the industry-standard HDR format for visual effects and computer-generated imagery. OpenEXR supports 16-bit and 32-bit floating-point channels, multiple layers, and arbitrary channel names.

## When to use
Use `media:exr;image` when a node produces or consumes OpenEXR files, such as a 3D renderer, a compositing node, or a VFX pipeline step. For standard dynamic range images, prefer [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image).

## Notes
OpenEXR files store linear light values and are not suitable for direct display without tone mapping. The format supports multi-channel data including depth, normals, and motion vectors in addition to RGB colour.

title: FBX Model
documentation:
## Purpose
FBX (Filmbox) is a proprietary Autodesk binary format for 3D models, scenes, and animations, widely used in game development, film production, and digital content creation pipelines. The [FBX Model](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fbx;model) media type represents any `.fbx` file in the binary FBX format.

## When to use
Use this type when exchanging 3D assets with tools in the Autodesk ecosystem (Maya, 3ds Max, MotionBuilder) or game engines (Unity, Unreal Engine) that natively import FBX. For open-standard 3D interchange, [GLB](https://capdag.com/browse/media:glb;model) or [glTF](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gltf;model;textable) are preferred when possible.

## Notes
The FBX format is proprietary; Autodesk provides an SDK but the binary format is not publicly documented. There are also ASCII-text FBX variants. Implementers should use the Autodesk FBX SDK or a compatible open-source library such as `ufbx` or Open Asset Import Library (Assimp). The `;model` tag indicates this is a 3D model artifact.

title: File Path
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a single file system path as a plain-text string, used to identify the location of an input or output file on disk.

## When to use
Use this type when a machine needs to read from or write to a specific file, rather than receiving content inline. Prefer inline content media types when the file is small and the path adds unnecessary indirection.

## Notes
The path must not contain null bytes. Relative or absolute paths are both valid; the interpretation is determined by the machine's runtime context. For multiple paths, use [File Path Array](https://capdag.com/browse/media:file-path;textable;list) instead.

title: Filter Pattern
documentation:
## Purpose

Filter Pattern is a text string that defines a matching rule used to select items from a collection. It acts as a general-purpose selector, and its syntax (glob, regex, or substring) depends on the consuming node.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node needs a caller-supplied pattern to narrow down a set of inputs, files, or results. Combine it with [Include Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:include-pattern;textable) and [Exclude Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:exclude-pattern;textable) for fine-grained control over what passes through.

## Notes

The validation constraint forbids null bytes, but otherwise any printable string is accepted. Consult the documentation of the consuming node to understand whether the pattern is interpreted as a glob, a regular expression, or a literal substring.

title: Fish Script
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Fish shell scripts, which use a syntax intentionally distinct from POSIX sh to improve readability and interactive usability. Fish is commonly used as an interactive shell with autosuggestions and syntax highlighting built in.

## When to use
Use `media:fish;textable;code` when a pipeline node specifically handles `.fish` scripts — for example, a dotfile management step or a shell function installer that targets Fish users. For POSIX-compatible scripts, use [Shell Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:shell;textable;code).

## Notes
Fish scripts are not POSIX-compatible; they cannot be run by `sh` or `bash`. Functions, completions, and configuration are all stored as `.fish` files, typically under `~/.config/fish/`. See also [Fish Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fish;textable;code).

title: FLAC Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a FLAC (Free Lossless Audio Codec) file, an open-source lossless audio format that compresses audio without any quality loss. FLAC is widely used for archival, music libraries, and audio processing pipelines where fidelity must be preserved.

## When to use
Use `media:flac;audio` when a node requires lossless audio — for example an archival step, a mixing pipeline, or a transcription node that benefits from full-quality input. For lossy compressed audio, see [MP3 Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mp3;audio) or [AAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aac;audio).

## Notes
FLAC files are larger than lossy equivalents but smaller than uncompressed WAV or AIFF. The format supports embedded metadata (Vorbis comments), album art, and cue sheets. FLAC decoding is computationally light and well-supported across platforms.

title: Flash Video
documentation:
## Purpose
FLV (Flash Video) is a container format developed by Adobe for delivering video over the internet via the Flash Player plugin. It was the dominant web video format from the mid-2000s until HTML5 video became widespread.

## When to use
