Use [Flash Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flv;video) only when processing or archiving legacy content from platforms that historically used Flash-based players. FLV is not suitable for new production; prefer [WebM Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webm;video) or [MP4](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video) for current web delivery.

## Notes
FLV files may contain Sorenson Spark (H.263), VP6, or H.264 video and MP3 or AAC audio. The related F4V format uses an MPEG-4 container but is often produced by the same Adobe tools. Adobe ended Flash Player support in December 2020; most modern browsers no longer play FLV natively.

title: Force Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that bypasses safety checks, cache lookups, or confirmation gates in a machine, forcing it to execute unconditionally. The exact behavior is machine-specific.

## When to use
Use this flag sparingly and only when you have confirmed that the bypassed checks are not needed for the current run (e.g., clearing a cache, re-running a completed step during debugging).

## Notes
Enabling this flag can have irreversible side effects depending on the machine. Document its intended effect clearly in any graph that uses it. It is separate from [Detailed Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:detailed-flag;textable), which controls output verbosity rather than execution behavior.

title: F# Source
documentation:
## Purpose
F# source files contain code written in F#, a functional-first, strongly typed language in the .NET ecosystem developed by Microsoft Research. F# is used for data science, financial modeling, domain-driven design, and any domain that benefits from immutable data and functional composition.

## When to use
Use [F# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fsharp;textable;code) for `.fs` implementation files, `.fsi` signature files (which declare the public interface of a module, analogous to C headers), and `.fsx` script files that run without a compilation step.

## Notes
F# source ordering matters: files and declarations within a file must appear before they are used, which is enforced by the compiler. F# interoperates fully with [C# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csharp;textable;code) and other .NET languages at the assembly level. The functional style means heavy use of discriminated unions, pattern matching, and pipeline operators, which may require F#-aware parsers for static analysis.

title: Genre
documentation:
## Purpose

Genre specifies the literary or content category of the text to be generated, guiding the model toward appropriate structural and stylistic conventions for that form.

## When to use

Use this media type as an input to creative or editorial writing nodes. Combine it with [Tone](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tone;textable) and [Creativity Level](https://capdag.com/browse/media:creativity-level;textable;numeric) to fully shape the character of the output.

## Notes

The allowed values span a range from purely creative forms (fiction, poetry) to professional forms (technical, academic, marketing). Choose the value that best matches the intended audience and publication context.

title: GeoJSON
documentation:
## Purpose
GeoJSON (RFC 7946) is a JSON-based format for encoding geographic features — points, lines, polygons, and their properties — in a coordinate reference system (WGS 84 by default). The [GeoJSON](https://capdag.com/browse/media:geojson;record;textable) media type represents any `.geojson` file containing a valid GeoJSON object.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes geographic vector data — for example, map layers, location datasets, geofencing boundaries, or route geometries. GeoJSON is the standard interchange format for web mapping tools such as Leaflet, Mapbox, and the Google Maps API.

## Notes
The top-level object is one of `FeatureCollection`, `Feature`, or a geometry type (`Point`, `LineString`, `Polygon`, etc.). The `;record;textable` tags indicate that the file is a structured record and human-readable text. Implementers should validate coordinate winding order (counterclockwise for exterior rings) as required by RFC 7946 and handle antimeridian-crossing geometries explicitly.

title: GGUF Model
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a binary file in the GGUF (GGML Universal File) format, the standard container for quantized LLM weights used by llama.cpp and compatible runtimes. GGUF files store model weights, vocabulary, and metadata in a single self-describing binary file.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap produces or consumes a GGUF model file directly. The GGUF format is the primary format for CPU and hybrid CPU/GPU inference. Use the corresponding model spec type [GGUF LLM Model Spec](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-spec;gguf;textable;llm) to reference a GGUF model by identifier rather than by file.

## Notes
GGUF files are identified by the `.gguf` file extension and the `application/octet-stream` MIME type. A model repository may contain multiple GGUF files at different quantization levels; the `model-spec` query string can filter to the desired quantization using `include` and `exclude` glob patterns.

title: GIF Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a GIF image, which may be a static raster image or an animated sequence of frames. GIF supports a maximum palette of 256 colours per frame and lossless compression.

## When to use
Use `media:gif;image` when a node produces or consumes GIF files, particularly animated GIFs. For static images requiring more colours or better compression, prefer [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [WebP Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webp;image).

## Notes
GIF supports transparency via a single transparent colour index, not an alpha channel. Animated GIFs can have large file sizes; consumers that display or process animations should be prepared for multi-frame content. Modern alternatives such as WebP or AVIF offer better compression for equivalent quality.

title: Git Ignore
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a `.gitignore` file containing patterns that tell Git which files and directories to exclude from version control. Proper ignore rules prevent build artifacts, secrets, and editor clutter from polluting the repository history.

## When to use
Use `media:gitignore;textable;list` when a pipeline node reads, generates, or merges `.gitignore` files — for example, a project scaffolder that assembles a language-specific ignore file or a security audit step checking whether sensitive paths are excluded.

## Notes
Git evaluates ignore patterns from multiple files: the repository-level `.gitignore`, per-directory `.gitignore` files, and a global user ignore file. Patterns support `*`, `**`, `?`, and negation with `!`. See also [Git Ignore](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gitignore;textable;list).

title: GLB Model
documentation:
## Purpose
GLB is the binary container form of the glTF 2.0 format, packing JSON scene description, binary geometry/animation data, and embedded textures into a single self-contained file. The [GLB Model](https://capdag.com/browse/media:glb;model) media type represents any `.glb` file conforming to the glTF 2.0 specification.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes 3D assets for real-time rendering — for example, web-based 3D viewers (three.js, Babylon.js), AR/VR pipelines, or game engines. GLB is the preferred packaging over [glTF](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gltf;model;textable) when all assets must travel as a single opaque artifact without external file references.

## Notes
A GLB file starts with a 12-byte header (`glTF`, version 2, total length) followed by a JSON chunk and an optional binary chunk. Implementers should use a glTF 2.0-compliant loader; the Khronos glTF-Validator tool can detect structural errors before processing. The `;model` tag indicates this is a 3D model artifact.

title: glTF Model
documentation:
## Purpose
glTF (GL Transmission Format) 2.0 is an open standard for efficient transmission and loading of 3D scenes and models, defined by the Khronos Group. The [glTF Model](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gltf;model;textable) media type represents the text (JSON) form of a glTF 2.0 file, which references external binary buffers and textures.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes 3D scene files that may reference external assets — for example, workflows that separately manage textures and geometry, or toolchains where human-readable scene description is needed for inspection and editing. When all assets must be bundled into one file, use [GLB](https://capdag.com/browse/media:glb;model) instead.

## Notes
A `.gltf` file is UTF-8 JSON. The `;model;textable` tags indicate it is both a 3D model and human-readable. External binary buffers are referenced via URIs in the `buffers` array; textures are referenced similarly. Implementers should resolve relative URIs against the `.gltf` file's base path and load all referenced files before parsing the scene graph.

title: Go Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Go source files contain code written in the Go programming language (also called Golang), developed by Google. Go is a statically typed, compiled language designed for simplicity, fast build times, and built-in concurrency via goroutines and channels.

## When to use
Use [Go Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:go;textable;code) for any `.go` file, including application code, test files (which follow the `_test.go` naming convention), and generated code. Go projects are organized into modules declared by `go.mod`.

## Notes
Go source is UTF-8 text. The `gofmt` tool enforces a canonical formatting style; unformatted Go code is considered non-idiomatic. Go supports build constraints (formerly `// +build`, now `//go:build`) embedded in source comments, which implementers must parse to understand conditional compilation.

title: Go Module
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a `go.mod` file that declares a Go module's identity, the minimum Go toolchain version, and its direct and indirect dependencies with their required minimum versions. Every Go module must have a `go.mod` at its root.

## When to use
Use `media:gomod;textable` when a pipeline node reads or updates Go module metadata — for example, a dependency update step running `go get`, a vulnerability scanner checking declared versions, or a build step that parses the module path to resolve imports.

## Notes
The `go.mod` format is managed by the `go` command and should not be edited by hand beyond simple version pins. Changes to `go.mod` are always accompanied by corresponding changes to [Go Checksum](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gosum;textable;list) (`go.sum`). See also [Go Module](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gomod;textable).

title: Go Checksum
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a `go.sum` file that records the cryptographic hashes of all module zip archives and `go.mod` files that a Go module depends on, either directly or transitively. The Go toolchain verifies these hashes on every build to detect tampering or supply chain corruption.

## When to use
Use `media:gosum;textable;list` when a pipeline node audits, regenerates, or validates Go dependency checksums — for example, a security scan step that cross-references hashes against a transparency log, or a CI step verifying that `go.sum` is consistent with `go.mod`.

## Notes
The `go.sum` file is maintained automatically by `go mod tidy` and related commands. It should be committed to version control alongside [Go Module](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gomod;textable) (`go.mod`). Removing or editing entries manually can break builds. See also [Go Checksum](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gosum;textable;list).

title: GPX
documentation:
## Purpose
GPX (GPS Exchange Format) is an XML schema for representing GPS tracks, routes, and waypoints with coordinates, elevation, and timestamps. The [GPX](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gpx;textable) media type represents any `.gpx` file conforming to the GPX 1.0 or 1.1 schema.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes GPS-recorded data — for example, fitness tracking exports, survey route recordings, vehicle telemetry, or hiking trail datasets. GPX is the standard output format of GPS devices and applications such as Garmin devices, Strava, and OpenStreetMap tools.

## Notes
GPX files are UTF-8 XML. The `;textable` tag indicates the file is human-readable. The three main elements are `<wpt>` (waypoints), `<rte>` (routes), and `<trk>` (tracks with segments). Implementers should use an XML parser with namespace awareness; the GPX 1.1 namespace is `http://www.topografix.com/GPX/1/1`.

title: GZIP Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
GZIP is a single-file compression format using the DEFLATE algorithm, defined in RFC 1952. It compresses one input stream and is the most universally available compression format on Unix-like systems.

## When to use
Use [GZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gzip;archive) when compressing a single file for transfer or storage and broad toolchain compatibility is important. GZIP is the standard compression layer for HTTP content encoding and is natively supported by virtually every operating system and programming language.

## Notes
GZIP operates on a single stream and cannot bundle multiple files; use [TAR.GZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:targz;archive) to compress a directory tree. Compression ratio is moderate — [XZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive) and [Zstandard](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zstd;archive) offer better ratios or speed respectively. The `.gz` extension may also appear alongside other extensions (e.g., `.json.gz`) indicating a gzip-wrapped file of another type.

title: Haskell Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Haskell source files contain code written in Haskell, a purely functional, statically typed language with lazy evaluation and a strong type system featuring type classes, higher-kinded types, and monads. Haskell is used in compiler research, formal verification, financial systems, and wherever correctness guarantees are paramount.

## When to use
Use [Haskell Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:haskell;textable;code) for `.hs` standard source files and `.lhs` literate Haskell files (where code is interspersed with prose in a Bird-style or LaTeX-style format). Haskell projects are typically managed with Cabal or Stack, with `cabal.project` or `stack.yaml` as the project root.

## Notes
Haskell's lazy evaluation model means expressions are not evaluated until their results are needed, which changes reasoning about performance and memory usage compared to strict languages. The Glasgow Haskell Compiler (GHC) supports numerous language extensions via `{-# LANGUAGE ... #-}` pragmas that significantly expand the type system; tools must account for active extensions when parsing source.

title: HDF5 Data
documentation:
## Purpose
HDF5 (Hierarchical Data Format version 5) is a binary format for storing large, complex, multidimensional datasets with rich metadata. The [HDF5 Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:hdf5) media type represents any `.h5` or `.hdf5` file produced by the HDF5 library.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes scientific or numerical datasets that are too large or multi-dimensional for tabular formats — for example, simulation outputs, telescope observations, climate model data, or neural network weight checkpoints. HDF5 is well suited when the data has a hierarchical group structure with attached attributes.

## Notes
HDF5 files can contain datasets, groups, named types, and arbitrary metadata attributes. Implementers should use the HDF5 C library or a binding such as `h5py` (Python) or `HDF5.jl` (Julia). Consumers should be aware that parallel HDF5 writes require MPI-enabled builds; single-writer/multiple-reader (SWMR) mode is available for concurrent access patterns.

title: HDR Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Radiance HDR (RGBE) image, a high dynamic range format commonly used in lighting and environment map workflows. HDR files store floating-point luminance values using a shared exponent encoding.

## When to use
Use `media:hdr;image` when a node produces or consumes Radiance HDR environment maps or HDR photographs, particularly in 3D rendering or image-based lighting (IBL) pipelines. For higher-fidelity HDR needs, prefer [OpenEXR Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:exr;image).

## Notes
Radiance HDR uses a lossy RGBE encoding that trades precision for compact file size compared to full floating-point formats like OpenEXR. HDR images require tone mapping before display on standard dynamic range monitors.

title: HEIC Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a HEIC/HEIF image, the High Efficiency Image Container format used by Apple devices and other modern cameras. HEIC uses HEVC (H.265) compression and supports HDR, wide colour gamut, depth maps, and multiple images in a single file.

## When to use
Use `media:heic;image` when a node processes images captured by iOS devices or other HEIC-capable cameras. Because HEIC support is limited outside Apple platforms, pipelines that need broad compatibility should convert to [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image) or [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) early in the workflow.

## Notes
HEIC files may contain multiple still images, live photo sequences, or depth data alongside the primary image. Consumers should handle both `.heic` and `.heif` extensions, as both refer to the same underlying container.

title: HTML Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an HTML document containing markup for structure, links, and embedded media. HTML is the primary format for web content and is also commonly produced by documentation tools and email renderers.

## When to use
Use `media:html;textable` when a node produces or consumes HTML content — for example a web scraper output, a rendered documentation page, or a formatted report. For plain prose without markup, use [Plain Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:txt;textable).

## Notes
HTML content may reference external resources (stylesheets, images) that are not included in the artifact. Nodes that render HTML should be prepared to handle or ignore external dependencies. The `textable` tag allows HTML to be consumed by any generic text node, though parsing will be needed to extract meaningful text.

title: ICNS Icon
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an Apple ICNS file, the native macOS icon container format. ICNS files store multiple resolutions and pixel densities of an icon in a single file, from 16x16 to 1024x1024, including Retina variants.

## When to use
Use `media:icns;image` when a node produces or consumes macOS application icons. ICNS files are required when packaging macOS applications and are not used on other platforms. For cross-platform icon formats, see [ICO Icon](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ico;image).

## Notes
An ICNS file is a container of multiple image representations. Tools that generate ICNS files typically require PNG source images at each supported resolution. Direct pixel-level access requires parsing the ICNS container structure.

title: ICO Icon
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Windows ICO file, a container format that stores one or more images at multiple sizes and colour depths for use as application or file-type icons on Windows and in web browsers (favicon).

## When to use
Use `media:ico;image` when a node produces or consumes ICO files, such as a favicon generator or a Windows application packaging step. For macOS icons, see [ICNS Icon](https://capdag.com/browse/media:icns;image).

## Notes
ICO files commonly include 16x16, 32x32, 48x48, and 256x256 PNG or BMP frames. Modern browsers accept PNG-encoded ICO files for favicons. Consumers that extract individual frames should parse the ICO container structure.

title: iCalendar
documentation:
## Purpose
iCalendar (RFC 5545) is a text format for representing calendar events, tasks, and scheduling data. The [iCalendar](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ics;textable;list) media type represents `.ics` files that may contain one or more `VEVENT`, `VTODO`, or `VJOURNAL` components.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes calendar data — for example, scheduling automation, event import/export, meeting invitations, or calendar feed aggregation. It is the universal interchange format supported by Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Outlook, and most other calendar applications.

## Notes
An iCalendar file begins with `BEGIN:VCALENDAR` and ends with `END:VCALENDAR`. The `;textable;list` tags reflect that the file is human-readable text containing an ordered list of calendar components. Implementers should use an RFC 5545-compliant parser (e.g., `icalendar` in Python) to handle recurrence rules, time zone definitions, and property parameter encoding correctly.

title: Image description
description: Description of the content of an image, as generated by a vision inference model
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the natural-language output produced by a vision model when asked to describe an image. The payload is plain text, but the `image-description` tag conveys that the text is specifically a model-generated caption or description rather than arbitrary prose.

## When to use
Use `media:image-description;textable` as the output type of vision inference nodes that produce textual descriptions of images. Downstream nodes expecting general text may treat this interchangeably with [Plain Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:txt;textable), while nodes that specifically aggregate or index image descriptions can match on the more specific type.

## Notes
The text content is unstructured natural language. If a structured representation (e.g. tags, bounding boxes) is needed, a different media type should be defined. The `textable` tag allows this type to participate in any pipeline that accepts readable text.

title: PNG Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a PNG-encoded raster image with semantic context indicating it is an image ready for vision or display use. Distinguished from the bare [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) type by the `image` tag, which makes routing to vision inference nodes explicit.

## When to use
Use `media:image;png` when a pipeline step expects a PNG specifically in the role of a visual artifact — for example as input to a vision model or image description node. For raw PNG bytes without semantic image context, use [PNG Image Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png).

## Notes
PNG supports lossless compression and alpha transparency, making it appropriate for screenshots, diagrams, and UI renderings. For photographic content where file size matters, prefer JPEG or WebP.

title: Include Comments Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that controls whether inline code comments are retained or stripped when a machine processes source code. Affects both analysis accuracy and token budget.

## When to use
Enable when comments contain intent, documentation, or context that the model should consider. Disable to reduce token usage or to focus processing on executable code only.

## Notes
See also [Include Tests Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:include-tests-flag;textable) for controlling test file inclusion and [Remove Boilerplate](https://capdag.com/browse/media:remove-boilerplate;textable) for stripping standard boilerplate patterns.

title: Include Order Indexes
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that instructs a machine to annotate output items with their ordinal position in the original sequence, preserving ordering information for downstream consumers.

## When to use
Enable when output items may be reordered, filtered, or merged with other data and the original sequence position must be recoverable. Disable when order is implicit or irrelevant to downstream processing.

## Notes
Order indexes are typically zero-based integers attached to each output record. The exact field name and format depend on the machine's output schema.

title: Include Pattern
documentation:
## Purpose

Include Pattern specifies items that should be explicitly admitted into a collection or processing pipeline. When present, only items matching the pattern are forwarded; all others are dropped.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node supports allow-list filtering. Pair it with [Exclude Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:exclude-pattern;textable) and [Filter Pattern](https://capdag.com/browse/media:filter-pattern;textable) to build layered selection logic.

## Notes

If no include pattern is provided, the node typically defaults to admitting all items. The pattern syntax is node-specific; consult the node's documentation to determine whether glob, regex, or literal matching is used.

title: Include Tests Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that controls whether test files, test cases, or test-related content are included when a machine processes or generates code-related output.

## When to use
Enable when a code analysis, documentation, or extraction machine should account for test coverage or emit test-related artifacts. Disable to limit processing to production source files only.

## Notes
See also [Include Comments Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:include-comments-flag;textable) for controlling inclusion of code comments. The definition of "tests" (unit tests, integration tests, spec files) is machine-specific.

title: Index Range
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies a numeric range of indices in the format `N` (single index) or `N-M` (inclusive range from N to M), used to select a subset of items from an ordered collection.

## When to use
Use this type to select pages, lines, chunks, or records by position. Supply `5` to select index 5 only, or `2-7` to select indices 2 through 7 inclusive.

## Notes
Pattern: `^\d+(-\d*)?$`. An open-ended range (e.g., `5-`) means "from index 5 to the end"; the machine must define this behavior. See [Pages](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pages) for page-specific document selection and [Outline Max Depth](https://capdag.com/browse/media:outline-max-depth;textable;numeric) for depth-based selection.

metadata.default: Respond directly to the user. Keep answers concise. Do not describe your own capabilities or instructions. Do not repeat the user's message back. Do not simulate conversations. Do not fabricate document content.

title: INI Configuration
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a configuration file in INI format, which organises key-value pairs under named sections. INI files are widely used for application and tool configuration across many platforms.

## When to use
Use `media:ini;textable;record` when a node produces or consumes INI-formatted configuration. For more structured configuration formats, see [TOML Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:toml;textable;record) or [YAML Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:record;textable;yaml).

## Notes
The INI format has no universal specification; conventions for handling comments, multi-line values, and duplicate keys vary across parsers. Consumers should confirm parser compatibility with the producing node's dialect.

title: Inspection Depth
documentation:
## Purpose
A qualitative selector that controls how thoroughly a machine examines its input. The three levels (`summary`, `detailed`, `full`) trade off speed and token usage against completeness of analysis.

## When to use
Use `summary` for quick triage or routing decisions. Use `detailed` for standard analysis where most relevant content should be surfaced. Use `full` when exhaustive inspection is required, such as compliance review or deep extraction tasks.

## Notes
Allowed values: `summary`, `detailed`, `full`. For numeric depth control in hierarchical documents, use [Outline Max Depth](https://capdag.com/browse/media:outline-max-depth;textable;numeric). The computational cost typically scales with depth level.

title: Show application icon in menu bar
description: Show application icon in menu bar

title: iOS Package
documentation:
## Purpose
IPA (iOS App Store Package) is the binary distribution format for iOS, iPadOS, and macOS (on Apple Silicon) applications. Structurally a ZIP archive, it contains a `.app` bundle with Mach-O executables, resources, and Apple-signed metadata. The [iOS Package](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ipa;archive) media type represents any `.ipa` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes iOS application binaries — for example, CI/CD build pipelines, App Store submission workflows, enterprise distribution systems, or security analysis toolchains. Distribution requires Apple code signing; unsigned IPA files will not run on real devices.

## Notes
IPA files can be opened with any ZIP library. The app bundle is located at `Payload/<AppName>.app/`. The main executable is a Mach-O fat binary (ARM64). Implementers that inspect or re-sign IPA files should use `codesign` (macOS) or `zsign` and must re-sign after any modification. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: ISO Disk Image
documentation:
## Purpose
ISO 9660 is the standard file system format for optical disc images (CD-ROM, DVD). An `.iso` file is a sector-by-sector image of a disc, typically containing a bootable operating system, software distribution, or read-only data archive. The [ISO Disk Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:iso;archive) media type represents any `.iso` image file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes optical disc images — for example, OS distribution pipelines, virtual machine provisioning workflows, bootable media creation, or long-term data archiving. ISO images are the standard delivery format for Linux distributions, Windows installation media, and software that historically shipped on physical media.

## Notes
ISO 9660 images may also include supplemental file systems (Joliet for Windows long filenames, Rock Ridge for POSIX attributes, El Torito for bootability). Implementers can mount ISO files on Linux with `mount -o loop`, on macOS with `hdiutil attach`, or on Windows natively. The `xorriso` and `genisoimage` tools support creating ISO images with custom boot records. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: JAR Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
JAR (Java ARchive) is a ZIP-format archive containing compiled Java `.class` files, resources, and optional metadata in `META-INF/MANIFEST.MF`. The [JAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jar;archive) media type represents any `.jar` file used for Java library or application distribution.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes Java libraries or standalone executable JARs — for example, Maven/Gradle build artifact pipelines, dependency distribution, Java agent deployment, or security scanning workflows that inspect bytecode. For Java web application bundles use [WAR](https://capdag.com/browse/media:war;archive).

## Notes
JAR files are ZIP archives and can be opened with any ZIP library. Executable JARs specify a `Main-Class` attribute in `MANIFEST.MF`. Signed JARs include `.SF` and `.RSA`/`.DSA` files in `META-INF/`; re-zipping breaks the signature. Implementers should use the JDK `jar` tool or libraries such as `java.util.jar` for reliable manifest handling. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: Java Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Java source files contain code written in the Java programming language, a class-based, object-oriented language designed for platform independence via the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Java is widely used in enterprise backends, Android development, and large-scale distributed systems.

## When to use
Use [Java Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:java;textable;code) for `.java` files containing class, interface, enum, or annotation declarations. Java source is compiled to `.class` bytecode files before execution; this media type covers the source form only.

## Notes
Java source files are conventionally named after the public class they declare (one public class per file). The Java version (language level) affects available syntax features — records, sealed classes, and pattern matching were introduced in later versions. Build tools such as Maven and Gradle manage compilation and dependency resolution.

title: JavaScript Source
documentation:
## Purpose
JavaScript source files contain code written in ECMAScript, the scripting language that powers web browser interactivity and Node.js server-side applications. JavaScript is the dominant language of the web and a common choice for full-stack development.

## When to use
Use [JavaScript Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:javascript;textable;code) for `.js`, `.mjs`, and `.cjs` files. The `.mjs` extension signals ES module format (using `import`/`export`), while `.cjs` signals CommonJS format (using `require`). For React JSX files, use [JavaScript JSX Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jsx;textable;code) instead.

## Notes
JavaScript is dynamically typed and interpreted; no compilation step is required for execution in a browser or Node.js environment, though bundlers (Webpack, Rollup, esbuild) and transpilers (Babel) are common in production workflows. TypeScript files (`.ts`) are distinct — see [TypeScript Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:typescript;textable;code).

title: JPEG Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a JPEG-encoded raster image. JPEG is a lossy compression format optimised for photographic content, achieving small file sizes at the cost of some image quality.

## When to use
Use `media:jpeg;image` when a node produces or consumes JPEG images — for example photographs, camera captures, or compressed image exports. For images requiring lossless quality or transparency, prefer [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [WebP Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webp;image).

## Notes
JPEG does not support transparency. Repeated re-compression degrades quality; nodes that modify JPEG images should work with a lossless intermediate where possible and re-encode only once at the final output step.

title: JSON Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a JSON document that also conforms to the JSON Schema specification. The `json-schema` tag signals that the payload can be used to validate other JSON documents, not merely that it is valid JSON itself.

## When to use
Use `media:json;json-schema;textable;record` when a node produces or requires a JSON Schema — for example a schema generator, a validator configuration, or a tool that introspects API contracts. For ordinary JSON objects, use [JSON Object](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;record).

## Notes
JSON Schema documents are themselves JSON objects, so this type inherits the `record` and `textable` traits. Consumers that simply need to read or display the content may treat this as a standard [JSON Object](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;record).

title: JSON Object
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a single JSON object (key-value map) as the primary payload. The `record` tag indicates a structured mapping, making this type suitable for configuration objects, API responses, and any structured data with named fields.

## When to use
Use `media:json;textable;record` when a node produces or consumes a JSON object at the top level. For a JSON array of objects, use [JSON Array of Objects](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;list;record;textable). For a bare JSON value that may not be an object, use [JSON Value](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable).

## Notes
The `textable` tag allows this type to flow to any generic text consumer. Structured nodes should parse the JSON before processing field values.

title: JSON Value
description: Single JSON value - can be object, string, number, boolean, or null
documentation:
## Purpose
The most permissive JSON type, representing any valid JSON value at the top level: object, array, string, number, boolean, or null. Use this when the shape of the JSON is unknown or intentionally unconstrained.

## When to use
Use `media:json;textable` when a node may produce or consume JSON of varying or unknown structure. When the structure is known to be an object, prefer [JSON Object](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;record). When it is an array, prefer [JSON Array](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;list).

## Notes
Nodes that accept this type must be prepared to handle any JSON value type. Consumers that require a specific shape should validate the payload against the expected structure before processing.

title: JSON Array
description: JSON array containing multiple values or objects
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a JSON array at the top level. The `list` tag signals that the payload is a sequence of items, which may be values, objects, or mixed types.

## When to use
Use `media:json;textable;list` when a node produces or expects a JSON array as its root element. If each array element is a JSON object, prefer the more specific [JSON Array of Objects](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;list;record;textable).

## Notes
Array element types are not constrained by this media spec alone. Consumers that require homogeneous elements should validate individual items after parsing.

title: JSON Array of Objects
description: JSON array where each element is an object with key-value fields
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a JSON array in which every element is a JSON object with key-value fields. This is the typical shape of tabular data or collections of structured records serialised as JSON.

## When to use
Use `media:json;list;record;textable` when a node produces or consumes a homogeneous array of objects — for example a list of search results, database rows, or structured log entries. For a simple array of mixed values, use [JSON Array](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;list).

## Notes
Consumers may assume all elements share the same field names, but should handle missing or null fields gracefully. The `textable` tag allows this type to flow to any generic text consumer.

title: JavaScript JSX Source
documentation:
## Purpose
JSX files contain JavaScript with embedded XML-like syntax extensions (JSX) used primarily with the React library to describe user interface trees. JSX is a compile-time syntax transformation and is not valid plain JavaScript.

## When to use
Use [JavaScript JSX Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jsx;textable;code) for React component files with the `.jsx` extension. If the file is TypeScript with JSX, use [TypeScript JSX Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tsx;textable;code) instead. Plain JavaScript without JSX should use [JavaScript Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:javascript;textable;code).

## Notes
JSX must be transformed to standard JavaScript before execution, typically via Babel, esbuild, or the TypeScript compiler. The JSX transform can target React's `createElement` API (classic) or the newer automatic runtime introduced in React 17. Linters and formatters must be configured to understand JSX syntax.

title: Julia Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Julia source files contain code written in Julia, a high-level, high-performance language designed for numerical and scientific computing. Julia achieves near-C speeds through LLVM-based just-in-time compilation while offering Python-like expressiveness.

## When to use
Use [Julia Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:julia;textable;code) for `.jl` files in Julia packages or scripts. Julia is commonly used in scientific research, computational mathematics, machine learning research, and quantitative finance where both performance and interactivity are required.

## Notes
Julia uses multiple dispatch as its core paradigm — functions are selected based on the types of all arguments, not just the receiver. Julia packages are managed via the built-in `Pkg` system with `Project.toml` and `Manifest.toml` files. The first-run compilation latency (time-to-first-plot problem) was a known issue in earlier versions; Julia 1.9+ significantly improved precompilation.

title: Jupyter Notebook
documentation:
## Purpose
Jupyter Notebook files (`.ipynb`) are JSON documents that store an ordered sequence of cells, each containing source code, markdown text, or raw content alongside captured cell outputs. The [Jupyter Notebook](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jupyter;record;textable) media type covers any file conforming to the nbformat specification.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a reproducible computational document — for example, data analysis reports, model training walkthroughs, or interactive tutorials that mix code and narrative. Jupyter files are the standard exchange format for notebooks in Python, R, and Julia ecosystems.

## Notes
The `;record;textable` tags indicate that the notebook is both a structured record and human-readable text. Cells have a `cell_type` field (`code`, `markdown`, or `raw`) and outputs are embedded as JSON. Implementers processing notebooks programmatically should use the `nbformat` library to validate and manipulate cells rather than parsing raw JSON.

title: Apple Keynote
documentation:
## Purpose
Apple Keynote is a presentation application, and its `.key` / `.keynote` format is a ZIP-based package containing SNAPPY-compressed Protobuf data alongside slide images and other resources. The [Apple Keynote](https://capdag.com/browse/media:keynote) media type represents any Keynote presentation file produced by Keynote for macOS or iOS.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes presentation files originating from the Apple Keynote application — for example, document archiving, slide content extraction, or conversion pipelines that transform Keynote files into PDF or other formats. For cross-platform presentation interchange, PPTX or PDF is preferred.

## Notes
The Keynote format is proprietary and uses the same ZIP + Protobuf structure as Numbers. Implementers that need to extract text or thumbnail images should use AppleScript automation on macOS (to export via Keynote itself) or open-source tools such as `keynote-export`. Direct binary parsing is impractical without reverse-engineered Protobuf schemas. Like Numbers, format detection requires checking ZIP magic bytes and internal path structure.

title: KML
documentation:
## Purpose
KML (Keyhole Markup Language) is an XML-based format for expressing geographic annotation and visualization within maps and Earth browsers. The [KML](https://capdag.com/browse/media:kml;textable) media type represents `.kml` files conforming to the OGC KML standard.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes geographic data intended for Google Earth, Google Maps, or other KML-capable viewers — for example, annotated maps, tour paths, or overlay layers with styling information. For simpler vector data interchange without styling, [GeoJSON](https://capdag.com/browse/media:geojson;record;textable) is often a lighter alternative.

## Notes
KML files are UTF-8 XML with namespace `http://www.opengis.net/kml/2.2`. The `;textable` tag indicates the file is human-readable. Key elements include `Placemark`, `Folder`, `GroundOverlay`, and `NetworkLink`. Implementers should note that KML uses longitude-latitude order (not latitude-longitude) in coordinate tuples, and optional altitude is interpreted relative to the `altitudeMode` element.

title: Kotlin Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Kotlin source files contain code written in Kotlin, a statically typed JVM language developed by JetBrains that is fully interoperable with Java. Kotlin is the preferred language for Android development and is also used for server-side JVM applications and Kotlin Multiplatform targets.

## When to use
Use [Kotlin Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:kotlin;textable;code) for `.kt` source files and `.kts` Kotlin script files. Kotlin Script (`.kts`) is used for Gradle build files and standalone scripts that do not require a compilation step; implementations may need to distinguish these from regular source.

## Notes
Kotlin compiles to JVM bytecode, JavaScript, or native binaries depending on the target. The language is null-safe by default, with nullable types expressed explicitly. Kotlin Multiplatform allows sharing code across JVM, iOS, and JS targets from a single source set, which can complicate source analysis that assumes a single compilation target.

title: Language Code
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a two-letter language code that machines use to tag or constrain content by language. Functionally equivalent to [Language](https://capdag.com/browse/media:language;textable) but signals explicit BCP 47 intent.

## When to use
Use this type when a machine port is documented to accept a language code rather than a free-text language name. Both types share the same validation set, so they are interchangeable in most graphs.

## Notes
Allowed values: `en`, `es`, `fr`, `de`, `it`, `pt`, `ru`, `zh`, `ja`, `ko`. For general language selection, [Language](https://capdag.com/browse/media:language;textable) is preferred unless the downstream machine specifically requires a coded value.

title: Language
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies the natural language in which a machine should produce or expect content. Uses two-letter language codes drawn from the allowed set.

## When to use
Attach this parameter to translation, summarization, or generation machines that must target a specific language. If the desired language is not in the allowed set, check [Language Code](https://capdag.com/browse/media:language-code;textable) for an equivalent entry.

## Notes
Allowed values: `en`, `es`, `fr`, `de`, `it`, `pt`, `ru`, `zh`, `ja`, `ko`. This type and [Language Code](https://capdag.com/browse/media:language-code;textable) share the same validation set; prefer `language` for human-readable configuration and `language-code` when the intent is explicitly a BCP 47-style tag.

title: LaTeX Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries LaTeX source files used for typesetting technical documents, academic papers, theses, and books. LaTeX provides precise control over typography and mathematical notation and is the standard format in many scientific disciplines.

## When to use
Use `media:latex;textable` when a pipeline node compiles, analyzes, or generates LaTeX — for example, a `pdflatex` or `xelatex` compilation step, a bibliography manager, or an LLM node that produces math-heavy reports in LaTeX syntax.

## Notes
LaTeX documents are compiled in multiple passes to resolve cross-references and generate a table of contents. The `.tex` extension is standard; `.latex` is less common but accepted. Auxiliary files (`.aux`, `.bbl`, `.log`) are produced during compilation and are not covered by this type. See also [LaTeX Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:latex;textable).

title: Less Stylesheet
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Less stylesheet files, a CSS preprocessor language that extends plain CSS with variables, nesting, mixins, functions, and arithmetic expressions. Less files are compiled to standard CSS before serving.

## When to use
Use `media:less;textable` when a pipeline node compiles or analyzes Less — for example, a build step invoking the Less compiler, or a migration tool converting a Less codebase to SCSS or native CSS custom properties.

## Notes
Less uses `@variable` syntax for variables, which conflicts with CSS at-rules. Modern projects often prefer SCSS or native CSS variables; Less remains common in older Bootstrap-based projects. See also [Less Stylesheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:less;textable).

title: Lisp Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Common Lisp source files, covering the ANSI-standardized dialect used in production systems (SBCL, CCL, ABCL). Common Lisp supports multiple programming paradigms and has a powerful macro system for domain-specific languages.

## When to use
Use `media:lisp;textable;code` when a pipeline node processes `.lisp`, `.cl`, or `.lsp` source files — for example, a compilation step targeting a Lisp image or a tool that performs code analysis across a Quicklisp-managed project.

## Notes
The `.cl` extension is the most common convention for Common Lisp files; `.lisp` and `.lsp` are also in wide use. This type does not cover Scheme or Clojure, which have their own media types. See also [Lisp Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:lisp;textable;code).

title: LLM Generation Request
description: Request object for LLM text generation containing prompt, model specification, and generation parameters
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries all inputs needed to invoke an LLM text generation cap: the prompt, the model to use, and any sampling or constraint parameters. A single request object lets callers express the full generation intent without needing multiple input ports.

## When to use
Pass this type to any cap that performs text generation, vocabulary inspection (`get_vocab`), or model information retrieval (`get_info`). For streaming responses the cap should produce a [LLM Text Stream](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-text-stream;ndjson); for batch responses it produces [Generated Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:generated-text;textable;record).

## Notes
The `request_type` field defaults to `generate` when absent. Constraint fields (`grammar`, `json_schema`, `constraint`) are mutually exclusive in practice; implementers should apply the first one present. The `model_spec` value follows the same format described in [Model Spec](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-spec;textable).

prompt.description: The input text prompt for generation

model_spec.description: Model specification (HuggingFace repo, local path, or alias)

json_schema.description: JSON schema constraint for structured output

constraint.description: Constraint specification (json_schema, regex, grammar, or tool_call)

title: Generated Text
description: Generated text structured as a map with metadata.
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the complete text generation result from an LLM cap, including the generated text and any associated metadata such as token counts, finish reason, and timing. This is the non-streaming counterpart to [LLM Text Stream](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-text-stream;ndjson).

## When to use
Use this type when a cap returns the full generated output in one shot rather than streaming tokens. Callers that do not need incremental delivery should prefer this simpler interface over the streaming variant.

## Notes
The outer object is a flat map; implementations should include at minimum a `text` or `generated_text` key containing the model output. Additional metadata keys such as `tokens_generated`, `finish_reason`, and `generation_time_ms` are recommended for observability.

title: LLM Model Info
description: Information about a loaded LLM model including capabilities and parameters
documentation:
## Purpose
Describes a loaded LLM's structural properties and capabilities, including vocabulary size, maximum context length, embedding dimensions, and feature flags such as chat template and tool-use support.

## When to use
Retrieve this type when a cap or orchestrator needs to know model capabilities before issuing a generation request. It is the response to a `get_info` request in a [LLM Generation Request](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-generation-request;json;record).

## Notes
The `model_spec` and `vocab_size` fields are required. All other fields are optional and may be absent if the model file does not expose the corresponding metadata. Callers should treat missing fields as unknown rather than assuming a default value.

context_length.description: Maximum context length the model supports

supports_chat.description: Whether the model has a chat template

supports_tools.description: Whether the model supports tool use

title: LLM Text Stream
description: Streaming output from LLM text generation. Each line is a JSON object with a 'type' field (token, status, complete, tool_request, or error).
documentation:
## Purpose
Provides incremental LLM output as newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON). Each line is a self-contained JSON object that consumers can parse and act on as tokens arrive, without waiting for the full response.

## When to use
Use this type for interactive or latency-sensitive applications where displaying tokens as they are generated improves perceived responsiveness. The final `complete` message signals end of stream and carries aggregate statistics. For non-interactive use, prefer [Generated Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:generated-text;textable;record).

## Notes
The `type` discriminator is required on every message. Consumers must handle all five types: `token` (partial text), `status` (progress update), `complete` (final summary), `tool_request` (the model wants to invoke a tool), and `error` (generation failed). Messages of unknown type should be silently skipped to allow forward compatibility.

title: LLM Vocabulary Response
description: Response containing the model's vocabulary tokens
documentation:
## Purpose
Returns the full token vocabulary of a loaded LLM, enabling token-level analysis, prompt engineering tools, and custom tokenization utilities to operate without a separate tokenizer library.

## When to use
This is the response to a `get_vocab` request in a [LLM Generation Request](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-generation-request;json;record). Use it when you need to enumerate, inspect, or count the tokens the model recognises.

## Notes
The `vocab` array is ordered by token ID. The `vocab_size` field should equal the length of `vocab`; include it as a convenience for consumers that only need the count without iterating the full array. Vocabulary extraction is typically slow on large models; cache the result when possible.

title: Log File
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents the text output of an application or system log. Log files are line-oriented and typically contain timestamps, severity levels, and messages, though the exact format varies by source.

## When to use
Use `media:log;textable` when a node emits or reads log output, such as a machine execution trace, build output, or server log. For general unstructured text, use [Plain Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:txt;textable).

## Notes
Log content is often large and may grow incrementally. Consumers that parse log lines should be tolerant of format variation across different sources. The `textable` tag allows log content to flow to any generic text consumer.

title: Lua Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Lua source files contain code written in Lua, a lightweight, embeddable scripting language designed for easy integration into host applications. Lua is the dominant scripting language for game engines (Roblox, World of Warcraft, Love2D), embedded systems, and configuration systems.

## When to use
Use [Lua Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:lua;textable;code) for `.lua` files, whether standalone scripts or embedded scripts loaded by a host application. Lua is commonly found alongside C/C++ projects that expose a Lua API for scripting and configuration.

## Notes
Lua 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, and 5.4 are not fully compatible with each other; the integer/float split introduced in 5.3 and bitwise operators in 5.3 are common compatibility boundaries. LuaJIT (a JIT compiler for Lua) targets Lua 5.1 semantics with extensions and is widely used in performance-sensitive game contexts. The `luarocks` package manager handles Lua library distribution.

title: LZMA Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
LZMA (Lempel-Ziv-Markov chain Algorithm) is the underlying compression algorithm used by [7-Zip Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:7z;archive) and [XZ Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive). The raw LZMA format wraps this algorithm in a minimal header without the XZ container structure.

## When to use
Use [LZMA Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:lzma;archive) when interoperating with legacy tools or embedded systems that produce raw `.lzma` streams. For new content, prefer [XZ Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive), which adds integrity checking and better streaming support around the same LZMA2 core.

## Notes
Raw LZMA files lack the checksums and stream framing present in XZ. The `.lz` extension is associated with the lzip tool, which uses LZMA with its own container format; implementers should verify which variant is present by inspecting the file magic bytes. LZMA2 (used in XZ and 7z) is an improved variant that supports multi-threading and is distinct from original LZMA.

title: M4A Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an M4A file, an audio-only MPEG-4 container typically carrying AAC-encoded audio. M4A is the default format for purchased or downloaded audio on Apple platforms and is widely used in iTunes and Apple Music libraries.

## When to use
Use `media:m4a;audio` when a node produces or consumes M4A files, such as Apple Music exports or iOS audio recordings. For the raw AAC stream without the MP4 container, see [AAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aac;audio). For lossless audio, see [FLAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flac;audio) or [AIFF Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aiff;audio).

## Notes
M4A files use the `.m4a` extension but are technically MP4 containers. Consumers that handle MP4 video can generally demux M4A files as well. DRM-protected M4A files (M4P) cannot be decoded without the appropriate licence.

title: Makefile
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Makefile source that defines targets, dependencies, and build recipes for the `make` utility. Makefiles are used across nearly every software project for compiling code, running tests, and automating repetitive tasks.

## When to use
Use `media:makefile;textable` when a pipeline node reads or generates a Makefile — for example, a build executor invoking `make`, a dependency graph extractor, or a code generation step that produces a project scaffold with a Makefile included.

## Notes
The primary filename `Makefile` (no extension) is not registered here; this type covers fragment files with the `.mk` extension that are included into a main Makefile. Tab characters are required for recipe lines — spaces are a syntax error. See also [Makefile](https://capdag.com/browse/media:makefile;textable).

title: Max Content Length
documentation:
## Purpose
Limits the number of characters or tokens of document content passed to a machine. Used to prevent oversized inputs from exceeding downstream capacity.

## When to use
Apply this parameter on ingestion or preprocessing machines that must truncate or split content before forwarding it. It complements [Max Context Length](https://capdag.com/browse/media:max-context-length;inference;limit;operator;model;textable;numeric), which governs the model's total window.

## Notes
Valid range: 256–131072. Content exceeding this limit should be chunked using [Chunk Size](https://capdag.com/browse/media:chunk-size;textable;numeric) rather than silently truncated.

title: Mbox Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
Mbox is a family of file formats for storing a collection of email messages as a plain-text file, where each message is delimited by a `From ` separator line. The [Mbox Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mbox;textable;list) media type represents any file following this convention.

## When to use
Use this type when a node exports or imports a batch of email messages as a single file — for example, mailbox backups, email dataset dumps, or migrations between mail servers. For single messages use the [Email Message](https://capdag.com/browse/media:eml;textable;record) type instead.

## Notes
There are several mbox variants (mboxo, mboxrd, mboxcl, mboxcl2) that differ in how they escape embedded `From ` lines. Implementers should use a library that handles the chosen variant's quoting rules rather than splitting on `From ` naively. The `;textable;list` tags indicate this is a human-readable ordered list of messages.

title: Markdown Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Markdown document whose content uses Markdown syntax for headings, lists, links, code blocks, and other formatting. Markdown is widely used for documentation, READMEs, and human-readable reports.

## When to use
Use `media:md;textable` when a node produces or expects Markdown-formatted output, such as a language model that has been prompted to respond in Markdown or a documentation generator. For unformatted prose, use [Plain Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:txt;textable).

## Notes
The `textable` tag means Markdown content can flow to any node that accepts readable text, though nodes that render or further process Markdown should match on this more specific type. Standard CommonMark is assumed; GitHub Flavored Markdown extensions are common but not guaranteed.

title: Critical threshold for available memory in MB
description: Critical threshold for available memory in MB

title: Emergency threshold for available memory in MB
description: Emergency threshold for available memory in MB

title: Pressure threshold for available memory in MB
description: Pressure threshold for available memory in MB

title: Warning threshold for available memory in MB
description: Warning threshold for available memory in MB

title: Mermaid Diagram
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Mermaid diagram source, a text-based syntax for declaring flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts, ER diagrams, and other visual artifacts. Mermaid diagrams are rendered to SVG or PNG by the Mermaid CLI or embedded directly in Markdown-aware renderers like GitHub.

## When to use
Use `media:mermaid;textable` when a pipeline node generates or renders Mermaid diagrams — for example, an LLM node that produces architecture diagrams from natural language, or a rendering step converting `.mmd` files to SVG for documentation.

## Notes
Mermaid syntax is case-sensitive for diagram type keywords. Supported diagram types include `flowchart`, `sequenceDiagram`, `classDiagram`, `stateDiagram`, `erDiagram`, `gantt`, and `pie`. See also [Mermaid Diagram](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mermaid;textable).

title: MIDI Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Standard MIDI File (SMF) containing a sequence of MIDI events — note on/off messages, control changes, tempo, and time signature. MIDI encodes musical performance data rather than audio waveforms.

## When to use
Use `media:midi;audio` when a node produces or consumes MIDI sequence files, such as a music composition tool, a DAW export step, or a music notation converter. MIDI is not interchangeable with audio waveform formats and requires a synthesiser or sample player for sound output.

## Notes
MIDI files encode performance instructions, not audio. Playback quality depends entirely on the synthesiser or soundfont used. Nodes that convert MIDI to audio must specify the target synthesiser and instrument mapping.

title: MKV Video
documentation:
## Purpose
MKV (Matroska Video) is an open-standard multimedia container based on the Extensible Binary Meta Language (EBML). It supports virtually any combination of video, audio, subtitle, and chapter tracks in a single file.

## When to use
Use [MKV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mkv;video) when storing high-quality video with multiple audio tracks, subtitle streams, or chapter markers — common in media archiving, home theater, and distribution workflows. MKV is well-suited for lossless and high-definition content where flexibility matters more than broad legacy compatibility.

## Notes
MKV files are self-describing and resilient to corruption: the container can be partially recovered even when truncated. Because MKV is a wrapper, the actual codec capabilities and quality depend entirely on the enclosed streams. Implementers must inspect inner codec types (H.264, HEVC, AV1, etc.) to determine processing requirements.

title: Mlx Model Path
description: Mlx Model Path file system path
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the local filesystem path to an MLX-format model directory on Apple Silicon. MLX models have a specific directory layout that differs from GGUF or safetensors formats, so a dedicated type allows caps to route MLX-specific paths without ambiguity.

## When to use
Use this type when passing a model path between caps that are specifically designed for the MLX backend, such as an MLX download cap and an MLX inference cap. For backend-agnostic path passing use [Model Path](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-path;textable;record).

## Notes
The path must be non-empty (min_length = 1). The directory at this path must conform to the MLX model layout expected by the target cap. This type is only meaningful on macOS with Apple Silicon; on other platforms the MLX backend is unavailable.

title: Kindle eBook
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Kindle and Mobipocket e-book files used on Amazon Kindle devices and apps. The `.mobi` extension covers the original Mobipocket format; `.azw` and `.azw3` are Amazon's proprietary variants with DRM and enhanced typography features.

## When to use
Use `media:mobi` when a pipeline node delivers or converts Kindle-targeted e-books — for example, a Calibre conversion step producing MOBI from EPUB, or a publishing pipeline that targets Kindle Direct Publishing.

## Notes
The AZW3 (Kindle Format 8) variant supports HTML5 and CSS3 features similar to EPUB 3, while older MOBI/AZW use a more limited markup subset. DRM-protected files cannot be read or converted without authorization keys. For open e-book formats, prefer [EPUB Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:epub). See also [Kindle eBook](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mobi).

title: Model Availability
description: Information about a model's availability both locally and in remote repositories
documentation:
## Purpose
Answers whether a given model is present on the local filesystem and whether it exists in its source repository. This is the lightweight check a cap can perform before deciding whether to download, load, or surface a model to the user.

## When to use
Use this type as the output of an availability-check cap when you need a simple yes/no answer before committing to a full download or load operation. For richer per-file inventory use [Model Contents](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-contents;textable;record); for download progress use [Model Status](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-status;textable;record).

## Notes
`local_available` and `remote_available` are both required. The `local_path` and `size_bytes` fields are optional and populated only when the model is locally present. The `last_modified` timestamp follows ISO 8601 and reflects the local file's modification time.

model_spec.description: The model specification that was checked

local_available.description: Whether the model is available locally

remote_available.description: Whether the model is available in the remote repository

local_path.description: Local filesystem path if available locally

last_modified.description: Last modification time if available locally (ISO 8601)

title: Model Contents
description: Complete repository structure with all files and directories for a model
documentation:
## Purpose
Provides a detailed inventory of all files and directories in a model repository, including sizes, checksums, and local availability. This allows callers to inspect exactly what a model package contains before or after downloading.

## When to use
Use this type when a user or orchestrator needs to understand which files make up a model, verify checksums, or determine which files have already been downloaded. It is the output of a model-contents inspection cap.

## Notes
The `all_files` array is a flat listing of every entry in the repository. The optional `root` object provides the same information as a hierarchy for UIs that need a tree view. The `metadata` object contains repository-level information such as tags or licence identifiers if the repository exposes them.

total_size_bytes.description: Total size of all files in bytes

file_count.description: Number of files in the repository

directory_count.description: Number of directories in the repository

all_files.description: Flat list of all files in the repository

size_bytes.description: File size in bytes (null for directories)

local_available.description: Whether this file is locally available

title: Model Dimensionality
description: Dimensionality of a machine learning model (e.g., embedding vector size)
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a single integer that describes the output dimensionality of a model, most commonly the size of its embedding vectors. This allows caps to advertise or negotiate the dimension of their output without needing a full model-info record.

## When to use
Use this type as a lightweight parameter or output when only the vector dimension is needed — for example, to size a vector database index or validate that an embedding model's output matches a downstream consumer's expected dimension.

## Notes
The minimum value is 1. Typical embedding models produce dimensions between 128 and 4096. The value should match the `embedding_dim` field in [LLM Model Info](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-model-info;json;record) or the `embedding_dim` field in [Embedding Vector](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-vector;textable;record) for the same model.

title: Model List
description: List of available models with their metadata and status
documentation:
## Purpose
Returns an enumeration of models known to the system, including their availability state, local paths, and repository metadata. This enables model management UIs and orchestrators to present users with a curated set of options.

## When to use
Use this type as the output of any cap that lists models, whether filtered by type, availability, or other criteria. Consumers that need fine-grained per-model information should request individual [Model Availability](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-availability;textable;record) or [Model Status](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-status;textable;record) records.

## Notes
The `count` field should equal the length of the `models` array. The optional `filter` field records the query pattern used to produce the list, enabling consumers to display or log the filter applied. Each entry's `local_available` and `remote_available` flags indicate whether the model can be used immediately or requires a download.

local_available.description: Whether the model is available locally

remote_available.description: Whether the model is available remotely

count.description: Total number of models in the list

title: Model Path
description: Local filesystem path to a model directory
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the resolved local path to a model directory after a model has been downloaded or located on disk. It pairs the filesystem path with the originating model spec and an existence flag so consumers know whether the model is actually present.

## When to use
Pass this type between caps that download a model and caps that load or inspect it. A download cap produces a [Model Download Result](https://capdag.com/browse/media:download-result;textable;record); a path-only reference uses this type when no additional download metadata is needed.

## Notes
The `path` field is required. The `exists` flag allows callers to distinguish between a path that was computed but not yet downloaded versus one that is on disk and ready to use. Use [Model Availability](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-availability;textable;record) when you need both local and remote availability in one response.

path.description: Local filesystem path to the model directory

exists.description: Whether the path exists on the filesystem

title: Model Repository
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies which external model repository a model spec resolves against. Currently the only supported value is `huggingface`, which causes the system to resolve model specifications against the HuggingFace Hub API and download infrastructure.

## When to use
Use this type as a parameter when a cap needs to know which registry to query. Most model specs encode the repository in their prefix (e.g. `hf:` for HuggingFace), so this type is primarily useful when the repository must be specified separately from the spec string.

## Notes
The allowed value is `huggingface`. Additional repository types may be added in future; implementations should reject unknown values rather than silently defaulting. See [Model Spec](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-spec;textable) for the combined spec format.

title: Model Spec
description: Model specification (HuggingFace repo or local path). Backend-agnostic.
documentation:
## Purpose
A model spec is a compact string that uniquely identifies a machine learning model and tells the system where to find it. The format is `<scheme>:<identifier>` with an optional query string for filtering (e.g. `hf:org/model-name?include=*Q8_0*`). The scheme determines which backend or repository resolves the spec.

## When to use
Use this type wherever a cap accepts a model identifier as input. Prefer the more specific tagged variants (e.g. `media:model-spec;gguf;textable;llm`) when the backend or task is known, so the routing system can match the most capable cap without ambiguity.

## Notes
The `pattern = ".*:.*"` validation requires the scheme separator colon to be present. Strings of up to 512 characters are allowed to accommodate long query strings. Local paths should use the `local:` prefix to distinguish them from repository references.

title: LLM Model Spec
description: Backend-agnostic LLM model specification. The model spec string determines which backend handles it.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies an LLM model without committing to a specific inference backend. The runtime inspects the model files and routes the request to the appropriate backend (GGUF, MLX, Candle, etc.) based on what is available.

## When to use
Use this variant when the cap or workflow should remain backend-agnostic and let the runtime select the best available backend for the current hardware. Use a backend-specific variant such as `media:model-spec;gguf;textable;llm` when you need to target a particular runtime.

## Notes
The default points to a Mistral-7B GGUF repository with filters for Q8_0 quantization. Operators should override this default to match the models they have provisioned.

title: Mistral Family LLM Model Spec
description: Model specification for Mistral-family instruct models. Uses [INST]/[/INST] chat template.
documentation:
## Purpose
Tags a model spec as belonging to the Mistral model family, allowing the runtime to apply the correct `[INST]/[/INST]` chat template and any Mistral-specific inference optimisations automatically.

## When to use
This tag is added by the ValueAdapter when model content inspection identifies a Mistral-family model. Do not set it manually unless you are certain the model uses the Mistral chat template.

## Notes
The tag is backend-agnostic. Mistral-family GGUF models will carry `media:gguf;llm;mistral;model-spec;textable` after full resolution.

title: Llama Family LLM Model Spec
description: Model specification for Llama-family instruct models. Uses header_id chat template.
documentation:
## Purpose
Tags a model spec as belonging to the Llama model family, enabling the runtime to apply the `<|header_id|>` / `<|eot_id|>` chat template used by Llama 3 and compatible models.

## When to use
This tag is added automatically during content inspection. Use it in cap selectors when you want to explicitly target Llama-family instruct models regardless of backend.

## Notes
Llama-family GGUF models resolve to `media:gguf;llama;llm;model-spec;textable` after full resolution. Llama 2 and Llama 3 use different template syntax; the runtime should detect the exact variant from the model's embedded chat template.

title: Phi Family LLM Model Spec
description: Model specification for Phi-family instruct models. Uses user/end chat template.
documentation:
## Purpose
Tags a model spec as belonging to Microsoft's Phi model family, enabling the runtime to apply the `<|user|>` / `<|end|>` chat template used by Phi-3 and related models.

## When to use
This tag is assigned during content inspection when a Phi-family model is detected. Use it in cap selectors when you need to guarantee that only Phi-compatible models are accepted.

## Notes
Phi models are typically compact (1B–7B parameters) and optimised for reasoning tasks. The chat template differs significantly from Llama and Mistral templates; mixing up the template will degrade output quality.

title: Qwen Family LLM Model Spec
description: Model specification for Qwen-family instruct models. Uses ChatML im_start/im_end template.
documentation:
## Purpose
Tags a model spec as belonging to Alibaba's Qwen model family, enabling the runtime to apply the ChatML `<|im_start|>` / `<|im_end|>` template used by Qwen2 and related models.

## When to use
This tag is assigned during content inspection when a Qwen-family model is detected. The Qwen family also covers multimodal variants (Qwen-VL); use the vision-specific spec types for those.

## Notes
Qwen models use the same ChatML template as some other model families. The runtime should verify the exact template from the model's tokenizer_config.json to avoid collisions.

title: GGUF Vision Model Spec
description: GGUF vision model specification for image description.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a GGUF-format vision-language model used for image captioning and visual question answering via the llama.cpp GGUF backend. The model accepts both text and image inputs and produces text output.

## When to use
Use this spec type when wiring an image description cap that targets the GGUF backend. The default points to moondream2, a compact open vision model. Swap the default for any GGUF-compatible vision model (e.g., LLaVA variants).

## Notes
Vision GGUF models typically require a separate image projection weights file (`.mmproj`) alongside the main `.gguf` file. Ensure the model repository contains both files before running inference.

title: GGUF LLM Model Spec
description: GGUF language model specification for text generation and inference.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a GGUF-format language model for text generation via the llama.cpp backend. This is the primary spec type for LLM inference on CPU or hybrid CPU/GPU hardware.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap explicitly targets the GGUF runtime. The default points to Mistral-7B-Instruct Q8_0, a well-tested general-purpose model. For Apple Silicon environments prefer `media:model-spec;mlx;textable;llm`.

## Notes
The query string parameters `include` and `exclude` filter which files to download from multi-file GGUF repositories. Quantization suffixes (Q4_K_M, Q8_0, etc.) in the `include` pattern select the quantization level.

title: GGUF Embeddings Model Spec
description: GGUF model specification for embedding vector generation.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a GGUF-format embedding model used to produce dense vector representations of text via the llama.cpp backend. These models are optimised for similarity search and retrieval rather than text generation.

## When to use
