Use this type when wiring an embedding cap that targets the GGUF backend. The default points to nomic-embed-text-v1.5, a high-quality open embedding model. Pair with [Embedding Chunk Size](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-chunk-size;search;limit;operator;task;textable;numeric) and [Embedding Chunk Overlap](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-chunk-overlap;search;limit;operator;task;textable;numeric) parameters.

## Notes
Embedding GGUF models are loaded differently from LLM models and should not be used for text generation. The model's output dimension is reported in [LLM Model Info](https://capdag.com/browse/media:llm-model-info;json;record) and can be confirmed via [Model Dimensionality](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-dim;integer;textable;numeric).

title: GGUF Mistral LLM Model Spec
description: GGUF model specification for Mistral-family language models.
documentation:
## Purpose
Combines the GGUF backend tag with the Mistral family tag, fully resolving the model to a GGUF Mistral instruct model. The runtime uses this to apply both the correct file loading path and the `[INST]/[/INST]` chat template.

## When to use
This URN is assigned by the ValueAdapter after inspecting a GGUF model file and confirming it is a Mistral-family model. Use it in cap selectors to match only GGUF Mistral models.

## Notes
This is a composed tag produced at runtime; it is not typically specified manually by users.

title: GGUF Llama LLM Model Spec
description: GGUF model specification for Llama-family language models.
documentation:
## Purpose
Combines the GGUF backend tag with the Llama family tag, fully resolving the model to a GGUF Llama instruct model. The runtime uses this to apply both the correct file loading path and the Llama header_id chat template.

## When to use
This URN is assigned by the ValueAdapter after inspecting a GGUF model file and confirming it is a Llama-family model. Use it in cap selectors to match only GGUF Llama models.

## Notes
This is a composed tag produced at runtime; it is not typically specified manually by users.

title: GGUF Phi LLM Model Spec
description: GGUF model specification for Phi-family language models.
documentation:
## Purpose
Combines the GGUF backend tag with the Phi family tag, fully resolving the model to a GGUF Phi instruct model. The runtime applies both the correct GGUF loading path and the `<|user|>` / `<|end|>` chat template.

## When to use
This URN is assigned by the ValueAdapter after inspecting a GGUF model file and confirming it is a Phi-family model. Use it in cap selectors to match only GGUF Phi models.

## Notes
This is a composed tag produced at runtime; it is not typically specified manually by users.

title: GGUF Qwen LLM Model Spec
description: GGUF model specification for Qwen-family language models.
documentation:
## Purpose
Combines the GGUF backend tag with the Qwen family tag, fully resolving the model to a GGUF Qwen instruct model. The runtime applies both the correct GGUF loading path and the ChatML `<|im_start|>` / `<|im_end|>` template.

## When to use
This URN is assigned by the ValueAdapter after inspecting a GGUF model file and confirming it is a Qwen-family model. Use it in cap selectors to match only GGUF Qwen models.

## Notes
This is a composed tag produced at runtime; it is not typically specified manually by users.

title: MLX Vision Model Spec
description: MLX vision model specification for image description on Apple Silicon.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies an MLX-format vision-language model for image captioning and visual question answering on Apple Silicon. The MLX runtime exploits the unified memory architecture of Apple Silicon for fast, energy-efficient multimodal inference.

## When to use
Use this spec type when targeting the MLX backend for vision tasks on macOS. The default points to a 4-bit quantized Qwen3-VL model from the mlx-community organisation on HuggingFace. For GGUF-based vision inference use `media:model-spec;gguf;textable;vision`.

## Notes
MLX vision models require the mlx-vlm package in addition to the base mlx library. Ensure the target machine is Apple Silicon (M1 or later); MLX does not run on Intel Macs or non-Apple platforms.

title: MLX LLM Model Spec
description: MLX language model specification for text generation on Apple Silicon.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies an MLX-format language model for text generation on Apple Silicon. MLX models run natively in Apple's array framework, offering fast inference on M-series chips without requiring llama.cpp or PyTorch.

## When to use
Prefer this spec type over the GGUF variant on Apple Silicon machines where MLX is available. The default points to a 4-bit quantized Llama 3.2-3B Instruct model from mlx-community. For cross-platform compatibility use `media:model-spec;textable;llm`.

## Notes
MLX LLM models are downloaded from HuggingFace repositories maintained by the mlx-community organisation. They store weights as NumPy arrays in safetensors format alongside a `config.json`. The quantization is baked into the weights rather than applied at load time.

title: MLX Embeddings Model Spec
description: MLX model specification for embedding vector generation on Apple Silicon.
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies an MLX-format embedding model for producing dense text representations on Apple Silicon. This enables fast, energy-efficient embedding generation without the overhead of a GGUF runtime.

## When to use
Use this type when wiring an embedding cap that targets the MLX backend on Apple Silicon. The default points to a 4-bit MiniLM-L6-v2 model from mlx-community, suitable for general-purpose text similarity tasks.

## Notes
MLX embedding models follow the same directory layout as MLX LLM models. Pair with [Embedding Chunk Size](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-chunk-size;search;limit;operator;task;textable;numeric) and [Embedding Chunk Overlap](https://capdag.com/browse/media:embedding-chunk-overlap;search;limit;operator;task;textable;numeric) parameters to control chunking.

title: Candle LLM Model Spec
description: Candle language model specification for text generation (Mistral/Llama safetensors).
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a safetensors-format language model for text generation via the Candle backend. Candle is a lightweight Rust-based ML framework that runs models in safetensors format directly on CPU, Metal, or CUDA.

## When to use
Use this spec type when targeting the Candle runtime specifically. The default points to Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 in safetensors format. For Apple Silicon deployments prefer the MLX backend; for quantized CPU inference prefer the GGUF backend.

## Notes
Candle LLM models are loaded from HuggingFace repositories containing `model.safetensors` or sharded safetensors files. The backend also requires `config.json` and `tokenizer.json` to be present in the repository.

title: Candle Vision Model Spec
description: Candle vision model specification for image description (BLIP).
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a vision-language model for image captioning via the Candle backend. The default points to Salesforce BLIP, a well-established image captioning model available in safetensors format on HuggingFace.

## When to use
Use this spec type when wiring an image description cap that targets the Candle runtime. For more capable multimodal models on Apple Silicon, consider `media:model-spec;mlx;textable;vision`.

## Notes
BLIP requires separate vision encoder and text decoder weights. The Candle backend handles the multi-component loading internally given the HuggingFace repository path.

title: Candle Embeddings Model Spec
description: Candle model specification for embedding vector generation (BERT, CLIP).
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a safetensors-format text embedding model for use with the Candle backend. Supports BERT-family and CLIP text encoder models for producing dense vector representations.

## When to use
Use this type when wiring a text embedding cap that uses the Candle runtime. The default points to all-MiniLM-L6-v2, a compact and widely used sentence embedding model.

## Notes
BERT-family embedding models in safetensors format require `config.json`, `tokenizer.json`, and `model.safetensors` to be present in the repository. For image embeddings use `media:model-spec;candle;image-embeddings;textable`.

title: Candle Image Embeddings Model Spec
description: Candle model specification for image embedding generation (CLIP).
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a CLIP model for producing dense vector representations of images via the Candle backend. Image embeddings can be compared with text embeddings in the same CLIP vector space for cross-modal retrieval.

## When to use
Use this type when wiring an image embedding cap that targets the Candle runtime. The default points to OpenAI's CLIP ViT-B/32 model. For text-only embeddings use `media:model-spec;candle;textable;embeddings`.

## Notes
The include query filter (`*.json,*.safetensors,pytorch_model.bin`) selects only the files needed for inference, skipping large optional files such as optimizer states. CLIP repositories often contain both safetensors and PyTorch variants; the filter ensures the lighter safetensors files are preferred.

title: Candle Transcription Model Spec
description: Candle model specification for audio transcription (Whisper).
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies an OpenAI Whisper model for audio-to-text transcription via the Candle backend. Whisper is an encoder-decoder model that accepts mel spectrograms of audio and produces text transcriptions.

## When to use
Use this type when wiring an audio transcription cap that targets the Candle runtime. The default points to Whisper Base, a compact model suitable for real-time transcription. Larger variants (Small, Medium, Large) offer higher accuracy at greater compute cost.

## Notes
Whisper models require audio preprocessing (resampling to 16 kHz, mel spectrogram extraction) before the model is invoked. This preprocessing is typically handled by the cap implementation, not the model spec itself.

title: Model Status
description: Detailed model status including state, download progress, and health information
documentation:
## Purpose
Reports the lifecycle state of a model including whether it is not yet downloaded, actively downloading, paused, ready for use, or in an error state. When a download is in progress it also carries progress metrics such as bytes transferred and estimated time remaining.

## When to use
Poll or stream this type from a model management cap to track download or loading progress. UIs and orchestrators can use the `state` field to drive state machines and display progress bars, and inspect `error_message` on failure.

## Notes
The `state` field is required. Progress fields (`downloaded_bytes`, `total_bytes`, `progress`, `speed_bps`, `eta_seconds`) are only meaningful when `state` is `downloading`. The `local_path` field is populated once the model reaches the `ready` state. See [Model Availability](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-availability;textable;record) for a simpler availability snapshot.

progress.description: Download progress as a percentage (0.0 to 1.0)

speed_bps.description: Download speed in bytes per second

finished_at.description: When the download/operation completed or failed (ISO 8601)

local_path.description: Local filesystem path if model is available locally

description: Maximum log level for file logging

title: Create unique log file per run
description: Create unique log file per run

description: Maximum log file size in MB before rotation

title: QuickTime Video
documentation:
## Purpose
MOV is Apple's QuickTime container format, which organizes video, audio, and metadata into a track-based structure. It is the native output format of many Apple devices, Final Cut Pro, and other professional macOS production tools.

## When to use
Use [QuickTime Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mov;video) when working with content originating from Apple cameras (iPhone, iPad), macOS screen recordings, or professional editing pipelines that target Final Cut Pro or Motion. MOV files often carry high-quality ProRes or H.264 video and are a natural intermediate format in Apple-centric workflows.

## Notes
MOV and MP4 share the ISO Base Media File Format heritage and are structurally similar, but MOV supports certain Apple-specific metadata atoms not found in standard MP4. On non-Apple platforms, playback and remuxing support varies by tool; most modern FFmpeg-based pipelines handle MOV without issue.

title: MP3 Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an MP3-encoded audio file, the most widely supported compressed audio format. MP3 uses lossy compression to achieve small file sizes while maintaining perceptually acceptable audio quality.

## When to use
Use `media:mp3;audio` when a node produces or consumes MP3 audio — for example a music file, a podcast episode, or a compressed voice recording. For lossless audio, see [FLAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:flac;audio). For speech intended for transcription, see [Speech Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:audio;speech;wav).

## Notes
MP3 quality is determined by the bitrate (typically 128–320 kbps). Re-encoding MP3 to MP3 accumulates quality loss; pipeline steps that modify audio should decode to PCM first and re-encode only at the final output stage.

title: MP4 Video
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an MP4 video file, the dominant container format for digital video. MP4 typically carries H.264 or H.265 video alongside AAC audio, and is used for everything from internet streaming to camera recordings and screen captures.

## When to use
Use `media:mp4;video` when a node produces or consumes MP4 video files, such as a screen recorder output, a video transcoder, or a video analysis node. For audio-only MP4 content, see [M4A Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:m4a;audio).

## Notes
MP4 is a container; the actual video and audio codecs vary. H.264 (AVC) and H.265 (HEVC) are the most common video codecs. Nodes that process video content should inspect the codec before decoding. MP4 files may also contain subtitles, chapters, and metadata streams alongside the primary video track.

title: MPEG Video
documentation:
## Purpose
MPEG refers to the family of video standards defined by the Moving Picture Experts Group, including MPEG-1 and MPEG-2 program streams. These formats were foundational for VCD, DVD, digital broadcast, and early internet video distribution.

## When to use
Use [MPEG Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mpeg;video) when ingesting legacy video content from DVD rips, broadcast capture, or archival sources that use `.mpeg` or `.mpg` files. For new production or distribution, prefer [MP4](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video) or [MKV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mkv;video) which carry more capable codecs.

## Notes
The `.mpeg` and `.mpg` extensions are ambiguous — they may contain MPEG-1 (up to 352x240 at 1.5 Mbit/s) or MPEG-2 video (DVD-quality up to 10 Mbit/s). Implementers should probe the bitstream to determine the exact stream type. MPEG-2 transport streams are handled separately under [MPEG Transport Stream](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ts;video).

title: MessagePack
documentation:
## Purpose
MessagePack is a compact binary serialization format that encodes the same data model as JSON but in fewer bytes. The [MessagePack](https://capdag.com/browse/media:msgpack) media type represents files or byte streams containing a single top-level MessagePack value (which may be a map, array, or scalar).

## When to use
Use this type when a node exchanges structured data with tight size or throughput constraints and JSON's verbosity is prohibitive — for example, IoT sensor payloads, inter-service RPC messages, or cache entries. MessagePack is a good alternative to CBOR when ecosystem libraries in the target language are more mature.

## Notes
MessagePack does not embed a schema; both producer and consumer must agree on the structure out of band. The format supports extension types for custom data (timestamps, binary blobs). Implementers should use a language-native library such as `msgpack` (Python), `msgpack-rust`, or `msgpack-java`. When storing multiple messages in a single file, prefix each with its byte length or use a newline-delimited framing convention.

title: NDJSON Data
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a file where each line is a complete, self-contained JSON value. NDJSON (also known as JSON Lines) is commonly used for streaming data, log exports, and large datasets that cannot be loaded into memory as a single JSON array.

## When to use
Use `media:ndjson;textable` when a node produces or consumes a stream of JSON records, one per line, such as event logs, batch inference outputs, or database export streams. For a JSON array in a single document, use [JSON Array](https://capdag.com/browse/media:json;textable;list).

## Notes
Each line must be valid JSON on its own; blank lines and trailing newlines should be ignored by consumers. NDJSON is well-suited for incremental processing because records can be parsed without loading the entire file.

title: Nim Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Nim source files, including both regular modules (`.nim`) and NimScript configuration files (`.nims`). Nim compiles to C, C++, or JavaScript and emphasizes performance and expressiveness.

## When to use
Use `media:nim;textable;code` when a pipeline step consumes or produces Nim source — for example, a transpilation node targeting C output or a static analysis tool operating on Nim modules.

## Notes
NimScript (`.nims`) files share the same syntax as Nim but run in a sandboxed interpreter used for build configuration. Both extensions are covered by this media type. See also [Nim Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:nim;textable;code).

title: Apple Numbers
documentation:
## Purpose
Apple Numbers is a spreadsheet application, and its `.numbers` format is a ZIP-based package containing SNAPPY-compressed Protobuf data alongside image and other resources. The [Apple Numbers](https://capdag.com/browse/media:numbers) media type represents any `.numbers` file produced by Numbers for macOS or iOS.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes spreadsheets originating from the Apple Numbers application — for example, data ingestion from macOS users, document archiving, or conversion pipelines that transform Numbers files into open formats. For cross-platform spreadsheet interchange, XLSX or CSV is preferred.

## Notes
The Numbers format is proprietary; there is no publicly documented specification. Implementers that need to extract data should use the `numbers-parser` Python library or convert via Numbers itself (File > Export). The internal structure changed significantly between Numbers 3 and later versions. The format does not include a `content_type` header in the file itself, so format detection must rely on the ZIP magic bytes and the presence of characteristic internal paths.

title: NumPy Array
documentation:
## Purpose
NumPy's native array format stores N-dimensional arrays with a small header describing dtype, shape, and byte order followed by raw array data. The [NumPy Array](https://capdag.com/browse/media:numpy) media type covers both `.npy` (single array) and `.npz` (zip archive of multiple named arrays) files.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes numerical arrays intended for Python-based downstream processing — for example, intermediate feature matrices, model inputs/outputs, or pre-computed embeddings. Prefer HDF5 for hierarchical or very large datasets; use NumPy format when simplicity and direct `np.load` interoperability matter most.

## Notes
`.npy` files begin with a magic string `\x93NUMPY` followed by version bytes and a Python-literal header dict. `.npz` archives are standard ZIP files and can be read without NumPy using any ZIP library. Implementers should use `numpy.save` / `numpy.load` for single arrays and `numpy.savez` / `numpy.savez_compressed` for multi-array bundles.

title: OBJ Model
documentation:
## Purpose
Wavefront OBJ is a plain-text format for describing 3D geometry — vertices, normals, texture coordinates, and faces — originally developed by Wavefront Technologies. The [OBJ Model](https://capdag.com/browse/media:obj;model) media type represents any `.obj` file following this convention.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes simple 3D mesh geometry in a widely-supported, human-readable format — for example, 3D printing preparation, mesh inspection, or interchange with geometry-processing tools. OBJ does not support animations or complex scene graphs; use [FBX](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fbx;model) or [GLB](https://capdag.com/browse/media:glb;model) when those features are needed.

## Notes
OBJ files are UTF-8 (or ASCII) text; material properties are referenced via a companion `.mtl` file. The `;model` tag indicates this is a 3D model artifact. Implementers should be aware that face indices in OBJ are 1-based, not 0-based. When processing large meshes, streaming line-by-line parsing is more memory efficient than loading the entire file at once.

title: Objective-C Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Objective-C source files contain implementation code in Objective-C, a dynamically dispatched superset of C that was Apple's primary application programming language before Swift. It remains in use in legacy Apple platform codebases and frameworks.

## When to use
Use [Objective-C Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:objc;textable;code) for `.m` implementation files. Header files are covered by [C Header](https://capdag.com/browse/media:c-header;textable;code) since Objective-C headers use the `.h` extension. Files that mix Objective-C and C++ use the `.mm` extension and are handled by [Objective-C++ Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:objcpp;textable;code).

## Notes
Objective-C uses a Smalltalk-inspired message-passing syntax (`[receiver message]`) alongside standard C syntax. It supports both manual reference counting (MRC) and Automatic Reference Counting (ARC); the memory management model affects how code must be analyzed. Most modern Apple development uses [Swift Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:swift;textable;code), but large Objective-C codebases remain common in mature iOS and macOS applications.

title: Objective-C++ Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Objective-C++ source files contain code that blends Objective-C and C++ in a single translation unit. This combination is common in Apple platform projects that need to interface high-level Objective-C frameworks with C++ libraries or game engines.

## When to use
Use [Objective-C++ Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:objcpp;textable;code) for `.mm` files. These files can freely intermix Objective-C classes, C++ classes, and plain C, making them the primary bridging mechanism between Apple's Cocoa/UIKit APIs and C++ codebases such as rendering engines or audio DSP libraries.

## Notes
Objective-C++ imposes restrictions on how Objective-C and C++ interact: Objective-C objects cannot inherit from C++ classes and vice versa, and C++ exceptions and Objective-C exceptions have distinct propagation rules. Compilers treat `.mm` files as Objective-C++ automatically; the file extension is the primary indicator of this mixed mode.

title: OCaml Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries OCaml source files, including both implementation files (`.ml`) and interface files (`.mli`). OCaml combines functional, imperative, and object-oriented styles with a strong static type system and efficient native-code compilation.

## When to use
Use `media:ocaml;textable;code` when a pipeline step processes OCaml source — for example, a build node invoking `dune` or a type-checking step that separately processes interface files.

## Notes
Interface files (`.mli`) define module signatures and are compiled independently from their implementation counterparts. Both extensions are covered by this single media type. See also [OCaml Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ocaml;textable;code).

title: OpenDocument Presentation
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries OpenDocument Presentation files (`.odp`), the ISO-standardized slide format used by LibreOffice Impress and Apache OpenOffice Impress. ODP is a ZIP archive of XML content following the ODF specification.

## When to use
Use `media:odp` when a pipeline node processes open-format presentations — for example, a slide rendering step via LibreOffice, a content extraction step, or a conversion step targeting PDF or PPTX output. For Microsoft PowerPoint format, see [PowerPoint Presentation](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pptx).

## Notes
ODP follows the OASIS OpenDocument Format standard (ISO/IEC 26300). LibreOffice Impress provides the most complete support, including complex animations and transitions. Conversion to PDF for archival or distribution is a common pipeline operation. See also [OpenDocument Presentation](https://capdag.com/browse/media:odp).

title: OpenDocument Spreadsheet
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries OpenDocument Spreadsheet files (`.ods`), the ISO-standardized spreadsheet format used by LibreOffice Calc, Apache OpenOffice Calc, and Google Sheets. ODS is a ZIP archive of XML content following the ODF specification.

## When to use
Use `media:ods` when a pipeline node processes open-format spreadsheets — for example, a data extraction step using `odfpy` or LibreOffice, or a step that produces tabular reports in an open standard format. For Microsoft Excel format, see [Excel Spreadsheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xlsx).

## Notes
ODS supports formulas, charts, and multiple sheets in the same manner as XLSX. The `odfpy` Python library provides programmatic access. LibreOffice can convert ODS to CSV, XLSX, or PDF. See also [OpenDocument Spreadsheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ods).

title: OpenDocument Text
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries OpenDocument Text files (`.odt`), the ISO-standardized word processing format used by LibreOffice Writer, Apache OpenOffice, and Google Docs. ODT is a ZIP archive of XML content and assets following the ODF specification.

## When to use
Use `media:odt` when a pipeline node processes open-format word documents — for example, a text extraction step, a cross-format conversion step via LibreOffice, or a template rendering step generating reports in an open standard format. For Microsoft Word format, see [Word Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:docx).

## Notes
ODT is defined by the OASIS OpenDocument Format standard (ISO/IEC 26300). Content is stored in `content.xml` within the archive. LibreOffice provides the most complete support for ODT, including complex styles and embedded objects. See also [OpenDocument Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:odt).

title: OGG Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an Ogg Vorbis audio file, an open and patent-free compressed audio format comparable in quality to AAC and MP3. Ogg Vorbis is commonly used in open-source games, web audio, and applications that require royalty-free audio compression.

## When to use
Use `media:ogg;audio` when a node produces or consumes Ogg Vorbis audio. For the modern Opus codec in an Ogg container, see [Opus Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:opus;audio). For MP3 or AAC equivalents, see [MP3 Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mp3;audio) or [AAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aac;audio).

## Notes
The Ogg container can carry multiple codec streams (Vorbis, Opus, Theora). This spec refers specifically to Ogg Vorbis audio. Consumers should confirm the codec before processing if the source may contain Opus or other streams.

title: OGG Video
documentation:
## Purpose
OGV is the video-carrying variant of the Ogg container format, developed by Xiph.Org. It typically holds Theora video with Vorbis audio, and was an early royalty-free web video option before VP8/VP9 became available.

## When to use
Use [OGG Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ogv;video) when working with legacy open-source video content or when content was originally encoded in the Theora/Vorbis combination. For royalty-free web video today, [WebM Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webm;video) is the preferred alternative.

## Notes
Theora video quality at equivalent bitrates is generally lower than VP9 or H.264. The Ogg container is also used for audio-only content (`.ogg`, `.oga`) and should not be confused with OGV. Browser support for OGV has declined as WebM has superseded it in most HTML5 video scenarios.

title: ONNX Model
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a model in the Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX) format, a cross-platform intermediate representation for machine learning models. ONNX models can be run by any compatible runtime including ONNX Runtime, TensorRT, and others.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap produces or consumes an ONNX model file. ONNX is particularly common for optimised encoder models used in embedding generation and for models exported from PyTorch or TensorFlow for deployment in production runtimes.

## Notes
Large models may be split across multiple `.onnx` files with external data files. Caps that load ONNX models should document whether they accept single-file or multi-file models. The ONNX format does not impose a chat template convention; these details are handled by the surrounding cap logic.

title: Opus Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an Opus-encoded audio file, an open and patent-free codec designed for low-latency speech and music transmission over networks. Opus delivers excellent quality across a wide range of bitrates and is the recommended codec for WebRTC and internet audio streaming.

## When to use
Use `media:opus;audio` when a node produces or consumes Opus audio, such as voice communication recordings, WebRTC captures, or streaming audio pipeline outputs. For general compressed audio, see [OGG Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ogg;audio) or [AAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aac;audio).

## Notes
Opus files are typically wrapped in an Ogg container (`.opus` extension). The codec is optimised for low latency and handles both speech and music well. Whisper and similar transcription models can accept Opus audio directly in many implementations.

title: Org-mode Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Emacs Org-mode files, which combine plain-text markup, structured outlines, TODO task management, literate programming (Babel code blocks), and tabular data in a single format. Org-mode is primarily used within Emacs but can be exported and processed by external tools.

## When to use
Use `media:org;textable` when a pipeline node processes `.org` files — for example, a Pandoc conversion step targeting HTML or Markdown output, a task extraction step reading TODO entries, or a literate-programming executor running embedded code blocks.

## Notes
Org-mode's export and Babel subsystems require Emacs for full fidelity. Standalone parsers (e.g., `org-ruby`, `orgparse`) support a subset of the format. Org files can embed code blocks in any language, making them a general-purpose literate programming format. See also [Org-mode Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:org;textable).

title: OpenType Font
documentation:
## Purpose
OpenType is a font format built on TrueType that adds support for CFF/CFF2 cubic Bezier outlines, advanced typographic features (ligatures, kerning, contextual alternates), and extended Unicode character coverage. The [OpenType Font](https://capdag.com/browse/media:otf;font) media type represents any `.otf` binary font file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes professional-grade desktop fonts with advanced OpenType layout features — for example, text rendering pipelines, publishing tools, or font packaging workflows. OpenType is the preferred format for high-quality typography; for web delivery use [WOFF2](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff2;font).

## Notes
OpenType files share the `sfnt` table structure with TrueType but may use CFF outlines in the `CFF ` or `CFF2` table instead of `glyf`. Implementers can distinguish CFF from TrueType outlines by checking which table is present. FontTools (`fonttools` Python package) and HarfBuzz are the standard open-source libraries for OpenType manipulation. The `;font` tag indicates this is a font artifact.

title: Max Depth
documentation:
## Purpose
Limits how many levels deep a machine traverses when extracting or generating a document outline or hierarchical structure.

## When to use
Set this parameter on outline extraction, table-of-contents generation, or tree-traversal machines to control output granularity. A depth of 1 returns only top-level headings; higher values include nested subsections.

## Notes
Valid range: 1–10. A depth of 1 is the shallowest (top-level only). Deep outlines may be large; balance depth against the token budget of downstream machines. See [Inspection Depth](https://capdag.com/browse/media:inspection-depth;textable) for a qualitative depth control.

title: Output Format
documentation:
## Purpose
Instructs a machine to produce its results in a specific format (e.g., `json`, `markdown`, `plain`, `csv`). The accepted values depend on the target machine's implementation.

## When to use
Attach this parameter when a machine supports multiple output formats and the downstream consumer requires a specific one. If a machine has a fixed output format, this parameter is unnecessary.

## Notes
This type carries a free-text value; consult each machine's documentation for its supported format identifiers. See [Output Length](https://capdag.com/browse/media:output-length;textable) for controlling verbosity rather than structure.

title: Output Length
documentation:
## Purpose
A qualitative or quantitative hint to a machine about the desired verbosity of its output. Common values include `short`, `medium`, `long`, or a specific token count.

## When to use
Use this parameter when a machine supports multiple verbosity levels and the consumer needs to control response length semantically rather than through a raw token limit. For hard token limits, use [Max Tokens](https://capdag.com/browse/media:max-tokens;inference;limit;user;task;textable;numeric) instead.

## Notes
Accepted values are machine-specific. See [Output Format](https://capdag.com/browse/media:output-format;textable) for controlling the structure of output, and [Max Tokens](https://capdag.com/browse/media:max-tokens;inference;limit;user;task;textable;numeric) for enforcing a hard generation limit.

title: Page Image Height
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies the pixel height to use when rendering a document page as an image. Controls the vertical resolution of the rasterized output.

## When to use
Set this parameter alongside [Page Image Width](https://capdag.com/browse/media:page-image-width;textable;numeric) when you need to control the full resolution of rendered pages. Increasing height improves vertical detail for tall or portrait-oriented documents.

## Notes
Valid range: 50–4000 pixels. Aspect ratio handling is machine-specific. For most document types, setting only width and allowing the machine to infer height is simpler and avoids distortion.

title: Page Image Width
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies the pixel width to use when rendering a document page as an image. Controls the horizontal resolution of the rasterized output.

## When to use
Set this parameter on page-rendering or PDF-to-image machines when the default resolution is too low for OCR or visual analysis, or too high for bandwidth-constrained pipelines.

## Notes
Valid range: 50–4000 pixels. Pair with [Page Image Height](https://capdag.com/browse/media:page-image-height;textable;numeric) to set both dimensions. Aspect ratio enforcement is machine-specific; some machines may adjust one dimension to preserve proportions.

title: Apple Pages
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the binary content of an Apple Pages document (`.pages` file) for processing by machines that support the Pages format.

## When to use
Use this media type when a pipeline ingests Apple Pages files directly, such as a document conversion or extraction machine. For page-number selection within a document, use [Index Range](https://capdag.com/browse/media:index-range;textable) alongside the appropriate document media type.

## Notes
MIME type is `application/vnd.apple.pages`. Machines that consume this type typically convert it to a more portable format (PDF, plain text, or HTML) as a first step. Not all document processing machines support the Pages format natively.

title: Parquet Data
documentation:
## Purpose
Apache Parquet is an open-source columnar storage format optimized for analytical workloads. The [Parquet Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:parquet;list;record) media type represents files that store an ordered list of typed records in Parquet's binary encoding.

## When to use
Use this type when exchanging large tabular datasets between nodes where column-level compression and predicate pushdown matter — for example, machine-learning feature tables, telemetry exports, or ETL pipeline outputs. Prefer Parquet over CSV or JSON for datasets with millions of rows or where downstream consumers are Spark, DuckDB, Pandas, or similar engines.

## Notes
Parquet files embed their schema in file metadata. Implementers should use the Apache Arrow or PyArrow libraries to read and write files; reading the schema before processing rows is recommended to validate that the expected columns are present. The `;list;record` tags indicate this type carries an ordered collection of structured records.

title: PDF Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Portable Document Format file containing text, images, and layout information. PDF is the canonical format for documents that must preserve visual fidelity across systems.

## When to use
Use `media:pdf` when a pipeline step produces or consumes a full PDF document — for example as the source input to a text extraction or page-rendering node. For individual pages extracted as text, see [Text Page](https://capdag.com/browse/media:textable;page).

## Notes
PDF files may contain selectable text, scanned raster images, or a combination of both. Nodes that extract text should handle the image-only case by invoking an OCR step when no text layer is present.

title: PEM Certificate
documentation:
## Purpose
PEM (Privacy Enhanced Mail) is a Base64-encoded text encoding of DER-format ASN.1 structures, delimited by `-----BEGIN ...-----` / `-----END ...-----` headers. The [PEM Certificate](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pem;textable) media type represents files containing X.509 certificates, private keys, certificate chains, or certificate signing requests in PEM encoding.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes TLS certificates, private keys, or certificate signing requests — for example, TLS provisioning pipelines, CA automation, secret management workflows, or mTLS configuration. PEM is the standard format used by OpenSSL, nginx, Apache, and most ACME clients.

## Notes
A single PEM file may contain multiple concatenated PEM objects (e.g., a leaf certificate followed by intermediate certificates). The `;textable` tag reflects that PEM is ASCII-safe. Implementers should treat PEM files as sensitive artifacts containing secrets; private key files should not be logged or stored without encryption. Use OpenSSL or a cryptography library to parse and validate PEM content.

title: Perl Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Perl source files across all three common roles: executable scripts (`.pl`), reusable modules (`.pm`), and Plain Old Documentation files (`.pod`). Perl is widely used for text processing, system administration, and legacy web applications.

## When to use
Use `media:perl;textable;code` when a pipeline node consumes or produces Perl source — for example, a linting step using `perlcritic`, a module packaging step for CPAN, or a legacy automation script runner.

## Notes
POD files (`.pod`) contain structured documentation embedded in or alongside Perl code and are parsed by tools like `perldoc`. All three extensions share the same media type since they use identical syntax. See also [Perl Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:perl;textable;code).

title: PHP Source
documentation:
## Purpose
PHP source files contain code written in PHP, a widely deployed server-side scripting language designed primarily for web development. PHP is the language underlying WordPress, Drupal, Laravel, and a large portion of the web's server-side infrastructure.

## When to use
Use [PHP Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:php;textable;code) for `.php` files and `.phtml` files (PHP-enhanced HTML templates). PHP files typically contain a mix of PHP code delimited by `<?php ... ?>` tags and HTML, though modern frameworks use pure PHP class files without HTML embedding.

## Notes
PHP 7 and PHP 8 introduced substantial changes including typed properties, named arguments, enums, and fibers; the PHP version must be known to correctly interpret syntax. PHP source is UTF-8 text. The `declare(strict_types=1)` directive at the top of a file enables strict type checking and affects how the file should be analyzed for type correctness.

title: PKCS#12 Certificate
documentation:
## Purpose
PKCS#12 (also known as PFX) is a binary format for bundling a private key, its corresponding certificate, and any intermediate CA certificates into a single password-protected file. The [PKCS#12 Certificate](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pkcs12) media type represents any `.p12` or `.pfx` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node must transport a complete credential bundle — private key plus certificate chain — as a single artifact, for example when provisioning TLS client certificates for mutual authentication, exporting credentials from a CA, or importing into a Java keystore. For situations where only a certificate or key (without the other) is needed, use [PEM](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pem;textable).

## Notes
PKCS#12 files are binary DER-encoded ASN.1 structures; they are not human-readable. The file is typically encrypted with a passphrase that must be provided at load time. Implementers should use OpenSSL (`pkcs12` command) or a cryptography library (e.g., Python `cryptography.hazmat.primitives.serialization.pkcs12`) to read and write PKCS#12 files. Passphrases must be handled securely and never embedded in pipeline configurations as plaintext.

title: macOS Package
documentation:
## Purpose
macOS `.pkg` files are XAR-format archives used by macOS Installer to install software, scripts, and resources onto a macOS system. The [macOS Package](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pkg;archive) media type represents any macOS installer package produced by `pkgbuild` or `productbuild`.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes macOS installer packages — for example, macOS application distribution pipelines, enterprise deployment tools (Jamf, Munki), notarization workflows, or package signing automation.

## Notes
PKG files are XAR archives (the `media_type` is `application/x-xar`). They contain a distribution XML or a flat package with a Bom (bill of materials) and a compressed payload. Implementers can inspect contents using `pkgutil --expand` on macOS or the open-source `xar` tool on other platforms. Apple notarization is required for packages distributed outside the Mac App Store on macOS 10.15+. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: Property List
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Apple Property List files used throughout macOS and iOS for configuration, preferences, and metadata. Plist files can be serialized in XML, binary, or JSON formats; the `application/x-plist` type covers all three variants.

## When to use
Use `media:plist;record` when a pipeline node reads or generates plist data — for example, an Xcode project configuration step, an app bundle inspector, a code-signing step that reads entitlements, or a tool that converts plist to JSON for cross-platform processing.

## Notes
Binary plists can be converted to XML using `plutil -convert xml1` on macOS. XML plists are human-readable but verbose. Common plist consumers include macOS preference domains (`defaults`), `Info.plist` in app bundles, and Xcode project files. See also [Property List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:plist;record).

title: Image Data
documentation:
## Purpose
The base type for raw PNG image bytes without additional semantic context. Covers any PNG-encoded raster image, regardless of its role in a pipeline.

## When to use
Use `media:png` when a node produces or consumes a PNG file and the semantic role (e.g. vision input, rendered page, icon) is not significant for routing. When the image is specifically intended as input to a vision model or image analysis node, prefer [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:image;png).

## Notes
PNG uses lossless compression and supports alpha transparency. It is a good default for screenshots, diagrams, and UI assets. For photographic content where file size is a concern, consider [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image) or [WebP Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webp;image).

title: PowerShell Script
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries PowerShell source files in their three common roles: runnable scripts (`.ps1`), module implementations (`.psm1`), and module manifest declarations (`.psd1`). PowerShell runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux via PowerShell Core.

## When to use
Use `media:powershell;textable;code` when a pipeline node executes or analyzes PowerShell — for example, a CI automation step invoking `.ps1` scripts or a policy analysis tool scanning module manifests.

## Notes
Module manifests (`.psd1`) are data files in PowerShell syntax describing module metadata, dependencies, and exported members. All three extensions share this media type. See also [PowerShell Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:powershell;textable;code).

title: PowerPoint Presentation (Legacy)
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries legacy Microsoft PowerPoint presentations in the binary format (`.ppt`) used by PowerPoint 97 through PowerPoint 2003. This format is encountered in archival collections and older enterprise document stores.

## When to use
Use `media:ppt` when a pipeline node must process old-format presentations — for example, a document conversion step using LibreOffice to render slides as images or PDF. For current presentations, prefer [PowerPoint Presentation](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pptx).

## Notes
The binary `.ppt` format has limited open-source tooling support. LibreOffice provides the most complete conversion path. Text and image extraction from `.ppt` files is less reliable than from PPTX. See also [PowerPoint Presentation (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ppt).

title: PowerPoint Presentation
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Microsoft PowerPoint presentations in the Open XML format (`.pptx`), the current standard for slide-based presentations. PPTX files are ZIP archives containing XML parts for slides, layouts, themes, and embedded media.

## When to use
Use `media:pptx` when a pipeline node processes PowerPoint presentations — for example, a slide extraction step using `python-pptx`, a PDF rendering step via LibreOffice, or a content analysis step that reads speaker notes and text from each slide.

## Notes
PPTX is an ISO/IEC 29500 standard. Individual slide XML files are accessible by treating the archive as a ZIP. For the older binary format, see [PowerPoint Presentation (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ppt). See also [PowerPoint Presentation](https://capdag.com/browse/media:pptx).

title: Precision
documentation:
## Purpose
Specifies the numeric precision used when loading or running a model. Controls the trade-off between computation speed, memory usage, and output quality.

## When to use
Set this parameter when deploying models to hardware with specific memory constraints or when optimizing for inference throughput. `float32` offers maximum accuracy; `int4` offers maximum compression with some quality loss.

## Notes
Allowed values: `float32`, `float16`, `int8`, `int4`. Not all models support all precision levels; check the target model's quantization support. Lower precision reduces VRAM usage and increases throughput but may introduce rounding artifacts in sensitive tasks.

title: Preserve All Data
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that prevents a machine from discarding any input data during transformation, summarization, or extraction. When enabled, all source content is carried through to the output.

## When to use
Enable when data loss is unacceptable, such as in archival workflows, compliance processing, or when a downstream machine requires the full original content alongside derived results.

## Notes
Enabling this flag typically increases output size and may conflict with token-limit or size constraints. See [Preserve Structure Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:preserve-structure-flag;textable) for a related flag that preserves document structure without necessarily retaining all content.

title: Preserve Structure Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that instructs a machine to maintain the hierarchical or organizational structure of the input document in its output, even when content is summarized or extracted.

## When to use
Enable for document conversion, reformatting, or summarization tasks where section hierarchy, list structure, or table layout must be retained. Disable when flat or free-form output is sufficient.

## Notes
See [Preserve All Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:preserve-all-data;textable) when full content fidelity is required in addition to structure. See [Remove Boilerplate](https://capdag.com/browse/media:remove-boilerplate;textable) for the complementary operation of stripping non-essential structural elements.

title: Programming Language
documentation:
## Purpose

Programming Language specifies the target or source language for a code-related operation. It constrains generation, analysis, and formatting to the syntax and idioms of the selected language.

## When to use

Use this media type as an input to code generation, code explanation, or syntax highlighting nodes. Combine it with [Code Context](https://capdag.com/browse/media:code-contextable) and [Coding Style](https://capdag.com/browse/media:coding-style;textable) to give the model a complete picture of the coding environment.

## Notes

The allowed values are a fixed enumeration. If the target language is not listed, use the closest equivalent or leave the field absent and rely on the model's inference from [Code Context](https://capdag.com/browse/media:code-contextable).

title: Prompt
description: Text prompt to guide model generation or analysis.
documentation:
## Purpose
A prompt is the primary text instruction sent to a model to drive generation or analysis. It defines what the model should produce, ask, or evaluate.

## When to use
Supply a prompt whenever a machine requires user-authored or dynamically constructed instruction text as a distinct input port, separate from the system prompt or base prompt. Prompts are typically short-lived and task-specific.

## Notes
See also [System Prompt](https://capdag.com/browse/media:system-prompt;textable) for session-level instructions and [Base Prompt](https://capdag.com/browse/media:base-prompt;inference;policy;user;task;textable) for operator-defined defaults.

title: Java Properties
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a Java `.properties` file, a flat key-value configuration format used throughout the Java ecosystem for application settings, internationalisation strings, and framework configuration.

## When to use
Use `media:properties;textable;record` when a node produces or consumes Java properties files, such as Spring Boot application properties or resource bundle files. For non-Java key-value configurations, see [INI Configuration](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ini;textable;record).

## Notes
Keys and values are separated by `=` or `:`. Lines beginning with `#` or `!` are comments. Values may use Unicode escape sequences (`\\uXXXX`). The encoding is ISO-8859-1 by default, though UTF-8 is common in modern tooling.

title: Protocol Buffers
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Protocol Buffers content across its three common forms: binary-encoded wire format (`.pb`), the generic binary alias (`.protobuf`), and schema definition language source (`.proto`). Protocol Buffers is Google's language-neutral serialization format used extensively in gRPC services and data pipelines.

## When to use
Use `media:protobuf` when a pipeline node serializes, deserializes, or analyzes protobuf content — for example, a gRPC message encoder/decoder, a schema registry step, or a code generation node that runs `protoc` on `.proto` definitions.

## Notes
Binary `.pb` files are not human-readable; use `.proto` schema files for text-based analysis. The `proto3` syntax is the current standard; `proto2` files remain in use in legacy services. Code generation via `protoc` and language-specific plugins produces stubs for Go, Python, Java, and other languages. See also [Protocol Buffers](https://capdag.com/browse/media:protobuf).

title: Photoshop Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an Adobe Photoshop PSD file, a layered image format that preserves editing state including layers, masks, adjustment layers, and blending modes. PSD is the native working format for Photoshop-based design workflows.

## When to use
Use `media:psd;image` when a node must read or produce a full Photoshop document with its layer structure intact, such as a design export step or a compositing tool. When only the flattened image is needed, export to [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image) instead.

## Notes
PSD files can be large due to uncompressed layer data. Parsing requires a PSD-aware library; most general image libraries can only read the flattened composite embedded in the file. The related PSB format extends PSD for files larger than 2 GB.

title: PSV Data
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents tabular data where fields are separated by pipe (`|`) characters. PSV is used in contexts where commas and tabs may appear as valid field content, making them unsuitable as delimiters.

## When to use
Use `media:psv;textable` when a node produces or consumes pipe-delimited tabular data. For comma-delimited data, see [CSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csv;list;record;textable). For tab-delimited data, see [TSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tsv;textable).

## Notes
There is no universal standard for PSV; consumers should treat the pipe as the sole field delimiter and handle quoting conventions based on the producing node's documentation.

title: Python Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Python source files contain code written in the Python programming language, one of the most widely used general-purpose languages for scripting, data science, machine learning, web development, and automation.

## When to use
Use [Python Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:python;textable;code) for any `.py` source file, including application code, scripts, and test files. The `.pyw` extension indicates a Windows GUI script that suppresses the console window; `.pyi` files are stub files used for static type checking and should be treated as Python source.

## Notes
Python source is plain text encoded in UTF-8 (PEP 3120). The language lacks a compiled binary form distributed to end users; it runs via the CPython interpreter or alternatives such as PyPy. Implementers performing static analysis should account for version differences (Python 2 vs. Python 3) as the syntax is not fully compatible between major versions.

title: PyTorch Model
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a model checkpoint or weights file saved in PyTorch's native serialization format (pickle-based). PyTorch files are the original distribution format for many open models before they are converted to safer or more portable formats.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap must consume a raw PyTorch checkpoint. In most deployment scenarios, prefer [SafeTensors](https://capdag.com/browse/media:safetensors;model) over PyTorch files because SafeTensors avoids the security risks of Python pickle deserialization and loads faster.

## Notes
PyTorch files use the `.pt` or `.pth` extensions and `application/octet-stream` MIME type. They may contain full model state dicts, partial checkpoints, or optimizer states — the exact contents depend on how the file was saved. Implementers should handle both `torch.save` state dicts and full `nn.Module` pickles.

title: Query Name
documentation:
## Purpose

A Query Name identifies a named query within a machine or cap, allowing it to be referenced by other components. It must consist only of alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores, with a maximum length of 128 characters.

## When to use

Use this media type when passing a query identifier as an input to a node that dispatches or looks up a named query. It is commonly used alongside [Substitutions](https://capdag.com/browse/media:substitutions;textable;record) to parameterize the resolved query.

## Notes

The pattern constraint (`^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$`) ensures query names are safe for use as identifiers in URLs, file names, and configuration keys without escaping.

title: Question Text
documentation:
## Purpose
A single natural-language question to be answered, evaluated, or routed by a machine. Suitable for Q&A, classification, and decision machines that process one question at a time.

## When to use
Use `media:question;textable` for single-question inputs. When processing multiple questions in bulk, use the companion `media:list;question;textable` type, which carries one question per line.

## Notes
Length is constrained to 5–4096 characters. See [Question List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;question;textable) for batch inputs and [Decision](https://capdag.com/browse/media:decision;json;record;textable) for the corresponding output type.

title: Question List
description: Text file with one question per line.
documentation:
## Purpose
A newline-delimited list of questions for batch processing. Each line is treated as an independent question by the receiving machine.

## When to use
Use this type when a machine must evaluate or answer many questions in a single invocation. For single questions, use [Question Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:question;textable) instead.

## Notes
Empty lines should be ignored by consumers. The `.txt` extension is conventional but not required.

title: R Source
documentation:
## Purpose
R source files contain code written in R, a language and runtime environment designed for statistical computing, data analysis, and visualization. R is the dominant language in academic statistics, bioinformatics, and data science workflows.

## When to use
Use [R Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:r;textable;code) for `.r` script files and `.rmd` R Markdown files. R Markdown (`.rmd`) interleaves prose, code chunks, and output specifications to produce reproducible reports; it requires an R Markdown-aware processor rather than a plain R interpreter.

## Notes
R is dynamically typed with a functional style and vector-first semantics. The CRAN package ecosystem is central to R workflows; packages are managed via `install.packages()` or the `renv` lockfile system. R Markdown files are processed by the `knitr` and `rmarkdown` packages and may generate HTML, PDF, or Word output depending on the document header.

title: RAR Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
RAR is a proprietary archive format developed by Eugene Roshal (WinRAR) that supports solid compression, multi-volume splitting, recovery records, and optional AES encryption. It has been widely used for distributing large files on the internet.

## When to use
Use [RAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:rar;archive) when processing or archiving content received from external sources that use RAR packaging, particularly split multi-part archives. For producing new archives, prefer open formats such as [ZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zip;archive) or [7-Zip Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:7z;archive).

## Notes
RAR is a proprietary format; creating RAR archives requires a commercial WinRAR license, while extraction is freely available via `unrar`. RAR5 (the current version) is not backward-compatible with RAR4. Implementers should detect the RAR version from the file signature before selecting a decoding library.

title: RAW Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents unprocessed sensor data captured directly by a digital camera, before demosaicing and colour processing. RAW files preserve maximum dynamic range and allow full control over white balance, exposure, and noise reduction in post-processing.

## When to use
Use `media:raw;image` when a node ingests or produces camera RAW files, such as an image ingestion pipeline, a RAW converter, or a photo library management tool. RAW files should be converted to a standard format like [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image) or [TIFF Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tiff;image) before passing to nodes that do not support RAW decoding.

## Notes
RAW formats are vendor-specific and vary widely (CR2 for Canon, NEF for Nikon, ARW for Sony, DNG as an open standard). Nodes that process RAW files should use a library such as LibRaw or dcraw. DNG is preferred when a portable RAW format is required.

title: Reasoning Context
documentation:
## Purpose

Reasoning Context supplies background knowledge, constraints, or domain rules that a model should apply when performing logical analysis, question answering, or multi-step reasoning tasks.

## When to use

Use this media type when a reasoning or QA node needs grounding beyond its training knowledge. It is particularly valuable for specialized domains such as legal, scientific, or proprietary business logic where the model cannot be expected to reason correctly without explicit context.

## Notes

The maximum length is 32,768 characters, the largest of the context media types, reflecting the depth of background that complex reasoning tasks may require. Organize the context with numbered facts or labeled sections to help the model reference specific points in its output.

title: Remove Boilerplate
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that tells a machine to strip standard boilerplate content from its input or output — such as legal disclaimers, headers, footers, or repeated standard clauses — before further processing.

## When to use
Enable when processing documents that contain recurring non-informative content that would consume token budget without adding analytical value (e.g., contract preambles, cookie banners, standard license headers).

## Notes
The definition of "boilerplate" is machine-specific. See [Include Comments Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:include-comments-flag;textable) for the analogous control over code comments and [Preserve Structure Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:preserve-structure-flag;textable) for retaining document structure.

title: Repetition Penalty
documentation:
## Purpose
A lightweight repetition penalty parameter for machines that apply token-level discouragement of repeated output without full inference metadata.

## When to use
Use this type when connecting a simple numeric penalty value to a machine that does not require override-level controls. For full inference configuration, prefer [Repeat Penalty](https://capdag.com/browse/media:repeat-penalty;inference;sampling;user;task;textable;numeric), which includes metadata and override scope.

## Notes
Minimum value is 1.0 (no penalty). No upper bound is defined in this spec; check the target machine's documentation for practical limits. Values significantly above 1.5 risk degrading output coherence.

title: Repository
documentation:
## Purpose

Repository identifies the source from which a model or resource should be fetched. It distinguishes between public model hubs, locally stored models, and custom or private registries.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node that loads models needs to know where to retrieve the artifact. Set to `huggingface` for models hosted on Hugging Face Hub, `local` for models already present on disk, and `custom` for any other registry accessible via the node's configuration.

## Notes

When `local` is selected, the node typically expects a corresponding path or identifier pointing to the on-disk location. When `custom` is selected, additional configuration (such as a base URL or credentials) is usually required via the operator's settings.

title: Require Explanation Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that instructs a machine to accompany its primary output with a natural-language explanation of how or why that output was produced.

## When to use
Enable in evaluation, audit, or user-facing pipelines where human reviewers need to understand model reasoning. Explanations increase output length; disable in high-throughput pipelines where latency is critical and rationale is not needed.

## Notes
See [Detailed Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:detailed-flag;textable) for broader verbosity control and [Decision](https://capdag.com/browse/media:decision;json;record;textable) for the canonical output type that may carry explanations alongside binary verdicts.

title: Python Requirements
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a single Python package requirement specifier or the full text of a `requirements.txt` file as a scalar value. This variant is used when the content is treated as an opaque string rather than a parsed list of entries.

## When to use
Use `media:requirements;textable` when a pipeline node passes requirements content as a single text blob — for example, an LLM node that generates or edits a requirements file as raw text. For structured access to individual package entries, use [Python Requirements List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:requirements;textable;list).

## Notes
Python package specifiers follow PEP 508 syntax (e.g., `requests>=2.28,<3`). Requirements files also support `-r` includes, `-c` constraints, and URL-based installs, which a list parser may need to handle separately. See also [Python Requirements](https://capdag.com/browse/media:requirements;textable).

title: Python Requirements List
description: Python requirements file with multiple package entries
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a `requirements.txt` file as a structured list, where each entry is a separate package specifier. This variant is used when the pipeline needs to iterate over, filter, or modify individual dependency entries.

## When to use
Use `media:requirements;textable;list` when a pipeline step processes individual dependency lines — for example, a vulnerability scanner that checks each package version, a dependency updater that pins versions, or a resolver that validates compatibility.

## Notes
Each line in a requirements file is a PEP 508 specifier, a comment, or a directive (`-r`, `-c`, `-e`). Blank lines and comment lines (`#`) should be handled gracefully. For treating the entire file as one text value, use [Python Requirements](https://capdag.com/browse/media:requirements;textable). See also [Python Requirements List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:requirements;textable;list).

title: RPM Package
documentation:
## Purpose
RPM (Red Hat Package Manager) is the binary package format for Red Hat-based Linux distributions (RHEL, Fedora, CentOS, Rocky Linux, openSUSE, etc.). An `.rpm` file contains a lead section, signature, header metadata, and a compressed CPIO payload. The [RPM Package](https://capdag.com/browse/media:rpm;archive) media type represents any `.rpm` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes RPM-family Linux packages — for example, software build and release pipelines, private YUM/DNF repository management, deployment automation, or security and compliance scanning workflows.

## Notes
RPM packages use a custom binary format; do not attempt to parse them as ZIP or tar archives. Implementers should use `rpm2cpio` to extract the CPIO payload or use the `rpm` command-line tools and `rpm-python` bindings for metadata access. Packages are signed with GPG; verification requires importing the publisher's public key via `rpm --import`. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: reStructuredText
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a reStructuredText (reST) document, the markup format used by the Python documentation ecosystem and Sphinx. reST provides directives, roles, and cross-referencing features that go beyond what Markdown offers.

## When to use
Use `media:rst;textable` when a node produces or consumes reST-formatted content, such as documentation generators targeting Sphinx, or extractors that read Python project docs. For simpler Markdown, use [Markdown Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:md;textable).

## Notes
reStructuredText syntax is stricter than Markdown and parsers are less interchangeable. Consumers should not attempt to render reST with a Markdown renderer. The `textable` tag allows reST to flow to generic text consumers.

title: Rich Text Format
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Rich Text Format documents (`.rtf`), a Microsoft-developed cross-platform document format that encodes text with formatting using ASCII control words. RTF is supported by virtually all word processors and has been widely used for formatted document interchange since the late 1980s.

## When to use
Use `media:rtf;textable` when a pipeline node processes RTF documents — for example, a text extraction step using `python-rtf` or LibreOffice, an email attachment handler that renders RTF bodies, or a legacy document conversion step.

## Notes
RTF is technically a plain-text format (ASCII control codes), making it `textable` even though it carries rich formatted content. LibreOffice and most word processors can convert RTF to DOCX or PDF. The format is largely superseded by DOCX but remains common in legal and email contexts. See also [Rich Text Format](https://capdag.com/browse/media:rtf;textable).

title: Ruby Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Ruby source files contain code written in Ruby, a dynamic, object-oriented scripting language known for its expressive syntax and the Rails web framework. Ruby is used for web development, scripting, build automation (Rake), and tool development.

## When to use
Use [Ruby Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ruby;textable;code) for `.rb` application files, `Rakefile` and `.rake` task files, and `.gemspec` gem specification files. All are syntactically valid Ruby and can be processed by the same toolchain.

## Notes
Ruby source is UTF-8 text. The language is highly dynamic with pervasive metaprogramming via `method_missing`, `define_method`, and open classes, which limits static analysis accuracy. Ruby version management (rbenv, rvm, asdf) is common in Ruby projects; the required version is typically declared in `.ruby-version` or the `Gemfile`.

title: Rust Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Rust source files contain code written in the Rust systems programming language, which emphasizes memory safety without a garbage collector, zero-cost abstractions, and high performance. Rust is used for systems software, WebAssembly, embedded targets, and high-performance services.

## When to use
Use [Rust Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:rust;textable;code) for any `.rs` file in a Rust crate. Rust projects are organized via Cargo; the source tree under `src/` holds the actual Rust files while `Cargo.toml` holds the manifest.

## Notes
Rust source is UTF-8 text. The compiler enforces strict ownership and borrowing rules at compile time, which eliminates many classes of runtime errors. Rust edition (2015, 2018, 2021, 2024) affects syntax and some language semantics; the edition is declared in `Cargo.toml` and should be considered when parsing or analyzing source.

title: SafeTensors Model
documentation:
## Purpose
Identifies a file in the SafeTensors format, a safe and fast alternative to PyTorch pickle files for storing model weights. SafeTensors files are commonly used for models distributed via HuggingFace and are required by the Candle backend.

## When to use
Use this type when a cap produces or consumes SafeTensors weight files. Models in this format are typically loaded by the Candle backend or any runtime that supports the HuggingFace Transformers ecosystem. See [Candle LLM Model Spec](https://capdag.com/browse/media:model-spec;candle;textable;llm) for the corresponding model reference type.

## Notes
A complete model in SafeTensors format usually consists of multiple `.safetensors` shards plus JSON configuration files in the same directory. Caps that load SafeTensors models typically expect a directory path rather than a single file path.

title: Scala Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Scala source files contain code written in Scala, a statically typed language that combines object-oriented and functional programming on the JVM. Scala is widely used in big data (Apache Spark is written in Scala), distributed systems, and domain-specific language construction.

## When to use
Use [Scala Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:scala;textable;code) for `.scala` source files and `.sc` Scala script files (used by tools like Ammonite and sbt's scripting mode). Scala projects are typically built with sbt, Maven, or Mill.

## Notes
Scala 2 and Scala 3 (Dotty) have significant syntax and macro system differences; tools and libraries often target one major version specifically. Scala source is UTF-8 text. The rich type system with implicits (Scala 2) or `given`/`using` (Scala 3) is central to the library ecosystem and requires Scala-aware tooling to reason about correctly.

title: Schema Variables
description: Schema Variables object with key-value pairs
documentation:
## Purpose

Schema Variables carry a JSON object of key-value pairs that are injected into a schema before validation or generation. They allow schemas to be templated and reused across different contexts by varying only the variable bindings.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node consumes a parameterized schema and needs the variable bindings resolved at runtime. It is similar in structure to [Substitutions](https://capdag.com/browse/media:substitutions;textable;record) but scoped specifically to schema processing.

## Notes

Variable names should match the placeholder tokens defined in the schema. Unresolved variables may cause validation failures, so ensure all required keys are present before passing this value downstream.

title: Scheme Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Scheme source files, a minimalist dialect of Lisp characterized by lexical scoping, first-class continuations, and tail-call optimization. Scheme is widely used in academic and research contexts as well as embedded scripting.

## When to use
Use `media:scheme;textable;code` when a pipeline step processes `.scm` or `.ss` source — for example, a REPL evaluation node or a macro-expansion analysis tool.

## Notes
Multiple Scheme implementations (Guile, Racket in compatibility mode, Chicken, Chez) share the `.scm` extension but may differ in available libraries and syntax extensions. See also [Scheme Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:scheme;textable;code).

title: SCSS Stylesheet
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries SCSS and Sass stylesheet files, a widely adopted CSS preprocessor that adds variables, nesting, mixins, functions, and partials to CSS. SCSS uses CSS-compatible brace syntax; the older indented Sass syntax uses `.sass` files.

## When to use
Use `media:scss;textable` when a pipeline node compiles or processes SCSS — for example, a Sass CLI compilation step, a PostCSS pipeline, or a design-token generation step that emits SCSS variable files.

## Notes
The `.scss` extension uses CSS-compatible syntax and is the dominant convention in modern projects. The `.sass` extension uses indentation-based syntax without braces or semicolons. Both are compiled by the Dart Sass implementation. See also [SCSS Stylesheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:scss;textable).

title: Cache size for embeddings in MB
description: Cache size for embeddings in MB

title: Seed
documentation:
## Purpose
An unsigned integer used to initialize the random number generator, making model outputs reproducible across runs when all other parameters are held constant.

## When to use
Supply a seed when you need deterministic output for testing, debugging, or auditing. Leave it unset in production when varied outputs are desirable.

## Notes
Valid range: 0–4294967295 (32-bit unsigned). Reproducibility depends on the runtime and hardware; results may differ across framework versions or GPU configurations even with the same seed. See [Sampling Seed](https://capdag.com/browse/media:sampling-seed;inference;sampling;user;task;textable;numeric) for the inference-specific variant with richer metadata.

title: Sequence Separator
documentation:
## Purpose

Sequence Separator is a plain-text string used to delimit elements when joining or splitting a sequence into or from a single text value. It allows callers to control how list items are serialized or parsed.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node that produces or consumes a flat text representation of a list needs a caller-supplied delimiter. Common values include newline (`\n`), comma (`,`), or a pipe (`|`).

## Notes

The separator is interpreted literally by the consuming node. Ensure the chosen separator does not appear within the sequence elements themselves, or the split/join operation will produce incorrect results.

title: Shell Script
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries POSIX shell and Bash script files used for system automation, build orchestration, and environment setup. Shell scripts are the lowest common denominator for scripting on Unix-compatible systems.

## When to use
Use `media:shell;textable;code` when a pipeline step runs or analyzes generic shell scripts — for example, a sandboxed executor running `.sh` bootstrap scripts or a static analyzer checking for unsafe patterns. For Zsh-specific scripts, prefer [Zsh Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zsh;textable;code); for Fish, prefer [Fish Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fish;textable;code).

## Notes
Both `.sh` (POSIX shell) and `.bash` (Bash-specific) extensions are covered. Scripts typically declare their interpreter via a shebang line (`#!/bin/sh` or `#!/bin/bash`), which determines runtime behavior independently of the extension. See also [Shell Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:shell;textable;code).

title: Source Media Urn
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries the URN of the media type that was used as the source or input for a derived artifact. Provides provenance information so downstream machines and users can trace what kind of content originated the current output.

## When to use
Attach this value to output records from conversion, extraction, or transformation machines where consumers need to know the original media type. It is particularly useful in multi-modal pipelines that accept several input formats.

## Notes
