Must match the pattern `^media:.+` with a minimum length of 7. The value should be a valid URN from the capdag media registry. See [File Path](https://capdag.com/browse/media:file-path;textable) for carrying the source file location alongside this URN.

title: SQL Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries SQL source files containing queries, DDL statements, stored procedures, or migration scripts. SQL is the standard language for interacting with relational databases across all major engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server, and others).

## When to use
Use `media:sql;textable;code` when a pipeline step executes, validates, or generates SQL — for example, a database migration runner, a query optimizer, or an LLM node asked to produce SQL from natural language.

## Notes
SQL syntax varies between database engines; a file targeting PostgreSQL-specific features (e.g., `RETURNING`, array types) may not be portable to MySQL or SQLite. Implementers should document which dialect a node expects when dialect matters. See also [SQL Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:sql;textable;code).

title: SQLite Database
documentation:
## Purpose
SQLite is a self-contained, serverless relational database engine stored as a single file. The [SQLite Database](https://capdag.com/browse/media:sqlite) media type represents any `.sqlite`, `.sqlite3`, or `.db` file that follows the SQLite file format.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes a structured relational dataset that must remain portable as a single file artifact — for example, query results, local caches, or embedded application data stores. It is a better choice than CSV or JSON when the data has multiple tables, foreign keys, or requires SQL querying.

## Notes
Implementers should use the SQLite C library or a language binding to open files; do not treat them as raw binary blobs. Consumers that only need read access should open the database in read-only mode to avoid unintended writes or WAL file creation.

title: SRT Subtitles
documentation:
## Purpose
SRT (SubRip Text) is a plain-text subtitle format that stores an ordered sequence of cue blocks, each with a sequence number, a timecode range, and one or more lines of text. The [SRT Subtitles](https://capdag.com/browse/media:srt;textable;list) media type represents any `.srt` file following this convention.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes subtitle or caption data — for example, transcription pipelines, video localization workflows, or accessibility tooling. SRT is the most widely supported subtitle format and is accepted by most video players and streaming platforms.

## Notes
SRT files are plain text (typically UTF-8 or Latin-1). Each block consists of: a 1-based sequence number, a timecode line in `HH:MM:SS,mmm --> HH:MM:SS,mmm` format, and one or more text lines followed by a blank line. The `;textable;list` tags reflect the human-readable ordered-list nature. Implementers should strip HTML-like tags (e.g., `<i>`, `<b>`) if plain text output is required, as many SRT files use them for styling.

title: IP address to bind the server to
description: IP address to bind the server to

title: Port number for the gRPC server
description: Port number for the gRPC server

title: STL Model
documentation:
## Purpose
STL (Stereolithography) is a format for representing the surface geometry of a 3D solid as a collection of triangular facets with normal vectors. The [STL Model](https://capdag.com/browse/media:stl;model) media type represents `.stl` files in either binary or ASCII encoding.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes 3D geometry for additive manufacturing (3D printing), CAD inspection, or mesh repair workflows. STL is the de-facto standard input format for slicing software (PrusaSlicer, Cura, etc.) and is supported universally across CAD and 3D-printing tools.

## Notes
Binary STL begins with an 80-byte header followed by a triangle count and packed triangle records; ASCII STL begins with `solid`. Implementers should detect the encoding by checking the first bytes rather than relying on a naming convention. STL does not support color, texture, or scene hierarchy — for richer 3D assets use [GLB](https://capdag.com/browse/media:glb;model) or [OBJ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:obj;model). Manifold (watertight) geometry is required for reliable 3D printing.

title: Stream Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that instructs a machine to emit output incrementally as tokens are generated, rather than buffering the complete response before returning it.

## When to use
Enable streaming for user-facing interfaces where progressive display improves perceived responsiveness. Disable it (the default) for batch pipelines, post-processing chains, or any consumer that requires the full output before proceeding.

## Notes
Streaming behavior is transport-dependent. Not all machine hosts or output ports support streaming; check the runtime documentation. When streaming is enabled, intermediate output frames may not conform to the final content type's schema.

title: Substitutions
documentation:
## Purpose

Substitutions carry a JSON object whose keys and values are plain strings. They are used to fill template placeholders in queries, prompts, or other parameterized content before execution.

## When to use

Use this media type when a node needs to receive a dynamic set of variable bindings at runtime. Pair it with [Query Name](https://capdag.com/browse/media:query-name;textable) to identify the query to execute and supply the values that replace its placeholders.

## Notes

Keys and values must both be strings. Nesting is not supported; flatten complex structures into dot-separated or underscore-separated key names before passing them as substitutions.

title: Summary Context
documentation:
## Purpose

Summary Context provides framing information that helps the model produce a more targeted summary, such as the intended audience, the reason for the summary, or key terms to prioritize.

## When to use

Use this media type alongside [Summary Type](https://capdag.com/browse/media:summary-type;textable) and [Summary Focus](https://capdag.com/browse/media:summary-focus;textable) when the summarization task requires audience-awareness or domain framing beyond what the source document itself provides.

## Notes

The maximum length is 8,192 characters. Brief, well-structured context (two to four sentences) is typically more effective than long narrative instructions.

title: Summary Focus
documentation:
## Purpose

Summary Focus narrows the aspect of the source material that the summary should emphasize, ensuring the model highlights the information most relevant to the reader's purpose.

## When to use

Use this media type alongside [Summary Type](https://capdag.com/browse/media:summary-type;textable) when the summarization node supports focused extraction. For general-purpose summaries, `main_points` or `overview` are safe defaults.

## Notes

Choosing `methodology` or `results` is most effective for research documents. `implications` suits strategic or analytical content. When the source does not contain the chosen focus area, the model will fall back to available material and may not produce the expected structure.

title: Summary Type
documentation:
## Purpose

Summary Type selects the structural approach the model uses when condensing content. The choice affects both the form of the output (prose vs. bullets) and the level of interpretation applied (extractive vs. abstractive).

## When to use

Use this media type as an input to summarization nodes. Combine it with [Summary Focus](https://capdag.com/browse/media:summary-focus;textable) to specify both the method and the aspect of the source material to emphasize, and with [Summary Context](https://capdag.com/browse/media:summary-contextable) to provide framing information.

## Notes

`extractive` summaries quote directly from the source; `abstractive` ones rephrase in the model's own words. `executive` and `brief` are length-oriented presets, while `bullet_points` imposes a list structure regardless of length.

title: Svelte Component
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Svelte single-file components that colocate template markup, scoped styles, and reactive JavaScript in one `.svelte` file. Svelte compiles components to vanilla JavaScript at build time rather than shipping a runtime framework.

## When to use
Use `media:svelte;textable;code` when a pipeline node handles `.svelte` files — for example, a SvelteKit build step, a component linting tool using `svelte-check`, or an LLM node generating Svelte UI components.

## Notes
Svelte's reactivity model uses a compiler transform rather than a virtual DOM, which produces smaller and faster output than equivalent React or Vue code. SvelteKit extends Svelte with file-based routing and server-side rendering. See also [Svelte Component](https://capdag.com/browse/media:svelte;textable;code).

title: SVG Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an SVG file, an XML-based vector image format that describes graphics using geometric primitives, paths, and text. SVG images scale without quality loss and can be styled and animated with CSS.

## When to use
Use `media:svg;image;textable` when a node produces or consumes SVG graphics — for example a diagram generator, an icon renderer, or a chart exporter. The `textable` tag indicates the payload is human-readable XML, while the `image` tag routes it to image-aware consumers.

## Notes
SVG files may embed raster images, reference external fonts, or include JavaScript. Consumers that render SVG should sanitize or sandbox the content to prevent unintended script execution. For raster alternatives, see [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [WebP Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webp;image).

title: Swift Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Swift source files contain code written in Swift, Apple's modern, statically typed programming language designed for iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and server-side development. Swift replaced Objective-C as Apple's primary application language and emphasizes safety, expressiveness, and performance.

## When to use
Use [Swift Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:swift;textable;code) for any `.swift` file in an Xcode project, Swift Package, or server-side Swift application. Swift is the dominant language for Apple platform development and is often found alongside [Objective-C Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:objc;textable;code) in projects that have migrated incrementally.

## Notes
Swift source is UTF-8 text. The language has evolved rapidly across major versions; source written for Swift 4 may require updates for Swift 6 due to changes such as the concurrency model (async/await, actors). Swift packages are declared via `Package.swift`, which is itself a Swift source file.

title: System Prompt
documentation:
## Purpose
The system prompt establishes the model's persona, constraints, and operational rules for a session. It is injected before user messages and is not typically visible to end users.

## When to use
Use this media type when a machine port accepts a session-level instruction block that governs all subsequent turns. Prefer [Base Prompt](https://capdag.com/browse/media:base-prompt;inference;policy;user;task;textable) for operator-defined default behaviors; reserve the system prompt for cap-specific or deployment-specific overrides.

## Notes
Maximum length is 8192 characters. Changes to the system prompt affect the entire session, so treat it as a high-trust input. See [Prompt](https://capdag.com/browse/media:prompt;textable) for per-request instructions.

title: TAR Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
TAR (Tape Archive) is a Unix file bundling format that concatenates files and directories into a single stream while preserving permissions, ownership, and symbolic links. It does not compress data on its own.

## When to use
Use [TAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tar;archive) when you need to bundle files for archival or transfer and want to apply compression separately, or when a downstream tool expects a plain tar stream. On Unix-like systems, TAR is the standard container for source distributions and system backups.

## Notes
Because TAR stores no compression, it is commonly combined with a compressor: [TAR.GZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:targz;archive) for gzip, or XZ/LZMA for higher ratios. TAR preserves POSIX metadata that [ZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zip;archive) does not, making it the preferred choice for Unix software distribution and container image layers.

title: Tarball (gzipped)
documentation:
## Purpose
A tarball (`.tgz` or `.tar.gz`) combines TAR bundling with gzip compression into a single file. It is the most common distribution format for Unix and Linux software packages, source code, and container layers.

## When to use
Use [Tarball (gzipped)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:targz;archive) when distributing source code, binaries, or data sets for Unix-like systems where POSIX metadata (permissions, symlinks) must be preserved and good compression is desired. It is the standard output format for `git archive` and many build systems.

## Notes
Gzip compression is solid — it compresses the entire archive stream rather than individual files — which gives better ratios than [ZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zip;archive) on collections of similar files. However, unlike ZIP, individual files cannot be extracted without reading from the start of the stream. For higher compression ratios at the cost of speed, consider [XZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive) or [Zstandard](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zstd;archive).

title: Text Page
description: Plain text content representing a single page of a document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a single page extracted from a multi-page document as plain text. The `page` tag signals that the content is a positional unit within a larger document rather than a standalone text file.

## When to use
Use `media:textable;page` when a node produces or consumes one page of text extracted from a PDF or similar paginated source. For the full plain-text content of a document, use [Plain Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:txt;textable).

## Notes
Page boundaries and whitespace are preserved where possible to maintain layout fidelity. Consumers should not assume any particular page numbering scheme; that context should be carried in the node's output metadata.

title: TIFF Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a TIFF (Tagged Image File Format) raster image. TIFF is a flexible, high-quality format widely used in professional photography, printing, scanning, and document imaging pipelines.

## When to use
Use `media:tiff;image` when a node produces or consumes TIFF images, particularly in archival, print, or scanner workflows where lossless quality and rich metadata (EXIF, IPTC) must be preserved. For web delivery or general-purpose use, prefer [PNG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:png) or [JPEG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jpeg;image).

## Notes
TIFF supports multiple compression schemes (LZW, ZIP, JPEG, uncompressed) and can store multiple pages in a single file. Consumers should handle multi-page TIFF files and confirm the compression type before processing.

title: Timestamps Flag
documentation:
## Purpose
A boolean flag that instructs a machine to annotate its output with timestamps, typically indicating when each segment was produced or what time range it corresponds to.

## When to use
Enable for audio transcription, log processing, or time-series analysis machines where temporal anchoring of output segments is required. Disable when timestamps would clutter or complicate downstream parsing.

## Notes
See also [Timestamps](https://capdag.com/browse/media:timestamps;textable), which carries timestamp data as a media value rather than as a flag controlling output behavior. Timestamp format is machine-specific.

title: Timestamps
description: Boolean flag indicating whether to include timestamps in output.
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a boolean value indicating whether the receiving machine should include timestamps in its output. Serves as a data-carrying counterpart to the behavioral [Timestamps Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:timestamps-flag;textable).

## When to use
Use this type when a graph passes a timestamps preference as a data value between machines rather than as a static configuration. For static configuration, prefer [Timestamps Flag](https://capdag.com/browse/media:timestamps-flag;textable).

## Notes
The exact interpretation of "timestamps" (segment start/end times, wall-clock annotations, etc.) is determined by the downstream machine. Ensure the receiving machine documents its timestamp format.

title: TOML Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a TOML (Tom's Obvious Minimal Language) configuration document. TOML is designed for human-friendly configuration with clear semantics, and is widely used for tool configuration files and application settings.

## When to use
Use `media:toml;textable;record` when a node produces or consumes TOML-formatted configuration data. For key-value configuration in other formats, see [INI Configuration](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ini;textable;record) or [YAML Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:record;textable;yaml).

## Notes
TOML documents map to a single key-value table at the root level. The `record` tag reflects this structure. Consumers should use a TOML-compliant parser; TOML syntax is not compatible with INI or YAML parsers.

title: Tone
documentation:
## Purpose

Tone specifies the emotional register and interpersonal stance of the generated text, shaping how readers experience the content rather than what information it conveys.

## When to use

Use this media type alongside [Genre](https://capdag.com/browse/media:genre;textable) and [Creativity Level](https://capdag.com/browse/media:creativity-level;textable;numeric) when configuring creative or editorial writing nodes. It is also applicable to customer-facing content nodes where brand voice matters.

## Notes

Tone affects word choice, sentence rhythm, and the degree of formality. When tone and genre conflict (for example, `humorous` with `academic`), the model will attempt to honor both, but results may vary; test outputs carefully.

title: Audio Transcription
description: Transcribed text structured as a map with metadata.
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents the structured output of a speech-to-text operation. The payload is a JSON object containing the transcribed text along with metadata such as language, confidence scores, and segment timestamps.

## When to use
Use `media:transcription;textable;record` as the output type of any node that produces transcription results from audio input, particularly from [Speech Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:audio;speech;wav). Downstream nodes that consume plain text should extract the relevant text field before passing it on.

## Notes
The `textable` and `record` tags indicate that the payload is both human-readable text and a structured key-value record. Consumers should treat the JSON object as the canonical form; the profile schema at `https://capdag.com/schema/transcription` defines the required and optional fields.

title: MPEG Transport Stream
documentation:
## Purpose
MPEG Transport Stream (TS) is a packetized container designed for transmission over lossy or unreliable channels such as broadcast television, satellite, and IPTV. It multiplexes one or more program streams with error correction and synchronization metadata.

## When to use
Use [MPEG Transport Stream](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ts;video) when working with video captured from broadcast receivers, satellite tuners, Blu-ray discs (M2TS), or IPTV pipelines. TS is also the standard container for HLS (HTTP Live Streaming) segments, making it common in adaptive streaming infrastructure.

## Notes
TS files use fixed 188-byte packets and carry PIDs to identify individual streams (video, audio, subtitles, EPG data). The `.mts` and `.m2ts` extensions are used by AVCHD camcorders and Blu-ray respectively, and may carry MPEG-2 or H.264 video. Implementers should use a demuxer that understands PID mapping before extracting elementary streams.

title: TSV Data
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents tabular data where fields are separated by tab characters. TSV is a common interchange format for spreadsheets and database exports, and is often easier to parse than CSV because tab characters rarely appear in field values.

## When to use
Use `media:tsv;textable` when a node produces or consumes tab-delimited tabular data. For comma-delimited tabular data, see [CSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csv;list;record;textable). For pipe-delimited data, see [PSV Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:psv;textable).

## Notes
TSV files may or may not include a header row; consumers should confirm this via node configuration or metadata. The `textable` tag allows TSV content to flow to any generic text consumer, though structured parsing will be needed to work with individual fields.

title: TypeScript JSX Source
documentation:
## Purpose
TSX files contain TypeScript source code that uses JSX syntax to define UI components. They combine the static type checking of [TypeScript](https://capdag.com/browse/media:typescript;textable;code) with the declarative component syntax of [JSX](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jsx;textable;code).

## When to use
Use [TypeScript JSX Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tsx;textable;code) for React component files written in TypeScript. The `.tsx` extension signals to the TypeScript compiler that JSX transformations are required; plain TypeScript without JSX should use the `.ts` extension and [TypeScript Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:typescript;textable;code).

## Notes
The TypeScript compiler requires `"jsx"` to be set in `tsconfig.json` (e.g., `"react"`, `"react-jsx"`, `"preserve"`) to process `.tsx` files. Generic type parameters in TSX require a trailing comma (`<T,>`) to disambiguate from JSX tags. TSX is the dominant format in modern React applications using TypeScript.

title: TrueType Font
documentation:
## Purpose
TrueType is a font format developed by Apple and Microsoft that stores glyph outlines using quadratic Bezier curves. The [TrueType Font](https://capdag.com/browse/media:ttf;font) media type represents any `.ttf` binary font file conforming to the TrueType specification.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes desktop or document fonts — for example, font bundling pipelines, PDF generation workflows, or document rendering engines that embed fonts. TrueType remains widely supported across operating systems and applications, though for web use the [WOFF2](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff2;font) format is preferred.

## Notes
TrueType files begin with an offset table (`sfnt` wrapper) and contain named font tables (`cmap`, `glyf`, `hmtx`, etc.). Implementers should use a font library such as FreeType, HarfBuzz, or FontTools (`fonttools` Python package) for reliable parsing and subsetting. The `;font` tag indicates this is a font artifact.

title: Plain Text
documentation:
## Purpose
The base type for human-readable text without markup or structural encoding. Any content that is UTF-8 text and does not conform to a more specific format (Markdown, HTML, CSV, etc.) should use this type.

## When to use
Use `media:txt;textable` as the output of nodes that produce raw text, or as the input to nodes that consume generic text such as language models or text summarizers. More specific text types like [Markdown](https://capdag.com/browse/media:md;textable) or [Log File](https://capdag.com/browse/media:log;textable) should be preferred when the structure is known.

## Notes
The `textable` tag makes this type compatible with any pipeline node that accepts readable text. Encoding is assumed to be UTF-8 unless stated otherwise in the node's configuration.

title: TypeScript Source
documentation:
## Purpose
TypeScript source files contain code written in TypeScript, a statically typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft. TypeScript adds optional type annotations, interfaces, generics, and other features that improve tooling support and catch errors at compile time.

## When to use
Use [TypeScript Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:typescript;textable;code) for `.ts` files. Note that `.ts` is also used for MPEG Transport Stream video files; context and MIME type must be used to disambiguate. For TypeScript files containing JSX, use [TypeScript JSX Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tsx;textable;code).

## Notes
TypeScript is compiled to JavaScript using the `tsc` compiler or a bundler plugin before execution. Declaration files (`.d.ts`) are a special form of TypeScript that contain only type information without runtime code; they may be processed differently by type-checking pipelines. The language version and compiler options are specified in `tsconfig.json`.

title: Visual Basic Source
description: Visual Basic programming language source code
documentation:
## Purpose
Visual Basic source files contain code written in Visual Basic (VB.NET) or its legacy predecessor Visual Basic 6. VB.NET is a .NET language that offers a gentle syntax for developers coming from classic BASIC, while sharing the full .NET runtime with [C# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:csharp;textable;code) and [F# Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:fsharp;textable;code).

## When to use
Use [Visual Basic Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vb;textable;code) for `.vb` VB.NET files, `.vbs` VBScript files used in Windows automation and legacy web ASP, and `.bas` BASIC module files from Visual Basic 6 projects. Treat `.vbs` files with care as they are often used in scripting contexts distinct from compiled .NET code.

## Notes
VB.NET and VB6 are syntactically similar but not directly compatible; VB6 lacks many .NET features and its runtime is separate. VBScript (`.vbs`) is an even more restricted subset used in Windows Script Host and Internet Explorer. Implementers should distinguish these three variants based on file extension and context before applying language-specific tooling.

title: vCard
documentation:
## Purpose
vCard (RFC 6350) is a text format for representing contact information — names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, and other properties. The [vCard](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vcf;textable;list) media type represents `.vcf` files that may contain one or more vCard records.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes contact data — for example, address-book exports, CRM integrations, contact synchronization pipelines, or QR-code contact cards. vCard is the standard format for contact exchange across Apple Contacts, Google Contacts, and Microsoft Outlook.

## Notes
A single `.vcf` file may contain multiple contacts, each delimited by `BEGIN:VCARD` / `END:VCARD`. The `;textable;list` tags reflect that the file is human-readable text containing an ordered list of contact records. Implementers should use an RFC 6350-compliant parser to handle property encoding, parameter quoting, and multi-line folding correctly.

title: Video Data
documentation:
## Purpose
This is the generic video semantic type covering any video content regardless of specific container or codec. It acts as a broad category for video data when the precise format is unknown, unimportant, or when a pipeline accepts multiple video types under a single type reference.

## When to use
Use [Video Data](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video) as a catch-all media type in graph nodes or machine inputs that can accept any video format. When the specific container is known, prefer the format-specific type such as [MKV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mkv;video), [WebM Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webm;video), or [MOV](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mov;video).

## Notes
The representative MIME type is `video/mp4` but this type is not limited to MP4 files. Implementers matching on this type should apply container detection at runtime. This type is useful in schema definitions where any video input is acceptable and strict format enforcement is deferred to the processing stage.

title: Void Value
documentation:
## Purpose
The [Void Value](https://capdag.com/browse/media:void) media type represents the absence of a value — analogous to `void` in programming languages or `null` in type theory. It is used to type ports or outputs that carry no data payload.

## When to use
Use this type for node outputs that signal completion or success without returning any data — for example, a side-effectful node that writes to a database and has nothing to return, or a terminal sink node. It is also appropriate as the type of an optional input port whose absence is meaningful.

## Notes
A void value produces no bytes; its byte length is always zero. Implementers should not transmit any payload when producing a void value, and consumers should ignore any payload bytes if present. This type is intentionally distinct from an empty byte sequence (`media:`) to make the semantic "no output" explicit in the graph schema.

title: WebVTT Subtitles
documentation:
## Purpose
WebVTT (Web Video Text Tracks, W3C specification) is a UTF-8 text format for timed cues used in HTML5 `<track>` elements. The [WebVTT Subtitles](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vtt;textable;list) media type represents any `.vtt` file that begins with the `WEBVTT` header line.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes subtitle, caption, or chapter data for web-based video players. WebVTT is the native format for HTML5 video tracks and supports additional features over SRT — including cue positioning, alignment, line settings, and region blocks — making it the preferred format for web delivery.

## Notes
A WebVTT file begins with the mandatory `WEBVTT` line, optionally followed by a header block, then cue blocks separated by blank lines. Timecodes use `HH:MM:SS.mmm --> HH:MM:SS.mmm` format (with a period before milliseconds, unlike SRT's comma). The `;textable;list` tags reflect the human-readable ordered-list structure. Implementers parsing cue settings should handle the optional `NOTE` and `REGION` blocks that may appear between cues.

title: Vue Component
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Vue.js single-file components (SFCs), which combine a `<template>`, `<script>`, and `<style>` block in one `.vue` file. Vue is a progressive framework for building web UIs and supports both Options API and Composition API styles.

## When to use
Use `media:vue;textable;code` when a pipeline node processes `.vue` files — for example, a Vite build step, an ESLint + vue-eslint-parser analysis step, or an LLM node generating Vue component scaffolding.

## Notes
Vue SFCs require a build tool (Vite, Vue CLI, or webpack with `vue-loader`) to transform them into browser-executable JavaScript. TypeScript is supported in `<script lang="ts">` blocks. See also [Vue Component](https://capdag.com/browse/media:vue;textable;code).

title: WAR Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
WAR (Web application ARchive) is a ZIP-format archive containing a Java web application — servlets, JSPs, static resources, and a `WEB-INF/web.xml` deployment descriptor — in the structure required by Java EE / Jakarta EE servlet containers. The [WAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:war;archive) media type represents any `.war` file.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes Java web application deployments — for example, CI/CD pipelines that build and deploy WAR files to Tomcat, JBoss, WildFly, or Jetty servers. For general-purpose Java archives without a web deployment structure use [JAR](https://capdag.com/browse/media:jar;archive).

## Notes
WAR files are ZIP archives that follow a specific directory layout: `WEB-INF/web.xml` (deployment descriptor), `WEB-INF/classes/` (compiled servlets), `WEB-INF/lib/` (dependency JARs), and static content at the root. Implementers can inspect WAR contents with any ZIP tool. Servlet containers deploy WAR files by extracting them to a work directory; re-deploying requires replacing the WAR and restarting the context. The `;archive` tag indicates this is a packaged archive artifact.

title: WebAssembly Binary
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries a compiled WebAssembly binary module. Wasm binaries are stack-machine bytecode that run in sandboxed browser environments and standalone runtimes (Wasmtime, WAMR, WasmEdge, Node.js). They are the compilation target for C, C++, Rust, Go, and other languages.

## When to use
Use `media:wasm` when a pipeline node executes, instruments, or analyzes a compiled Wasm binary — for example, a Wasmtime execution step, a binary analysis tool using `wasm-tools`, or a deployment step uploading a module to an edge runtime.

## Notes
Wasm binaries are not human-readable; for readable text representation, use [WebAssembly Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wat;textable;code) (`.wat`). The Wasm binary format begins with the magic bytes `\0asm` followed by a 4-byte version field. See also [WebAssembly Binary](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wasm).

title: WebAssembly Text
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries WebAssembly Text Format (WAT) source, the human-readable S-expression representation of WebAssembly bytecode. WAT is used for debugging, manual authoring of small Wasm modules, and educational purposes.

## When to use
Use `media:wat;textable;code` when a pipeline node reads or writes WAT source — for example, a `wat2wasm` compilation step, a disassembly step converting a `.wasm` binary to readable WAT, or an educational tool that generates annotated Wasm examples.

## Notes
WAT and the binary Wasm format have a 1:1 correspondence; conversion between them is lossless via tools like `wat2wasm` and `wasm2wat` from the WebAssembly Binary Toolkit (WABT). For compiled binary modules, use [WebAssembly Binary](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wasm). See also [WebAssembly Text](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wat;textable;code).

title: WAV Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Generic audio media type for WAV-encoded audio without additional semantic constraints. Covers any waveform content regardless of what it contains (music, speech, sound effects, recordings).

## When to use
Use `media:wav;audio` when the semantic role of the audio is not specified or when the consumer can handle arbitrary audio content. If the content is specifically speech for transcription, prefer [Speech Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:audio;speech;wav) instead.

## Notes
Despite the description mentioning MP3 and other formats, the MIME type and extension for this spec are WAV. Other audio formats have their own dedicated media specs.

title: WebM Video
documentation:
## Purpose
WebM is an open, royalty-free video format developed by Google, based on the Matroska container and designed for efficient streaming on the web. It typically carries VP8, VP9, or AV1 video with Vorbis or Opus audio.

## When to use
Use [WebM Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:webm;video) for web-targeted video delivery where royalty-free codecs are required, or when targeting browsers that prefer VP9/AV1 over H.264. WebM is also a good choice for video generated by browser-based recording APIs (e.g., MediaRecorder).

## Notes
WebM is a strict subset of Matroska and limits the allowed codec set compared to [MKV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mkv;video). AV1 in WebM offers excellent compression at low bitrates, making it well-suited for streaming pipelines. Most server-side processing tools support WebM via libwebm or FFmpeg.

title: WebP Image
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a WebP image, a modern format developed by Google that supports both lossy and lossless compression as well as alpha transparency and animation. WebP typically achieves significantly smaller file sizes than JPEG or PNG at equivalent quality.

## When to use
Use `media:webp;image` when a node produces or consumes WebP images, particularly for web delivery or when storage efficiency is important. WebP is a good default for both photographic and graphical content where broad codec support is available.

## Notes
WebP support is now universal in modern browsers and operating systems. Older toolchains or embedded systems may lack native WebP decoding; consider providing a JPEG or PNG fallback when targeting those environments.

title: WMA Audio
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a WMA (Windows Media Audio) file, Microsoft's proprietary compressed audio format. WMA was widely distributed through the Windows Media Player ecosystem and Windows portable devices.

## When to use
Use `media:wma;audio` when a node must read legacy WMA files from Windows-based archives or media libraries. WMA is rarely used in new workflows; for interoperability, convert to [MP3 Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:mp3;audio) or [AAC Audio](https://capdag.com/browse/media:aac;audio) early in the pipeline.

## Notes
WMA support outside the Windows ecosystem requires FFmpeg or equivalent libraries. Some WMA files include DRM protection, which prevents decoding without the original Windows Media licence. Lossless WMA (WMA Lossless) is a distinct codec variant from the standard lossy WMA format.

title: WMV Video
documentation:
## Purpose
WMV (Windows Media Video) is Microsoft's proprietary video container and codec family, part of the Windows Media framework. It was widely used for video distribution on Windows platforms and embedded in Microsoft Office documents and Windows Media Player.

## When to use
Use [WMV Video](https://capdag.com/browse/media:wmv;video) when ingesting video sourced from Windows-based capture tools, legacy corporate media libraries, or content delivered via Microsoft Silverlight or Windows Media Player. For cross-platform delivery, transcode to [MP4](https://capdag.com/browse/media:video).

## Notes
WMV wraps the VC-1 (or older WMV7/8/9) codec using the Advanced Systems Format (ASF) container. Playback on non-Windows platforms requires third-party codec support. DRM-protected WMV files are common in legacy digital distribution and require license acquisition before decoding.

title: WOFF Font
documentation:
## Purpose
WOFF (Web Open Font Format) is a compressed container for TrueType or OpenType fonts designed for efficient web delivery, compressing font tables with zlib. The [WOFF Font](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff;font) media type represents any `.woff` file conforming to the W3C WOFF 1.0 specification.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes web fonts for CSS `@font-face` rules in environments that must support older browsers (IE 9+). For modern deployments, [WOFF2](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff2;font) provides better compression and is now universally supported in current browsers.

## Notes
WOFF wraps the underlying sfnt font tables with a short header and per-table zlib compression. Metadata and private data blocks are optional. Implementers can convert between WOFF and raw TTF/OTF using FontTools (`fonttools woff`) or `sfnt2woff`. The `;font` tag indicates this is a font artifact.

title: WOFF2 Font
documentation:
## Purpose
WOFF2 (Web Open Font Format 2) is the modern web font container format that uses Brotli compression to achieve significantly smaller file sizes than WOFF 1.0. The [WOFF2 Font](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff2;font) media type represents any `.woff2` file conforming to the W3C WOFF2 specification.

## When to use
Use this type when a node produces or consumes web fonts for CSS `@font-face` delivery to modern browsers. WOFF2 is the recommended format for web font distribution, offering 30% smaller files than WOFF on average. All current browsers support it. For environments requiring IE support, also provide a [WOFF](https://capdag.com/browse/media:woff;font) fallback.

## Notes
WOFF2 uses a custom Brotli-based compression scheme applied to a transformed font table representation, not to raw sfnt tables. Implementers should use the `woff2` C++ library (by Google) or FontTools to convert between WOFF2 and TTF/OTF. The `;font` tag indicates this is a font artifact.

title: Excel Spreadsheet (Legacy)
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries legacy Microsoft Excel spreadsheets in the binary BIFF format (`.xls`) used by Excel 97 through Excel 2003. This format is still present in archival datasets and systems that predate the Open XML transition.

## When to use
Use `media:xls` when a pipeline node must handle old-format Excel files — for example, a data ingestion step using `xlrd` or LibreOffice to read legacy spreadsheets, or an archival conversion step targeting XLSX. For current Excel files, prefer [Excel Spreadsheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xlsx).

## Notes
The `xlrd` library dropped `.xls` support in newer versions; use `xlrd<2.0` or libraries like `openpyxl` (for XLSX) or `xlwings` when processing legacy files. See also [Excel Spreadsheet (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xls).

title: Excel Spreadsheet
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Microsoft Excel spreadsheets in the Open XML format (`.xlsx`), the current standard for tabular data with formulas, charts, and formatting. XLSX files are ZIP archives containing XML parts for worksheets, styles, shared strings, and relationships.

## When to use
Use `media:xlsx` when a pipeline node reads or produces Excel spreadsheets — for example, a data extraction step using `openpyxl`, a report generation step that writes formatted tables, or a conversion step producing CSV from a given sheet.

## Notes
XLSX is an ISO/IEC 29500 standard and is supported by LibreOffice, Google Sheets, and most BI tools. The ZIP structure can be inspected by renaming the file to `.zip`. For the older binary format, see [Excel Spreadsheet (Legacy)](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xls). See also [Excel Spreadsheet](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xlsx).

title: XML Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents an XML document with a well-formed tree structure of elements, attributes, and text nodes. XML is used for data interchange, configuration, document markup, and protocol payloads across many domains.

## When to use
Use `media:xml;textable` when a node produces or consumes XML content — for example a web service response, a configuration file, or a document format such as XHTML or SVG source. For SVG specifically, prefer [SVG Image](https://capdag.com/browse/media:svg;image;textable).

## Notes
Consumers that process XML should use a namespace-aware XML parser. Documents may include an XML declaration, a DOCTYPE, or external entity references; parsers should be configured to handle these safely.

title: XZ Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
XZ is a single-file compression format using the LZMA2 algorithm, offering very high compression ratios at the cost of significant memory and CPU usage during compression. It is the standard compression format for Linux kernel releases and many distribution packages.

## When to use
Use [XZ Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:xz;archive) when distributing large files where download size is critical and compression time is acceptable — for example, release artifacts, database dumps, or firmware images. XZ is frequently combined with [TAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tar;archive) as `.tar.xz`.

## Notes
XZ compression is slow but decompression is reasonably fast. Memory requirements during compression can be large (hundreds of MB at high preset levels). For scenarios where compression speed matters more than ratio, [Zstandard](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zstd;archive) is a better choice. XZ includes integrity checksums (CRC32/CRC64/SHA-256) selectable at creation time.

title: YAML
description: YAML content - may be a scalar value, sequence, or mapping
documentation:
## Purpose
The most permissive YAML type, used when the top-level structure is unknown, a bare scalar, or otherwise unconstrained. Covers any valid YAML document regardless of shape.

## When to use
Use `media:textable;yaml` when the structure of the YAML payload is not determined ahead of time. When the structure is known, prefer [YAML Document](https://capdag.com/browse/media:record;textable;yaml), [YAML List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;textable;yaml), or [YAML List of Mappings](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;record;textable;yaml).

## Notes
YAML supports multiple documents in a single file separated by `---`. Consumers that expect a single document should parse only the first one.

title: YAML Document
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a YAML document whose root is a mapping (key-value pairs). This is the most common YAML shape for configuration files and structured data.

## When to use
Use `media:record;textable;yaml` when a node produces or consumes a YAML mapping at the top level, such as a configuration file, a machine definition, or a metadata document. For a sequence of mappings, use [YAML List of Mappings](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;record;textable;yaml).

## Notes
YAML mappings allow nested structures and references (anchors and aliases). Consumers should use a full YAML parser rather than simple line-by-line processing.

title: YAML List
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a YAML document whose root is a sequence (ordered list of items). Items may be scalars, mappings, or nested sequences.

## When to use
Use `media:list;textable;yaml` when a node produces or expects a YAML sequence as its root element. If each item is a mapping, prefer [YAML List of Mappings](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;record;textable;yaml) for a more precise type.

## Notes
Item types within the sequence are unconstrained by this spec. Consumers that require uniform item types should validate each element after parsing.

title: YAML List of Mappings
description: YAML sequence where each item is a mapping with key-value structure
documentation:
## Purpose
Represents a YAML sequence in which every item is a mapping with key-value fields. This shape corresponds to tabular or collection data serialised as YAML — for example a list of tasks, records, or configuration entries.

## When to use
Use `media:list;record;textable;yaml` when a node produces or consumes a homogeneous YAML list of mappings. For a general YAML list without a record constraint, use [YAML List](https://capdag.com/browse/media:list;textable;yaml).

## Notes
Consumers may assume all items share a common set of keys, but should handle optional or missing keys gracefully. The `textable` tag allows this type to flow to any generic text consumer.

title: Zig Source
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Zig source files for systems programming tasks where manual memory control and no hidden allocations are required. Zig targets bare-metal, embedded, and high-performance application domains.

## When to use
Use `media:zig;textable;code` when a pipeline node processes `.zig` source — for example, a cross-compilation step or a build-system integration that invokes `zig build`.

## Notes
Zig has no preprocessor or macros; metaprogramming is done through comptime expressions in the same source file. The `.zig` extension covers both library files and executables. See also [Zig Source](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zig;textable;code).

title: ZIP Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
ZIP is a widely supported archive format that stores one or more files with per-file deflate compression and an end-of-central-directory index. It is the de facto standard for distributing collections of files across all major platforms.

## When to use
Use [ZIP Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zip;archive) when packaging multiple files for distribution, upload, or handoff where the recipient is expected to extract the contents. ZIP is the correct choice when broad compatibility is essential, including with Windows Explorer, macOS Finder, and mobile operating systems without additional software.

## Notes
ZIP supports individual file extraction without decompressing the entire archive, which makes it efficient for large bundles where only a subset of files is needed. Compression ratio is generally lower than [TAR.GZ](https://capdag.com/browse/media:targz;archive) or [7-Zip](https://capdag.com/browse/media:7z;archive) because compression is applied per-file rather than across the whole archive. The format supports 64-bit extensions (ZIP64) for archives exceeding 4 GB.

title: Zsh Script
documentation:
## Purpose
Carries Zsh script files, which extend POSIX shell with advanced completion, globbing, and theming features. Zsh is the default shell on macOS and is widely adopted as an interactive shell on Linux.

## When to use
Use `media:zsh;textable;code` when a pipeline node handles `.zsh` scripts specifically — for example, a dotfile provisioner deploying shell configuration or a linting step checking Zsh-specific syntax. For generic POSIX shell scripts, use [Shell Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:shell;textable;code).

## Notes
Zsh is largely compatible with Bash but adds features like extended globbing (`**`), associative arrays, and a richer completion system. Scripts often rely on Zsh-specific constructs that would not run under plain `sh`. See also [Zsh Script](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zsh;textable;code).

title: Zstandard Archive
documentation:
## Purpose
Zstandard (zstd) is a modern, fast compression algorithm developed by Meta that provides compression ratios comparable to [GZIP](https://capdag.com/browse/media:gzip;archive) or better while operating significantly faster in both compression and decompression. It is designed for real-time compression scenarios.

## When to use
Use [Zstandard Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:zstd;archive) when compression speed and decompression latency matter — for example, in data pipelines, streaming storage backends, or packaging large datasets where wait time affects throughput. Zstd supports trained dictionaries that dramatically improve ratio on small files.

## Notes
Zstd has a configurable compression level from 1 (fastest) to 22 (maximum ratio), with level 3 being the typical balance. It is natively supported in Linux kernels (since 5.1), most modern package managers, and Facebook's RocksDB. For bundling multiple files, pair with [TAR Archive](https://capdag.com/browse/media:tar;archive) to produce `.tar.zst`.
